react-native.title: JavaScript & React Prerequisites
react-native.description: React Native is React for mobile. Before touching the mobile layer, you need solid JavaScript and React fundamentals — components, hooks, and state — everything carries over.
topics: ES6+ — destructuring, spread, arrow functions, modules
react-native.challenge: Build a small React web app (notes app) to solidify your hooks and state management knowledge before moving to mobile.

children.title: Event Loop & Async Patterns
children.description: React Native's JS thread runs a single event loop inherited from the browser model. If you block it with synchronous work, the entire UI freezes because animations and gestures also queue on the same loop. Understanding the call stack, microtask queue, and macrotask queue lets you predict exactly when your state updates and effects fire.

children.title: Closures & Memory Leaks
children.description: Closures capture the surrounding scope by reference, not by value. In React hooks, stale closures inside useEffect or useCallback can hold references to old state, causing silent bugs and memory leaks that are notoriously hard to trace. Recognising closure scope is the single most common skill gap for developers moving from backend JS to React Native.

children.title: useState & Immutable State Updates
children.description: React schedules re-renders only when you replace state with a new reference. Mutating an array or object in place breaks this contract — the component sees no change and silently skips the render, leaving your UI stale. Every state update in React Native must produce a new object or array, not modify the existing one.

resources.label: React Docs — Updating Objects in State

children.title: useEffect Dependency Array
children.description: The dependency array tells React when to re-run an effect. An empty array means run once on mount; missing a dependency causes stale data bugs; over-including a dependency causes infinite loops. In React Native, misusing useEffect for data fetching without proper cleanup leads to state updates on unmounted components and crashes.

children.title: Destructuring & Spread Operator
children.description: Destructuring and spread are the idiomatic tools for working with props, state, and API responses in React Native. Misunderstanding shallow vs deep spread leads to accidental state mutation or unexpectedly shared object references — a frequent source of bugs when merging style objects or updating nested state.

children.title: ES Modules & Import Resolution
children.description: React Native uses Metro bundler with Node-style module resolution. Knowing how named vs default exports work, how barrel files affect bundle size, and how Metro resolves platform-specific files (e.g., Button.ios.js vs Button.android.js) prevents cryptic import errors and accidental inclusion of dev-only code in release builds.

children.title: Component Composition & Props Drilling
children.description: React Native UIs are component trees where data flows down through props. When components become deeply nested, prop drilling couples unrelated components and makes refactoring painful. Learning when to lift state, when to use Context, and when to reach for a state manager determines the long-term maintainability of any app.

children.title: React.memo & Preventing Re-renders
children.description: Every parent re-render causes all child components to re-render by default. On a long FlatList with complex cells, this tanks frame rate. React.memo shallow-compares props to skip unnecessary renders, but its effectiveness depends entirely on passing stable references — a lesson that underpins all React Native performance work.

react-native.title: React Native Core
react-native.description: Set up your dev environment, understand how React Native bridges JavaScript to native code, and master the core components and styling system.
topics: Expo vs bare React Native — when to use each
Core components — View, Text, Image, TextInput, ScrollView, FlatList
StyleSheet API — style objects, platform-specific styles
Flexbox in React Native — differences from web
Metro bundler — fast refresh, module resolution
Debugging — Expo DevTools, React DevTools, Flipper
react-native.challenge: Build a cross-platform mobile UI for your notes app — recreate the layout from scratch using only RN core components and StyleSheet.

children.title: Old Architecture: JS-to-Native Bridge
children.description: The legacy bridge serialises all JavaScript-to-native communication to JSON and sends it asynchronously across a single shared queue. This means every style update, layout measurement, and native module call has inherent latency. If you over-communicate across the bridge (e.g., calling native APIs in tight loops), you create a bottleneck that manifests as jank even when both threads are idle.

resources.label: React Native Docs — The New Architecture

children.title: JavaScript Thread vs UI Thread
children.description: React Native runs your JavaScript on a separate thread from the native UI thread. Animations driven by JS (e.g., Animated API) communicate frame-by-frame across the bridge and drop frames when JS is busy. Reanimated 3 solves this by running animation worklets directly on the UI thread. Misunderstanding this split is why developers see animations stutter during API calls.

resources.label: React Native Reanimated — How it Works

children.title: StyleSheet API vs Inline Styles
children.description: StyleSheet.create() sends style definitions to the native side once at startup and references them by integer ID thereafter, dramatically reducing bridge traffic compared to recreating plain objects on every render. Inline style objects create new object references each render, forcing the differ to re-send styles across the bridge even when nothing changed.

children.title: Expo Managed vs Bare Workflow
children.description: Expo Managed workflow restricts you to the Expo SDK surface area but provides OTA updates, universal builds, and zero native configuration. Bare workflow gives you full access to native code but requires you to manage iOS and Android projects yourself. Choosing the wrong workflow early forces a costly migration; most production teams start managed and eject only when blocked.

children.title: Flexbox Differences from Web
children.description: React Native's layout engine is Yoga, a cross-platform C++ Flexbox implementation. Key differences from CSS: flex direction defaults to column (not row), flex accepts only a single number (not the CSS shorthand), and percentage values behave differently without a defined parent size. Ignoring these differences causes layouts that look correct in a browser emulator but break on real devices.

resources.label: React Native Docs — Layout with Flexbox
React Native Docs — Height and Width

children.title: SafeAreaView & Platform Insets
children.description: Modern devices have notches, dynamic islands, and home indicator bars that overlap app content. Without SafeAreaView (or react-native-safe-area-context), UI elements render behind these system chrome elements. This is invisible in the simulator but catastrophically breaks real device layouts, especially on iPhone 14+ and Android devices with notch cutouts.

children.title: React DevTools & Component Inspection
children.description: React DevTools lets you inspect the component tree, view props/state, and profile re-renders in real time. In React Native, you can attach DevTools to a running app via Metro's inspector. Not using DevTools when debugging unexpected renders wastes hours — the component tree shows you exactly which component is re-rendering and why.

children.title: Metro Bundler & Fast Refresh
children.description: Metro is React Native's JavaScript bundler that resolves modules, transforms code, and serves the bundle to the device. Fast Refresh re-applies only the changed module without losing component state — a critical DX feature. Understanding Metro's module graph helps you diagnose circular dependency warnings and slow build times in large codebases.

react-native.title: Navigation
react-native.description: Mobile apps are multi-screen experiences. React Navigation is the standard — learn stack, tab, and drawer navigators, pass data between screens, and handle deep linking.
topics: React Navigation setup — native stack, dependencies
Stack Navigator — push, pop, goBack, params
Drawer Navigator — gestures, custom drawer content
react-native.challenge: Extend your notes app with full navigation — a tab bar (Notes, Settings), a stack navigator inside Notes for list → detail, and params passed between screens.

children.title: Native Stack vs JS Stack
children.description: React Navigation offers a JavaScript-based stack and a native stack backed by UINavigationController on iOS and Fragment on Android. The native stack delivers platform-authentic gestures and transitions at 60fps because transitions run on the native thread. The JS stack gives more customisation but renders transitions via Animated over the bridge, causing frame drops under load.

resources.label: React Navigation Docs — Stack vs Native Stack

children.title: Navigation State & Screen Lifecycle
children.description: React Navigation maintains a navigation state tree that determines which screens are mounted. A screen stays mounted (but hidden) when you navigate away in a tab navigator, meaning useEffect on that screen still fires and subscriptions stay active. Treating every screen like a freshly mounted component leads to duplicate data fetches, multiple WebSocket subscriptions, and runaway memory growth.

children.title: Tabs Inside Stacks & State Persistence
children.description: Nesting a Tab navigator inside a Stack navigator means tabs are unmounted when you pop the stack, losing all tab state. Nesting a Stack inside each Tab preserves independent history per tab — the standard pattern for production apps. Getting this hierarchy wrong means users lose their scroll position or form input every time they navigate.

children.title: TypeScript-safe Navigation with Params
children.description: React Navigation's type parameter system enforces that every navigation call provides the exact params the target screen expects. Without typed params, passing the wrong key name compiles without error but causes a runtime crash when the screen tries to read the param. This is the most common source of hard-to-reproduce navigation bugs in production React Native apps.

children.title: URL Schemes vs Universal Links
children.description: URL schemes (myapp://) are app-specific but can be claimed by any installed app — a security risk on Android. Universal links (https://myapp.com/path) are validated by the OS against an apple-app-site-association or assetlinks.json file hosted on your server, making them hijack-resistant. Using URL schemes for production deep links exposes users to link-hijacking attacks.

children.title: Linking to the Correct Authenticated Screen
children.description: Deep links that arrive before authentication completes must be queued and replayed after login, not ignored. React Navigation's linking configuration handles initial URL but not mid-session authenticated deep links unless you implement a custom linking subscriber and redirect logic. Apps that ignore this drop deep link traffic and frustrate push-notification-driven users.

resources.label: Expo Docs — Notifications with Deep Links

children.title: Gesture-Driven Drawer & Interop with Screens
children.description: The Drawer navigator uses React Native Gesture Handler to detect swipe gestures. Without installing and properly wrapping the app in GestureHandlerRootView, gestures silently fall back to a broken JS implementation that conflicts with FlatList scrolling and causes intermittent swipe failures that only appear on real devices, not simulators.

resources.label: React Native Gesture Handler Docs — Setup

children.title: Custom Drawer Content & Navigation Context
children.description: React Navigation exposes a drawerContent prop so you can render any component as the drawer — user profile, settings links, sign-out button. This component receives the navigation object and the drawer state, but accessing navigation outside the drawer context (e.g., from a button rendered inside the drawer that navigates to a modal) requires useNavigation from the hook, not a passed prop.

react-native.title: State Management
react-native.description: Manage app-wide state cleanly. Choose the right state solution — Zustand for simplicity, Redux Toolkit for complex flows — and persist state between app launches.
topics: Redux Toolkit — createSlice, createAsyncThunk, RTK Query
react-native.challenge: Add Zustand to your notes app with MMKV persistence — notes survive app kills, and a global theme toggle (light/dark) persists across sessions.

children.title: Zustand Store Design
children.description: Zustand stores are plain JavaScript objects with actions co-located. Unlike Redux, there is no forced separation between state shape and update logic, which speeds up development but requires discipline to prevent stores from becoming God objects. Subscribing to the entire store rather than individual slices causes every component to re-render on any state change, defeating the purpose of selective reactivity.

resources.label: Zustand Docs — Preventing Re-renders with useShallow

children.title: Redux Toolkit & RTK Query
children.description: Redux Toolkit eliminates the boilerplate of raw Redux with createSlice and createAsyncThunk. RTK Query adds a data-fetching layer with automatic caching, invalidation, and optimistic updates — removing most manual useEffect-for-fetching patterns. Teams that skip RTK Query and implement their own caching logic inevitably rewrite the same cache invalidation bugs RTK Query already solved.

children.title: AsyncStorage vs MMKV
children.description: AsyncStorage is an unencrypted, asynchronous key-value store backed by SQLite on Android and flat files on iOS. MMKV is a synchronous memory-mapped storage engine developed by WeChat that is 10–30x faster for reads. Using AsyncStorage to persist animation state or theme preferences causes visible flicker on app launch because the async read completes after the first render paints the default value.

children.title: Hydration & Initial State Race Conditions
children.description: Persisted state must be rehydrated before the first render that depends on it, or the UI flashes the default (empty/light-theme) state before snapping to the persisted value. Zustand's persist middleware handles this with a hasHydrated flag; Redux Persist exposes a PersistGate component. Ignoring hydration timing is the single most common bug report from users on app launch.

children.title: Memoised Selectors to Prevent Over-rendering
children.description: Selectors derive data from stored state — filtered lists, computed totals, joined entities. Without memoisation, a selector that filters an array runs on every render and returns a new array reference even when the data hasn't changed, causing every subscribed component to re-render. In large React Native apps with complex state, un-memoised selectors are frequently responsible for 80% of unnecessary renders.

children.title: Optimistic Updates
children.description: Optimistic updates apply the expected result of a mutation to local state immediately, before the server confirms success. This makes the UI feel instantaneous. If the server rejects the mutation, you must roll back to the previous state. Implementing optimistic updates without a rollback strategy leaves the UI in an inconsistent state that requires a hard refresh to fix.

children.title: When Context Causes Performance Problems
children.description: React's Context API re-renders every consumer when any value in the context changes. A single AuthContext that holds both the user object and a loading flag will re-render all navigation components, headers, and profile avatars every time the loading flag toggles. Splitting contexts by update frequency or using a state manager like Zustand prevents this cascade.

resources.label: React Docs — Scaling with Context and Reducers

children.title: useReducer for Complex Local State
children.description: When a component manages multiple related pieces of state that change together (e.g., form fields, validation errors, and submission status), using separate useState calls creates synchronisation bugs where one state updates before another. useReducer co-locates all transitions in a single function, ensuring atomic state updates and making the logic testable in isolation.

resources.label: React Docs — Extracting State Logic into a Reducer

react-native.title: Networking & APIs
react-native.description: Connect your mobile app to a backend. Fetch data efficiently, handle caching and background refresh, manage auth tokens, and build real-time features with WebSockets.
topics: Fetch API and Axios in React Native
Auth token storage — SecureStore (Expo) or Keychain
react-native.challenge: Connect your notes app to a REST backend — fetch notes with TanStack Query, store JWT in SecureStore, and add optimistic mutations for create/delete.

children.title: TanStack Query Cache & Stale-While-Revalidate
children.description: TanStack Query caches responses by query key and serves stale data immediately while revalidating in the background. If you manually refetch inside useEffect instead of letting TanStack Query manage cache lifetime, you bypass caching entirely and hammer your API on every navigation event. Understanding staleTime vs cacheTime is the difference between a snappy app and one that shows loading spinners constantly.

children.title: Axios Interceptors for Auth Token Refresh
children.description: JWT access tokens expire. An interceptor catches 401 responses, silently requests a new token using the refresh token, and replays the original request — invisible to the user. Without an interceptor, every screen must manually handle token expiry, resulting in inconsistent logout behaviour and race conditions when multiple parallel requests all try to refresh the token simultaneously.

children.title: Network State Detection & Offline-First
children.description: Mobile devices lose connectivity constantly — tunnels, elevators, rural areas. An offline-first app shows cached data and queues mutations when offline, then syncs automatically on reconnect. Apps that show empty screens or crashes on network loss instead of cached data lose user trust permanently. NetInfo from @react-native-community/netinfo enables proactive offline UI states.

children.title: WebSockets & Connection Lifecycle
children.description: WebSocket connections on mobile must be managed carefully because the OS kills background connections when the app goes to the background. Without reconnection logic and connection state tracking, the app silently misses real-time events after the user returns from background. socket.io-client's reconnection options and React Native's AppState API together provide robust connection lifecycle management.

children.title: SecureStore vs AsyncStorage for Tokens
children.description: Storing JWT tokens or refresh tokens in AsyncStorage is a security vulnerability — AsyncStorage data is readable without root on Android and by any process with file system access. Expo SecureStore uses the iOS Keychain and Android Keystore, which are hardware-backed encrypted storage systems. Leaking tokens from AsyncStorage enables session hijacking across app restarts.

resources.label: OWASP Mobile Security — Insecure Data Storage

children.title: Token Refresh Race Conditions
children.description: When multiple parallel API calls all receive a 401, a naive interceptor fires multiple simultaneous token refresh requests. Only the first should refresh; the rest must wait and reuse the new token. Implementing a token refresh queue with a promise that all 401 interceptors share prevents revoked-token errors caused by racing refresh calls.

children.title: useMutation & Cache Invalidation
children.description: TanStack Query's useMutation handles the loading, error, and success states of data-writing operations. After a successful mutation, invalidating the relevant query key forces a background refetch that keeps the cache consistent. Forgetting to invalidate leaves stale data on screen — a user creates a post but doesn't see it in the list until they pull-to-refresh.

children.title: Prefetching for Instant Navigation
children.description: Prefetching loads data before the user navigates to a screen, making the transition feel instantaneous. TanStack Query's queryClient.prefetchQuery can be called on hover or on a list item press event before navigation fires. Without prefetching, every screen transition shows a loading spinner, doubling the perceived load time versus a desktop web app.

react-native.title: Native Device Features
react-native.description: Leverage the hardware. Use Expo SDK and community libraries to access the camera, push notifications, location services, and other native device capabilities.
topics: Linking — opening URLs, phone numbers, maps
react-native.challenge: Add camera capture to your notes app — users can attach a photo to a note using the camera or gallery, with proper permissions handling on both iOS and Android.

children.title: iOS vs Android Permission Lifecycle
children.description: iOS asks for a permission exactly once; if denied, the user must go to Settings to grant it — there is no second prompt. Android allows you to explain the rationale and re-request if not permanently denied. Treating both platforms identically causes iOS apps that silently fail after a first denial, leaving features broken with no explanation to the user.

children.title: Runtime vs Usage Description Strings
children.description: iOS requires NSCameraUsageDescription, NSLocationWhenInUseUsageDescription, and similar plist keys to be present at build time, before any runtime permission request. Missing these causes an immediate crash with no user-visible error. App Store review will also reject apps that request permissions without a meaningful description explaining exactly why the app needs them.

children.title: expo-camera vs react-native-vision-camera
children.description: expo-camera is simpler and works with managed workflow but is limited to basic photo/video capture. react-native-vision-camera supports frame processors — real-time per-frame JavaScript or native code execution — enabling QR scanning, AR overlays, and ML inference on the camera feed. Using expo-camera for a feature that requires frame processing leads to a rewrite when the limitation is hit in production.

children.title: Image Compression & Memory
children.description: A raw camera photo on a modern smartphone is 4–12 MB. Loading multiple full-resolution images into a FlatList without compression causes the app to hit the 512 MB memory ceiling on older iPhones and be killed by the OS. Always compress images client-side with expo-image-manipulator before upload or display, and use FastImage or expo-image for memory-efficient loading.

children.title: FCM, APNs & Expo Push Service
children.description: iOS uses Apple Push Notification service (APNs) and Android uses Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM). Both require device tokens to be registered server-side and renewed on token rotation. Expo's push notification service abstracts both behind a single API, but production apps with >100k users should send directly to FCM/APNs to avoid rate limits and dependency on Expo's infrastructure.

children.title: Foreground vs Background Notification Handling
children.description: Notifications behave differently depending on whether the app is in the foreground, background, or killed. Foreground notifications do not show a system banner by default on iOS — you must call setNotificationHandler. Background and killed-state notifications wake the app and require a background fetch or silent push payload. Missing these states leads to notifications that only work some of the time.

children.title: react-native-maps & API Key Setup
children.description: react-native-maps uses Google Maps on Android and Apple Maps on iOS. Google Maps requires an API key with the Maps SDK enabled in Google Cloud Console. A missing or restricted API key shows a blank grey map on Android in production builds — it often works in debug because Metro bypasses certain checks. Always test maps with a production-equivalent build.

children.title: Background Location & Battery Impact
children.description: Background location tracking requires the always-on location permission, which iOS grants only after the user upgrades from while-in-use permission in a separate system dialog. Apps that drain battery with high-frequency location updates risk App Store rejection and user uninstalls. Use significant-change location mode for coarse tracking and only upgrade to GPS precision when the feature is actively in use.

react-native.title: Animations & Performance
react-native.description: Mobile users expect buttery-smooth 60fps animations. Use React Native Reanimated for worklet-based animations that run on the UI thread, and optimize lists with FlashList.
topics: Gesture Handler — pan, pinch, tap gestures
React Native Profiler — JS thread vs UI thread
react-native.challenge: Add swipe-to-delete with a smooth Reanimated gesture to your notes list, with a spring animation on the note cards when they enter the list.

children.title: Worklets & the UI Thread
children.description: Reanimated 3 compiles JavaScript animation functions (worklets) and runs them directly on the UI thread via JSI, bypassing the bridge entirely. This is why Reanimated animations stay smooth at 60fps even when the JS thread is blocked by heavy computation. Any function called from a worklet must also be a worklet — calling a regular JS function from a worklet silently falls back to a slow async bridge call.

children.title: useSharedValue vs useAnimatedValue
children.description: useSharedValue stores animation state on the UI thread, making it readable and writable from worklets without crossing the bridge. The old Animated.Value communicates via asynchronous events and cannot be read synchronously during a gesture. Mixing the two APIs in a single component breaks gesture-driven animations and causes inconsistent frame timing.

children.title: Gesture Composition & Simultaneous Gestures
children.description: React Native Gesture Handler supports composing gestures with Race, Simultaneous, and Exclusive combinators. A pan gesture inside a ScrollView requires Simultaneous recognition to decide mid-gesture whether the user is scrolling or interacting with a card. Getting gesture composition wrong causes scroll views to become unresponsive or swipe gestures to fail when nested inside lists.

children.title: GestureHandlerRootView Requirement
children.description: All gesture handling requires GestureHandlerRootView to wrap the application at the root level. Without it, gestures compile but fail silently on Android in production builds — they work in debug because the development overlay provides a fallback. This is one of the most common setup mistakes, responsible for gesture-related production bug reports that cannot be reproduced in the simulator.

children.title: FlashList vs FlatList
children.description: FlatList recycles cell views using a windowed rendering approach, but its JavaScript-side layout calculation still runs on the JS thread for each visible item. FlashList by Shopify moves cell recycling to a fixed-size pool determined at render time, reducing JS work and eliminating the most common source of FlatList jank — the blank cells visible during fast scrolling on low-end Android devices.

children.title: keyExtractor, getItemLayout & Cell Memoisation
children.description: FlatList's keyExtractor must return a unique, stable string per item — using the array index causes React to unmount and remount items on reorder, losing scroll position and triggering unnecessary network requests. getItemLayout allows FlatList to skip layout measurement for fixed-height items, enabling instant scrollToOffset and dramatically reducing mount time for long lists.

children.title: React Native Profiler & Frame Budget
children.description: A 60fps display gives each frame 16.67ms to be produced. The React Native Profiler in DevTools shows JS render time per component, while the native Systrace shows UI-thread and GPU work. Frames that exceed the budget drop — users see jank. Identifying which component's render function, effect, or style calculation is consuming the frame budget is the first step in all React Native performance work.

children.title: Hermes Engine & Bundle Optimisation
children.description: Hermes is a JavaScript engine optimised for React Native that pre-compiles JS to bytecode at build time, reducing app startup time by 20–40% on Android. Without Hermes, the JS engine must parse and compile the entire bundle on every launch. Hermes is enabled by default in React Native 0.70+ and Expo SDK 47+; disabling it for compatibility reasons should be a last resort.

react-native.title: Testing & Publishing
react-native.description: Ship your app confidently. Test components and flows with React Native Testing Library, use EAS Build for cloud builds, and publish to both the App Store and Google Play.
topics: React Native Testing Library — render, fireEvent, waitFor
E2E testing with Detox or Maestro
EAS Build — managed vs bare workflow, build profiles
App signing — iOS provisioning profiles, Android keystore
App Store Connect & Google Play Console submission
react-native.challenge: Write component tests for your notes app with RNTL, set up EAS Build, create a production build, and follow the App Store/Play Store submission checklist.

children.title: React Native Testing Library Philosophy
children.description: RNTL queries components by accessibility role, label, and text — the same signals a screen reader uses — rather than by implementation details like component names or test IDs. Tests that query by class name break on any refactor; tests that query by accessible label survive major rewrites and simultaneously verify accessibility compliance. This is why RNTL's query API is deliberately accessibility-first.

children.title: Async Rendering & waitFor
children.description: React Native components that fetch data or run animations render asynchronously. Testing them without waitFor captures the loading state, not the final rendered content, causing tests that always pass in isolation but fail in CI because the assertion runs before the async update completes. waitFor retries the assertion until it passes or times out, correctly handling async render cycles.

children.title: EAS Build Profiles & Environments
children.description: EAS Build defines development, preview, and production profiles in eas.json. Each profile can target a different server URL, enable/disable Flipper, and use different signing identities. Teams that skip multi-profile setup end up debugging production issues with a development build, or accidentally ship internal feature flags to App Store users because there is no environment separation.

children.title: iOS Code Signing & Provisioning Profiles
children.description: iOS code signing requires a distribution certificate, a provisioning profile that lists the App ID and allowed devices, and both to be present on the build machine. A mismatch between the certificate and profile causes a cryptic build failure — 'no matching provisioning profile found'. EAS Build manages credentials automatically with eas credentials, eliminating the most common source of iOS CI build failures.

children.title: expo-updates & Update Channels
children.description: Expo Updates (EAS Update) delivers JavaScript and asset changes to installed apps over the air, bypassing the App Store review cycle. Updates are scoped to a channel, allowing staging builds to receive staging updates and production builds to receive production updates. Pushing an update to the wrong channel can accidentally ship unreleased features to production users.

children.title: OTA Update Limitations
children.description: OTA updates can only ship JavaScript and bundled asset changes. Any modification to native code — adding a new native library, modifying Info.plist, changing AndroidManifest.xml — requires a full store release. Teams that add native modules and then try to OTA-update them discover this limitation during a production incident, making it critical to plan native changes for store releases.

resources.label: Expo Docs — What Changes Require a Build

children.title: App Store Review Guidelines & Common Rejections
children.description: Apple's review team rejects apps for missing privacy manifests (required since iOS 17 for certain APIs), requesting permissions without using them, and offering in-app purchases outside StoreKit. Understanding the most common rejection reasons before submission saves the average 2-3 day review wait time per cycle and prevents launch delays caused by avoidable policy violations.

resources.label: Expo Docs — Submitting to App Store

children.title: Android Keystore & Play Store Signing
children.description: The Android keystore file contains the private key used to sign APKs and AABs. If this file is lost, you can never publish an update to your existing Play Store listing — you must create a new app with a new package name and migrate users. Google Play App Signing stores a backup of your key server-side, providing recovery options. Treating the keystore file as a non-critical build artifact is a catastrophic mistake.

react-native.title: Advanced Native Integration and New Architecture
react-native.description: Write custom native modules and turbo modules using the JSI layer to eliminate the bridge bottleneck and unlock synchronous JavaScript-to-native calls. Understand the Fabric renderer and how the new React Native architecture changes component and layout pipelines.
topics: JSI fundamentals and synchronous native calls
Turbo Modules authoring for iOS and Android
Fabric renderer and concurrent rendering on mobile
Bridging third-party native SDKs into RN
Hermes engine internals and bytecode optimization
Debugging native code with Xcode and Android Studio

resources.label: Advanced React Native by William Candillon

react-native.challenge: Create a Turbo Module that exposes a platform-specific biometric authentication API to JavaScript, wire it through Codegen, and benchmark call latency compared to the legacy bridge module.

children.title: JavaScript Interface (JSI) — Synchronous Native Calls
children.description: JSI replaces the JSON-serialized bridge with a C++ layer that hosts JavaScript objects backed directly by native C++ objects. JavaScript can call native methods synchronously without serialization overhead. Any native module that holds a reference accessible from JS via JSI must be thread-safe — JSI calls can arrive from any thread, and unsynchronized native state causes memory corruption crashes.

resources.label: React Native Docs — New Architecture Introduction

children.title: Fabric Renderer & Concurrent Rendering
children.description: Fabric is the C++ reimplementation of React Native's UI layer that enables concurrent rendering — the same capability that powers React 18's Suspense and transitions. It measures and commits layouts on a shadow thread rather than the JS thread, removing a major source of layout-induced JS thread jank. Apps on the old renderer cannot use Suspense for data fetching or React's transition APIs.

children.title: Codegen & Type-safe Native Specs
children.description: The New Architecture requires Turbo Module specs written in TypeScript or Flow that Codegen uses to generate type-safe C++ and Java/ObjC glue code. Mismatches between the spec and the native implementation cause build-time errors rather than runtime crashes, shifting the failure earlier in the development cycle. Skipping Codegen and writing manual JNI/JNA bindings removes this safety net.

resources.label: React Native Docs — Turbo Native Modules — Android
React Native Docs — Turbo Native Modules — iOS

children.title: Hermes Bytecode & Module Loading
children.description: With Hermes, the JavaScript bundle is compiled to bytecode at build time and memory-mapped at runtime — the OS loads only the pages the app actually executes. Large bundles with unused exports that were fine with JavaScriptCore become a problem with Hermes because the bytecode format is less forgiving of circular dependencies and dynamic requires that defeat static analysis.

children.title: Xcode & Android Studio for Native Crashes
children.description: When a React Native app crashes in native code, the JS stack trace is useless — the crash originates in C++, ObjC, or Kotlin. Xcode's crash reporter with dSYM symbolication and Android Studio's Logcat with NDK stack traces are the only tools that reveal the true crash site. Developers who never learn to read a native stack trace cannot debug crashes from third-party native libraries or their own native modules.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Understanding a Crash Report

children.title: Thread Safety & Main Thread Violations
children.description: All UIKit and many Android View operations must run on the main (UI) thread. Native modules default to a background thread, so calling a UI API directly from a module callback causes a main thread checker violation — a crash in debug builds and silent corruption in release builds. Always dispatch UI updates via dispatch_async(dispatch_get_main_queue()) on iOS or runOnUiThread on Android.

resources.label: React Native Docs — Native Modules iOS
React Native Docs — Native Modules Android

children.title: CocoaPods & Gradle Dependency Conflicts
children.description: Third-party native SDKs introduce their own native dependencies via CocoaPods or Gradle. Version conflicts between two libraries' transitive native dependencies cause build failures that produce cryptic linker errors. Running pod install after every native library addition and checking Gradle dependency trees with ./gradlew dependencies prevents most native dependency conflicts before they reach CI.

resources.label: React Native Docs — Linking Libraries iOS

children.title: Expo Config Plugins for Native Modifications
children.description: Expo Config Plugins modify native iOS and Android project files (Info.plist, AndroidManifest.xml, build.gradle) programmatically as part of the expo prebuild step, without requiring manual native code editing. Libraries that modify native config without a Config Plugin break the managed workflow and force an ejection. Any native SDK integration in an Expo project should first check for a Config Plugin.

react-native.title: CI/CD, OTA Updates and Production Release Management
react-native.description: Establish a full release pipeline for React Native apps covering automated builds, over-the-air update channels, crash monitoring, and staged rollouts. Learn how to manage multiple environments, signing configurations, and app store submissions without manual steps.
topics: EAS Build and EAS Submit for automated store uploads
OTA update channels and rollback strategies with EAS Update
Fastlane lanes for certificate and profile management
Crash reporting and source-map upload with Sentry
GitHub Actions workflows for RN mono-repo builds
Feature flag rollout and A/B testing on mobile
App store review guidelines and rejection remediation

resources.label: React Native CI/CD with GitHub Actions

react-native.challenge: Configure an EAS project with three update channels (development, staging, production), automate TestFlight and Play Internal Track uploads via GitHub Actions, and verify Sentry receives symbolicated crash reports.

children.title: EAS Build — Cloud Builds & Build Caches
children.description: EAS Build runs builds on Expo's cloud infrastructure, eliminating the need for macOS hardware to build iOS apps. Build caches store node_modules, CocoaPods, and Gradle artifacts between runs, reducing build times from 20 minutes to under 5 minutes for unchanged native dependencies. Bypassing caches by running full clean builds on every commit wastes CI minutes and slows the release cycle without any quality benefit.

children.title: EAS Submit — Automated Store Uploads
children.description: EAS Submit authenticates with App Store Connect and Google Play APIs to upload builds directly from CI, eliminating manual Transporter or drag-and-drop uploads. It handles the complex App Store Connect API token authentication, ASC API keys, and service account JSON for Play. Teams that skip EAS Submit spend 15–30 minutes per release on manual upload steps that compound across weekly release cadences.

children.title: Update Channel Strategy
children.description: EAS Update channels map native build configurations to JavaScript update streams. A native build on the production channel only receives updates published to production — it ignores staging and development updates. Misconfiguring channels so production builds receive staging updates is the most dangerous CI/CD mistake in React Native, as it can silently deploy untested code to all production users.

children.title: OTA Rollback Strategy
children.description: EAS Update supports rolling back to a previous update by republishing a prior bundle as the current update on a channel. Without a tested rollback procedure, a bad OTA update that causes crashes can strand all production users until a new build passes App Store review — a process taking 24–72 hours. Documenting and practicing the rollback procedure before a production incident is mandatory for production-grade operations.

children.title: Fastlane match for Certificate Management
children.description: Fastlane match stores all iOS certificates and provisioning profiles in an encrypted Git repository and syncs them to every CI machine on demand. Without match, certificates exist only on individual developer machines — when that developer leaves or their machine is lost, all CI builds fail and a new certificate must be manually generated and distributed. match makes certificate rotation a one-command operation.

children.title: Fastlane Lanes for Beta & Production
children.description: A Fastlane lane is a sequence of actions — build, test, sign, upload — that executes as a single command. Defining separate beta and production lanes with different signing identities, build numbers, and upload targets enforces a consistent, documented release process. Teams that release manually without lanes introduce human error: wrong build number, wrong provisioning profile, wrong API environment.

children.title: Sentry Source Maps & dSYM Upload
children.description: JavaScript errors in production builds are minified — stack traces reference line 1, column 34819 of the bundle, not your source file. Sentry requires source maps uploaded at build time to de-minify these traces. iOS crashes require dSYM files for native symbolication. Without both, production crash reports are unactionable, and engineers spend hours trying to reproduce crashes that took users seconds to trigger.

children.title: Feature Flags & Staged Rollouts
children.description: Releasing a new feature to 5% of users before a full rollout reduces the blast radius of undiscovered bugs. Feature flags decouple deployment from release — code ships in every build but activates only for flagged users. Without staged rollouts, a bug that crashes 0.1% of devices appears in a full user base simultaneously, making the Play Store and App Store ratings drop before the fix reaches review.

flutter.title: Dart Fundamentals
flutter.description: Flutter apps are written in Dart. Learn Dart's syntax, type system with null safety, and async programming before building your first widget.
topics: Functions — named params, arrow functions, closures
Collections — List, Map, Set with generics
flutter.challenge: Build a Dart CLI app that models a simple bank account — deposit, withdraw, balance history — demonstrating OOP, null safety, and async operations.

children.title: Sound Null Safety
children.description: Dart's null safety is enforced at compile time, not runtime. If you treat nullable types carelessly you get type errors that block the build entirely — the compiler refuses to compile code that could dereference null. Understanding the difference between T, T?, and T! is essential because incorrect use of the ! (bang) operator silently converts a null-safety guarantee into a runtime crash.

children.title: Dart Type Inference & Generics
children.description: Dart infers types at compile time, and generic classes like List<T> and Map<K,V> make your collections type-safe. Misusing dynamic defeats null safety and disables autocomplete across the codebase. Explicit type annotations on public APIs prevent subtle bugs when types are widened during refactors.

children.title: Future & async/await
children.description: A Future represents a single asynchronous computation that will eventually complete with a value or an error. Forgetting to await a Future means your code moves on before the result is ready, leading to race conditions and unhandled exceptions that surface as vague LateInitializationErrors or stale UI state.

children.title: Dart Streams
children.description: Streams are sequences of asynchronous events — think real-time data like Firestore listeners or user input. Using a StreamSubscription without cancelling it in dispose() causes memory leaks that accumulate silently. Understanding broadcast vs single-subscription streams prevents errors when multiple listeners attach to the same data source.

children.title: Mixins & Multiple Inheritance
children.description: Dart mixins let a class inherit behavior from multiple sources without multiple inheritance's diamond problem. Applying a mixin to a class that does not satisfy the mixin's on constraint causes a compile-time error. Misunderstanding mixin linearization order causes unexpected method resolution when two mixins define methods with the same name.

children.title: Factory Constructors & Named Constructors
children.description: Dart's factory constructor can return a cached or subclassed instance rather than always creating a new object — critical for singletons and union types like json deserialization. Confusing factory constructors with const constructors breaks tree-shaking and widget identity checks in Flutter, causing unnecessary rebuilds.

children.title: Structured Error Handling
children.description: In Dart, uncaught exceptions in async functions do not propagate to the caller's try/catch unless you properly await them. Swallowing errors with empty catch blocks hides crashes that would be visible in Flutter's error boundary. Using custom exception classes instead of raw strings gives you typed error handling that survives refactoring.

children.title: Dart Isolates for Concurrency
children.description: Dart runs single-threaded on the main isolate — long-running computations block the event loop and freeze the UI. Spawning a background Isolate moves CPU work off the main thread, but isolates share no memory, so communication happens only through message passing via SendPort/ReceivePort. Missing this model causes dropped frames that appear as jank in Flutter apps.

flutter.title: Flutter UI Fundamentals
flutter.description: In Flutter, everything is a widget. Learn the widget tree, layout system, and Material Design 3 to build beautiful, responsive UIs from scratch.
topics: Layout — Row, Column, Stack, Flex, Expanded, Padding
Common widgets — Text, Container, Image, Icon, AppBar
flutter.challenge: Build a shopping app home screen in Flutter — responsive grid of product cards, a search bar AppBar, bottom navigation, and a custom color theme.

children.title: StatelessWidget vs StatefulWidget
children.description: Flutter builds UI as an immutable widget tree that is cheap to reconstruct — the real cost is in the render objects beneath it. Choosing StatefulWidget when state does not change adds unnecessary rebuild overhead, and lifting state too high causes entire subtrees to rebuild on every change. Understanding the three-layer architecture (Widget, Element, RenderObject) is what separates performant Flutter code from naive code.

children.title: BuildContext & Widget Lifecycle
children.description: BuildContext is a handle to a widget's location in the tree — using it after the widget has been disposed causes the infamous 'mounted' crash. Calling setState() after dispose() or using context across async gaps without checking mounted leads to unhandled exceptions that are difficult to reproduce. Every async gap that touches context must guard with if (mounted) before proceeding.

children.title: ThemeData & ColorScheme
children.description: Material 3 introduces a dynamic color system built on ColorScheme, replacing hard-coded color constants. Ignoring ThemeData and writing hardcoded colors throughout your widgets makes dark mode, accessibility contrast modes, and dynamic color (Material You) impossible to support without a complete rewrite. Always reference Theme.of(context).colorScheme instead of constant hex values.

children.title: Custom Themes & TextTheme
children.description: TextTheme defines the typographic scale (displayLarge through bodySmall) used consistently across Material widgets. Manually setting font sizes everywhere creates visual inconsistency and makes a brand typography update require changes across hundreds of widgets. Defining a custom TextTheme in ThemeData propagates through the entire app with a single change.

children.title: Flex Layout — Row, Column, Expanded
children.description: Flutter's layout model passes constraints down and bubbles sizes up — a child that returns a size larger than its constraint causes a RenderFlex overflow error visible as the red/yellow striped error bar. Misunderstanding when to use Expanded vs Flexible vs SizedBox leads to either overflow errors or unwanted stretching that is hard to debug without the widget inspector.

children.title: Stack & Positioned Widgets
children.description: Stack positions children relative to its own edges, and an unpositioned child fills the entire stack by default. Using Stack without understanding alignment and fit causes children to overlap or be clipped unexpectedly. Deeply nesting Stacks without Positioned constraints leads to render performance issues because Flutter cannot skip rendering occluded children automatically.

resources.label: Flutter Widget of the Week — Stack

children.title: MediaQuery & LayoutBuilder
children.description: MediaQuery provides screen dimensions relative to the root, while LayoutBuilder provides the constraints a specific widget receives from its parent — they are not the same. Using MediaQuery inside a widget deep in the tree when you only need the parent's available width makes the code fragile and recalculates on every screen change. LayoutBuilder is the correct tool for building widgets that adapt to their parent's constraints, not the screen.

children.title: Scrollable Layouts — ListView & SingleChildScrollView
children.description: ListView is lazy by default and only renders visible children — placing it inside a Column without a constrained height causes an unbounded height error because the Column gives infinite height to the ListView. Understanding shrinkWrap and physics parameters prevents the most common layout errors beginners encounter when combining scrollable and non-scrollable widgets.

flutter.title: State Management
flutter.description: Flutter's reactive UI is powered by state. Learn when to use setState, Provider, and Riverpod 2.0 — the modern solution for scalable Flutter state management.
topics: setState — when it's enough and when it's not
Shared state patterns — dependency injection with Riverpod
flutter.challenge: Rewrite your shopping app with Riverpod — a cart provider, a product list with async fetch state (loading/error/data), and a global theme toggle.

children.title: setState — Scope and Pitfalls
children.description: setState() triggers a rebuild of only the widget that calls it and all its descendants — calling it at a high level in the tree for a change that only affects a leaf widget causes expensive unnecessary rebuilds throughout your subtree. Moving state as close to where it is consumed as possible (state locality) is the first optimization principle in Flutter performance.

children.title: InheritedWidget & Provider Internals
children.description: Provider is built on top of InheritedWidget — it propagates state down the tree and notifies only the widgets that call context.watch(). Using context.read() instead of context.watch() when you need reactivity silently subscribes without triggering rebuilds, causing stale UI. Understanding the difference between watch, read, and select is fundamental to writing efficient Provider-based apps.

children.title: Providers & Notifiers
children.description: Riverpod 2.0 providers are globally declared and scoped by the ProviderScope — they are not tied to the widget tree lifecycle, making them easier to test than Provider. Forgetting to wrap your app in ProviderScope causes a ProviderNotFoundException at runtime, and using an autodispose provider for persistent state (like a user session) causes data loss on navigation.

children.title: AsyncNotifier & Async State
children.description: AsyncNotifier manages async state with three built-in states — loading, error, and data — expressed through AsyncValue. Trying to handle these three states manually with separate booleans leads to impossible combinations (isLoading && hasError) and inconsistent UI. ref.watch on an AsyncNotifier automatically rebuilds the widget on each state transition, eliminating the need for manual StreamBuilder or FutureBuilder wiring.

children.title: Events, States & BlocBuilder
children.description: BLoC separates UI events from business logic by routing all interactions through an event stream that emits new immutable states. Emitting the same state object reference twice does not trigger a rebuild because BLoC uses equality checks — using Equatable or copyWith to produce new state instances is mandatory. Skipping this produces a subtle bug where your UI does not update even though the data changed.

children.title: Cubit vs BLoC
children.description: Cubit is a simplified BLoC that emits states directly from methods rather than reacting to events — it is appropriate for UI-local state with straightforward transitions. Choosing full BLoC for every feature adds indirection without benefit; choosing Cubit for complex async flows with multiple concurrent events loses the event-driven traceability that makes BLoC debuggable. Pick the right abstraction for the complexity of the feature.

children.title: Persisting State Across Sessions
children.description: In-memory state is lost when the OS kills the app process — state management libraries alone do not persist data to disk. Hydrating a Riverpod provider or BLoC from SharedPreferences or Hive on startup must happen before the first frame renders to avoid a flash of default/empty state. Using synchronous read operations during initialization instead of async ones causes ANRs on Android if the storage read takes too long.

children.title: Dependency Injection with Riverpod
children.description: Riverpod replaces service locators and manual DI containers by making every provider a lazily-created singleton that can be overridden in tests. Not understanding provider overrides means your tests hit real network endpoints or databases, making them slow, flaky, and non-repeatable. Overriding providers in ProviderScope for tests is the Riverpod-idiomatic way to inject fakes without any DI framework.

flutter.title: Navigation & Routing
flutter.description: Learn Flutter's navigation system from the imperative Navigator API to the modern declarative GoRouter with deep linking support.
topics: GoRouter — route definitions, path params, redirect guards
Deep linking — Android intent filters, iOS URL schemes
Auth guards — redirect to login if not authenticated
flutter.challenge: Add GoRouter to your shopping app — product list → product detail route with path params, a protected checkout route, and a bottom tab shell.

children.title: Navigator 1.0 — Imperative Navigation
children.description: Navigator 1.0 uses a stack metaphor: push adds a route on top, pop removes it. Calling Navigator.push with the wrong context (e.g., one that has already been disposed) throws a 'Looking up a deactivated widget's ancestor is unsafe' error. Passing large objects directly to route constructors creates tight coupling between screens — prefer route arguments or state management for data sharing.

children.title: Named Routes & Route Generation
children.description: Named routes define URL-like paths for screens and enable deep linking but lose type safety when passing arguments as dynamic Maps. Forgetting to define a route in the routes table causes a runtime exception with no compile-time warning. For large apps, onGenerateRoute gives you centralized route handling with typed arguments, but GoRouter supersedes both patterns in modern Flutter.

children.title: Route Definitions & Path Parameters
children.description: GoRouter uses URL-based route definitions with path parameters (:id) that are parsed from the URL string — mistyping the parameter name in go() versus the route definition causes silent navigation failures where the parameter arrives as null. GoRouter integrates with the Flutter web URL bar and Android/iOS back button out of the box, unlike Navigator 1.0 which requires manual back-button handling.

children.title: Auth Guards & Redirect Logic
children.description: GoRouter's redirect callback runs on every navigation event and can divert users to login when they are unauthenticated. Not making the redirect reactive to auth state changes means a user who logs out stays on a protected screen until they navigate away. Returning null from redirect (instead of the login path) when unauthenticated causes an infinite redirect loop that crashes the app.

children.title: Android Intent Filters & iOS URL Schemes
children.description: Deep links are OS-level URLs that open your app at a specific screen. On Android, misconfiguring the intent filter's host or pathPattern causes the OS to not intercept the link — your browser opens instead of your app. On iOS, the Associated Domains entitlement must be correctly provisioned and the apple-app-site-association file must be served over HTTPS from your domain's root or .well-known path, or universal links silently fail.

children.title: ShellRoute & Nested Navigation
children.description: ShellRoute in GoRouter wraps multiple routes inside a persistent shell widget (like a bottom navigation bar) so the shell does not rebuild when navigating between tabs. Wrapping tab routes in a plain GoRoute instead of ShellRoute tears down and rebuilds the tab screens on every switch, losing scroll positions and UI state. The nested navigator pattern is the key to building tab-based apps that preserve per-tab state.

children.title: Router & RouteInformationParser
children.description: Navigator 2.0 is the low-level declarative API that GoRouter builds on top of. Understanding it explains why GoRouter behaves the way it does when the OS pushes a new route from a deep link or back gesture. The Router widget owns the back stack as a data structure — navigation is driven by changing that data, not by calling imperative methods, which is a fundamental mental model shift from Navigator 1.0.

children.title: Page & Route Transitions
children.description: Custom page transitions in Navigator 2.0 require implementing a Page subclass and a Route, not just wrapping push() with AnimationController. Using PageRouteBuilder with GoRouter requires custom transition wrappers per route — applying them globally requires a custom GoRouter page builder. Misapplying transitions on the wrong side of the navigator stack boundary causes animations that fight each other on tab switches.

resources.label: Flutter — Animate a Page Route Transition

flutter.title: Networking & Firebase
flutter.description: Connect your Flutter app to backend services. Use Dio for typed HTTP requests, Firebase for real-time data and auth, and Firestore for a serverless database.
topics: Firebase setup — FlutterFire CLI, platform config
Cloud Firestore — collections, queries, real-time listeners
flutter.challenge: Connect your shopping app to Firestore — real products from a Firestore collection, Firebase Auth for login, and a real-time cart shared across devices.

children.title: Dio Interceptors & Error Handling
children.description: Dio interceptors run before every request and after every response, making them the correct place for auth token injection and global error handling — not individual API call sites. Forgetting to add a retry interceptor means expired tokens cause 401 errors that surface as crashes instead of being transparently refreshed. Throwing DioException without catching it at the provider layer converts network errors into unhandled exceptions that crash the app.

children.title: JSON Serialization with Freezed
children.description: Freezed generates immutable data classes with copyWith, deep equality, and fromJson/toJson — hand-writing these for every model is error-prone and produces inconsistent equality behavior. Failing to run build_runner after changing a Freezed model leaves the generated .g.dart file out of sync, causing type errors that look like the model is missing fields. Using dynamic Map<String, dynamic> throughout your network layer instead of typed models hides API contract mismatches until runtime.

children.title: FlutterFire CLI & Platform Config
children.description: FlutterFire CLI generates the google-services.json (Android) and GoogleService-Info.plist (iOS) configuration files and registers them in the build system. Missing or misplaced config files cause Firebase initialization to fail at runtime with a 'No Firebase App [DEFAULT] has been created' error. Running flutterfire configure again after adding a new Firebase product regenerates these files with the new service enabled.

children.title: Firebase Auth — Email & Google Sign-In
children.description: Firebase Auth user state is asynchronous — the first authStateChanges event fires after the SDK restores the persisted session from disk, which takes a frame or two. Showing the home screen before the first auth event causes a visible flash of unauthenticated UI. Listening to authStateChanges as a stream (not a one-shot Future) handles sign-in, sign-out, and token refresh transparently across the app's lifetime.

children.title: Collections, Documents & Real-Time Listeners
children.description: Firestore's real-time listeners (snapshots()) are billed per document read on every change — attaching a listener to a large collection without a where clause reads every document on every write and can exhaust your free tier in minutes. Snapshots return QuerySnapshot objects that must be mapped to your typed models manually or via converter, and forgetting to cancel a StreamSubscription in dispose() leaks the Firestore connection.

children.title: Firestore Security Rules
children.description: Firestore security rules run server-side and are the only true security boundary — disabling rules during development and forgetting to re-enable them exposes every user's data publicly. Rules that use request.auth.uid to scope reads/writes to the authenticated user prevent horizontal privilege escalation even if a malicious client sends arbitrary document paths. Testing rules with the Firebase Emulator Suite before deploying prevents accidental data exposure.

children.title: Cloud Functions for Flutter
children.description: Cloud Functions let you run server-side Dart/Node.js logic triggered by Firestore events, HTTP calls, or Auth events — placing business logic like order processing in Cloud Functions prevents clients from bypassing it. Calling a Cloud Function from Flutter via httpsCallable passes structured data but requires matching the data schema exactly on both sides; type mismatches produce opaque 'INTERNAL' errors with no stack trace on the client.

children.title: Firebase Cloud Storage
children.description: Firebase Storage stores binary files (images, videos) indexed by a path that mirrors Firestore's document hierarchy. Uploading without storage security rules allows any authenticated user to overwrite any other user's files — always scope storage paths to request.auth.uid. Using putFile() streams the upload and provides progress callbacks; loading an entire file into memory with putData() on large files causes OOM crashes on low-end devices.

flutter.title: Local Storage & Native Features
flutter.description: Access device capabilities — store data locally, use the camera, send push notifications, and leverage platform-specific native APIs.
topics: Hive or Isar for local structured data
flutter.challenge: Add offline support to your shopping app — cache the product list in Hive, add a profile photo using image_picker, and send a local notification when an order is placed.

children.title: Hive for Structured Local Data
children.description: Hive is a lightweight NoSQL box-based database that stores typed Dart objects using generated adapters — it is significantly faster than SQLite for simple key-value and list storage because it uses binary encoding without SQL overhead. Forgetting to register a TypeAdapter before opening a box causes a HiveError at runtime; running build_runner and including generated adapter registrations in main() before runApp() is mandatory. Using a single box for all data without structuring it into multiple named boxes makes deletion and cache invalidation difficult.

children.title: SharedPreferences for Preferences
children.description: SharedPreferences is a synchronous-read, async-write key-value store backed by NSUserDefaults on iOS and SharedPreferences on Android — it is only suitable for small primitive values like theme settings or onboarding flags. Storing large blobs or complex objects in SharedPreferences (serialized as JSON strings) degrades startup performance because the entire file is loaded into memory. Use Hive or Isar for anything beyond simple scalar preferences.

children.title: image_picker & Camera Permissions
children.description: image_picker accesses the device camera and photo library, both of which require permission strings in Info.plist (iOS) and AndroidManifest.xml — submitting to the App Store without the NSCameraUsageDescription key causes immediate rejection. The returned XFile provides a path to a temporary file that is deleted after the session; copying it to the app's document directory before processing is required for persistence. Not handling the null return (user cancelled) causes a null dereference crash in the calling widget.

children.title: Permission Handler
children.description: Android and iOS both use a request-once model for sensitive permissions — once denied permanently, your app cannot ask again and must direct users to system settings. Using permission_handler to check the status before requesting prevents the redundant permission dialog that degrades UX. On iOS 14+, location requires both whenInUse and always permissions requested in the correct order, or the always permission request is silently ignored.

children.title: Firebase Cloud Messaging Setup
children.description: FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) delivers push notifications to both Android and iOS through a single API but requires separate platform setup — APNs (Apple Push Notification service) auth key configuration in the Firebase console is mandatory for iOS, and missing it causes iOS push to silently fail while Android continues working. The FCM token is device-specific, rotates periodically, and must be stored in Firestore per user to target specific devices; using a stale token causes delivery failures with no error visible to the user.

children.title: Local Notifications
children.description: flutter_local_notifications schedules and displays notifications entirely on-device without a server, making it ideal for reminders, alarms, and offline events. On Android 13+, POST_NOTIFICATIONS is a runtime permission that must be explicitly requested — apps that target API 33 without requesting it display no notifications silently. On iOS, foreground notification display requires configuring the presentation options (alert, badge, sound) in the onDidReceiveNotificationResponse handler.

children.title: Platform Channels for Custom Native Code
children.description: When no pub.dev package exists for a native API, Platform Channels let Flutter call native iOS (Swift/ObjC) or Android (Kotlin/Java) code through a typed message-passing interface. The channel name must match exactly on both sides — a typo causes MissingPluginException at runtime. MethodChannel calls are asynchronous and happen on the platform thread, so blocking work on the platform side freezes the entire app; dispatch to a background thread before doing heavy native work.

children.title: Isar — High-Performance Local DB
children.description: Isar is a fast, ACID-compliant NoSQL database for Flutter with a type-safe Dart query API generated at build time — it handles millions of records with indexed queries that return in microseconds. Unlike Hive, Isar supports transactions, relations, and full-text search natively. Forgetting to open the Isar instance in an Isolate for write-heavy operations blocks the main isolate and causes UI jank similar to unguarded network calls on the main thread.

flutter.title: Testing & Performance
flutter.description: Test your widgets and user flows, then profile and optimize rendering performance using Flutter DevTools.
topics: Unit tests — testing Riverpod providers, pure logic
Widget tests — pumpWidget, finder, tap, enter text
Flutter DevTools — widget inspector, performance overlay

resources.label: Very Good Ventures — Flutter Testing Guide

flutter.challenge: Write widget tests for your shopping app's cart — add to cart, remove, and total price. Run the Flutter performance overlay and eliminate any jank on the product list scroll.

children.title: Widget Tests — pumpWidget & Finders
children.description: Widget tests run in a simulated Flutter environment without a real device, making them faster than integration tests but more complete than unit tests. Forgetting to call tester.pump() after a state-changing action leaves the widget in its pre-action state, causing assertions to fail on the old UI. Using find.byType instead of find.byKey for widgets that appear multiple times returns multiple matches and causes a 'found multiple widgets' test error.

children.title: Unit Testing Riverpod Providers
children.description: Riverpod providers can be unit-tested outside the widget tree using ProviderContainer — creating a container with overrides injects fakes and makes tests deterministic without spinning up Flutter. Not awaiting a Future provider's result before asserting produces a race condition where the assertion runs against the loading state rather than the data state. Testing providers in isolation before testing the widgets that consume them makes failures easier to localise.

children.title: integration_test Package
children.description: The integration_test package runs real Flutter tests on a device or emulator, driving the full app stack including navigation and platform channels. Forgetting to call IntegrationTestWidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized() in the test main causes the binding to fall back to the widget test binding, silently skipping all real-device interactions. Integration tests are the only way to catch timing-dependent bugs like animations that stall network calls.

children.title: Golden Tests — Visual Regression
children.description: Golden tests capture a pixel-perfect screenshot of a widget and fail when the rendered output changes — they are the only automated way to catch unintended visual regressions from dependency upgrades. Running golden tests without --update-goldens first creates the reference images; running them without updating after an intentional UI change produces false failures. Golden files must be committed to version control and regenerated explicitly to approve visual changes.

children.title: Performance Overlay & Frame Analysis
children.description: Flutter's performance overlay shows two graphs: the UI thread (Dart) and the raster thread (GPU). Jank occurs when either thread takes more than 16ms per frame. Most beginners look at the wrong thread — a long raster bar usually means expensive ClipRRect or BackdropFilter operations, not Dart logic. Using RepaintBoundary to isolate frequently-animating subtrees stops their repaints from triggering repaints in static parent widgets.

children.title: Widget Inspector & Rebuild Counts
children.description: The Widget Inspector in DevTools shows the live widget tree, sizes, and — critically — how many times each widget rebuilt during a frame. Widgets that rebuild thousands of times per second indicate state is placed too high in the tree or a watch() is used where select() would only rebuild on the specific field that changed. The 'Track widget rebuilds' toggle highlights rebuilt widgets in orange so you can visually identify the expensive subtrees without reading profiler traces.

children.title: const Constructors & Widget Identity
children.description: Marking a widget constructor const lets the Dart compiler create a single canonical instance at compile time — Flutter's element reconciliation skips rebuilding elements whose widget reference has not changed. Omitting const on widgets that could be const forces Flutter to create new widget objects on every build() call, and the equality check fails even though the widget is logically identical. The 'prefer_const_constructors' lint rule flags these missed optimizations automatically.

children.title: ListView.builder & Lazy Loading
children.description: ListView.builder creates child widgets on demand as they scroll into view — using ListView with a pre-built list of children materializes every widget at once and causes memory spikes for large datasets. The itemExtent parameter unlocks scroll position prediction that lets Flutter skip layout for off-screen items; omitting it forces Flutter to lay out every realized item to compute the scrollbar position, which is measurably slower for lists above 100 items.

flutter.title: Publishing & CI/CD
flutter.description: Ship your Flutter app to both stores. Automate builds and releases with Fastlane and GitHub Actions, and navigate the App Store and Play Store submission process.
topics: iOS — provisioning profiles, certificates, App Store Connect
Android — keystore, Play Console, aab build
GitHub Actions for Flutter — build, test, deploy
flutter.challenge: Set up a GitHub Actions pipeline that runs tests, builds release APK and IPA, and uses Fastlane to publish to TestFlight and the Play Store internal track.

children.title: iOS Code Signing & Provisioning
children.description: iOS code signing requires a valid Development or Distribution certificate paired with a provisioning profile that lists your app's bundle ID, entitlements, and allowed devices. A mismatch between the provisioning profile's bundle ID and the Xcode project's bundle identifier causes 'No provisioning profiles found' errors that block every build. Fastlane match automates certificate and profile synchronization across team members via a shared encrypted Git repository, eliminating manual certificate management.

children.title: Android Keystore & Release Signing
children.description: Android release builds must be signed with a keystore file — losing the keystore or its password means you can never publish an update to an existing app on the Play Store, requiring a brand-new app listing. The keystore must be stored securely outside the repository and referenced via environment variables or a secrets manager in CI. Signing with the debug keystore for a release build produces an APK that Google Play rejects with 'APK is not signed with the upload key' error.

children.title: Fastlane Lanes for Flutter
children.description: Fastlane lanes are Ruby scripts that chain actions (build, sign, upload) into repeatable pipelines invoked by a single command. Running flutter build ios and flutter build appbundle before fastlane deliver ensures Fastlane uploads the already-built artifact rather than triggering a second build inside the lane. Not setting the FASTLANE_APPLE_APPLICATION_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD environment variable causes App Store Connect authentication to fail with a two-factor auth error in CI environments.

children.title: Shorebird OTA Updates
children.description: Shorebird patches Dart-layer code changes over the air without a store release — only Dart code and assets can be patched; native code changes (Kotlin/Swift/C++) always require a full store update. A patch is downloaded on the next app launch after Shorebird's update check, not immediately; users who never restart the app never receive the patch. Shorebird's updater is incompatible with Flutter's DevTools attach mode, so development must still use normal flutter run.

children.title: Flutter CI Workflow Setup
children.description: A minimal Flutter CI workflow installs the correct Flutter version with subosito/flutter-action, runs flutter pub get, flutter analyze, flutter test, and then flutter build — running tests before the build fails fast and avoids spending minutes on a build for code that does not pass linting. Caching the pub cache between runs with actions/cache reduces CI time by 2-3 minutes per run for large dependency graphs.

children.title: Secrets & Environment Variables in CI
children.description: Firebase config files, keystore passwords, and App Store Connect API keys must never be committed to the repository — they should be stored as encrypted GitHub Actions secrets and written to disk during the workflow. Hardcoding secrets in YAML files leaks them in the repository history even after deletion, as git history is public on open-source repos. Using a base64-encoded secret for binary files (keystores, p12 certificates) and decoding them in the workflow step is the standard pattern.

children.title: App Store Connect Submission
children.description: App Store Connect requires a valid app icon at 1024x1024 (no transparency), at least one screenshot per supported device size, and a privacy manifest listing all data collected. Submitting without the PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file in Flutter apps targeting iOS 17+ causes an automatic rejection email from Apple. The binary upload must be done via Xcode Organizer or Transporter — direct HTTP uploads to the iTunesConnect API require the App Store Connect API key set up in Fastlane deliver.

children.title: Google Play Console Submission
children.description: Google Play requires an Android App Bundle (AAB), not an APK, for new app submissions — AABs allow Play to serve device-optimized APK splits that reduce download size by up to 50%. The app must pass pre-launch report testing on the Play Console's automated test devices before promotion to production; crashes detected in the pre-launch report block promotion.
