Setting up internal, closed, and open tracks allows staged rollouts that limit blast radius for regressions.

flutter.title: Advanced Flutter - Custom Rendering and Multi-Platform Targets
flutter.description: Go beyond standard widgets by implementing custom render objects and painters for pixel-perfect UI needs that the widget layer cannot express. Extend Flutter to desktop and web targets while bridging platform-specific native SDKs through platform channels.
topics: Custom RenderObject and RenderBox from scratch
CustomPainter and Canvas API for complex graphics
Platform channels and MethodChannel for native SDK bridging
Flutter Web with CanvasKit vs HTML renderer trade-offs
Desktop window management and keyboard/mouse input handling
FFI with dart:ffi for calling native C libraries
Isolate communication for CPU-intensive render work

resources.label: Advanced Flutter - Custom Widgets and Rendering

flutter.challenge: Build a chart widget using a fully custom RenderObject with hit-testing, animate it with an AnimationController, expose it as a platform channel to swap the data source from a native SQL query on iOS and Android.

children.title: CustomPainter & Canvas API
children.description: CustomPainter gives direct access to Flutter's Skia/Impeller canvas, enabling arbitrary 2D graphics that no built-in widget can produce. The paint() method is called on every animation frame, so performing heavy computations (path calculations, trigonometry) inside paint() without caching drops frame rate to below 60fps. The shouldRepaint() method must return true only when the painter's inputs change — always returning true forces repaints every frame even for static graphics, wasting GPU resources.

children.title: Custom RenderObject
children.description: RenderObject is the lowest level of Flutter's rendering pipeline — it controls layout, painting, and hit testing without any widget-layer overhead. Implementing a custom RenderBox requires correctly implementing performLayout() to measure children and set their sizes, and paint() to draw into the canvas at the correct offset. Forgetting to call markNeedsPaint() when inputs change causes the render object to display stale visuals without rebuilding, a bug that is invisible in widget tests.

children.title: MethodChannel for Native SDK Bridging
children.description: MethodChannel is the standard way to call iOS or Android native APIs that have no Flutter plugin — the channel name is an arbitrary string that must match exactly on the Flutter and native sides. All method channel calls cross an asynchronous IPC boundary, so calling them in a tight loop (e.g., per-frame) creates significant latency and jank. For high-frequency native calls, use BasicMessageChannel with a binary codec or dart:ffi to avoid the serialization overhead of MethodChannel.

children.title: dart:ffi for Native C Libraries
children.description: dart:ffi lets Dart call native C functions directly without the platform channel hop, making it appropriate for CPU-bound tasks like image processing or cryptography where MethodChannel latency is prohibitive. ffi calls execute synchronously on the calling Dart isolate — blocking ffi calls (e.g., a slow C library) still freeze the UI thread unless you dispatch them to a separate isolate using Isolate.spawn or compute(). Incorrect pointer arithmetic or missing null checks in ffi code causes native crashes that produce no Dart stack trace.

children.title: CanvasKit vs HTML Renderer
children.description: Flutter Web supports two rendering backends: HTML renderer (smaller initial download, uses browser DOM) and CanvasKit (full Skia in WebAssembly, pixel-perfect fidelity with mobile but ~2MB download overhead). Using CanvasKit for apps where SEO and initial load time matter is the wrong trade-off; using the HTML renderer for apps with complex custom painting produces visual differences from mobile that are difficult to debug. Choosing the renderer in the flutter build web --web-renderer flag affects every user's experience, not just development.

children.title: Impeller Rendering Engine
children.description: Impeller is Flutter's replacement for Skia on iOS (default since Flutter 3.10) and Android (opt-in), using pre-compiled Metal/Vulkan shaders to eliminate the shader compilation jank that caused first-frame animation stutter in Skia. Apps that see a jank spike on the first animation frame after switching from Skia to Impeller have custom shaders (via FragmentProgram) that need to be updated for Impeller's shader API. Impeller is not yet available on Flutter Web, which continues to use Skia/CanvasKit.

children.title: Flutter Desktop Targets
children.description: Flutter supports macOS, Windows, and Linux as first-class targets — the same Dart/Flutter code compiles to a native desktop app with platform window chrome. Desktop apps must handle keyboard and mouse input, window resizing, and focus management that mobile apps never encounter; a widget that works on mobile but crashes on desktop is usually missing MouseRegion or FocusNode handling. Desktop plugin support lags behind mobile — always check the pub.dev platform availability matrix before choosing a dependency for a multi-platform project.

children.title: Isolate Communication for Render Work
children.description: CPU-intensive work (JSON parsing, image decoding, path tessellation) that blocks the main isolate drops the frame rate visibly. Flutter's compute() is a convenience wrapper that sends a single function to a background isolate and returns the result as a Future — it is appropriate for one-shot tasks. For streaming data (e.g., a custom codec that emits decoded frames), a persistent isolate with a ReceivePort loop is more efficient than spawning a new isolate per task.

flutter.title: Production Flutter Apps - Analytics, A/B Testing and Store Optimization
flutter.description: Operate Flutter apps in production by integrating crash reporting, event analytics, and controlled feature rollouts that let you validate changes without full store releases. Learn how to manage code-push updates, optimize store listings, and use data to drive product decisions.
topics: Crash reporting and stack trace symbolication with Firebase Crashlytics
Event analytics schema design and funnel tracking
A/B testing experiments with Firebase Remote Config
Shorebird code-push for Dart-layer OTA updates
App Store and Play Store listing optimization (ASO)
Staged rollouts and phased releases on both stores
Compliance with store policies for data privacy and permissions
flutter.challenge: Integrate Crashlytics and Firebase Analytics into an existing Flutter app, set up two Remote Config A/B test variants for an onboarding screen, deploy a Shorebird patch to fix a UI bug, and verify the fix via the Shorebird dashboard without a store update.

children.title: Firebase Crashlytics Integration
children.description: Crashlytics captures Flutter exceptions and native crashes and symbolically maps them to source code line numbers using dSYMs (iOS) or ProGuard mappings (Android). Without uploading debug symbols to Crashlytics, stack traces show obfuscated method names that are impossible to map to source code — symbol upload must be automated in every CI build that produces a release artifact. Setting a custom key or user identifier in Crashlytics before the crash occurs provides the reproduction context that makes intermittent bugs diagnosable.

children.title: Firebase Analytics Event Schema
children.description: Firebase Analytics automatically logs screen views, sessions, and first opens — custom events require a deliberate naming scheme because Firebase limits you to 500 distinct event names per app and the names cannot be deleted. Logging raw UI action names (button_tap) without semantic context (checkout_started) produces analytics that cannot answer product questions. Planning the event taxonomy before instrumentation avoids the naming debt that forces full analytics re-instrumentation later.

children.title: Firebase Remote Config
children.description: Remote Config lets you change app behavior without a store update by fetching key-value parameters from Firebase — the fetch is cached for a minimum interval (12 hours by default) to prevent excessive network calls. Using Remote Config to control feature flags requires calling fetchAndActivate() at app startup and awaiting it before reading any parameter, otherwise you read the previous session's cached values instead of the latest ones. Parameter values are always strings on the wire — using getInt/getBool helpers prevents type coercion bugs.

children.title: A/B Testing Experiments
children.description: Firebase A/B Testing runs controlled experiments by splitting users into variant groups based on Remote Config parameter values — users are randomly assigned once per install and stay in the same variant for the experiment's duration. Assigning variant groups at feature entry rather than at app startup causes users to switch variants mid-session as they navigate, invalidating the experiment's statistical assumptions. The experiment must define a single primary metric (e.g., checkout conversion rate) before launch; adding metrics post-hoc to find significance is p-hacking.

children.title: Deploying Patches with Shorebird
children.description: Shorebird patches replace the compiled Dart snapshot in an installed app without a store review — the patch is applied on the next cold start, not hot reload style during a running session. A patch can only be applied on top of the exact release build it targets; patching a version that users have not yet downloaded does nothing for those users. Shorebird's rollback command reverts all users on the next launch if a patch introduces a regression, making it safer than a traditional hotfix that requires a full store update.

children.title: Shorebird Limitations & Native Code
children.description: Shorebird can only patch Dart code and Flutter asset changes — any change that touches Kotlin, Swift, Objective-C, or C++ code requires a full native rebuild and store submission. Attempting to use Shorebird to patch an app that added a new native plugin dependency produces a compatibility error because the native library is missing from the installed binary. Designing your architecture to isolate native-dependent features (camera, BLE, biometrics) from frequently-changing Dart business logic maximizes what can be hot-patched.

children.title: ASO — Keywords & Store Listing
children.description: App Store Optimization (ASO) is the practice of improving an app's discoverability and conversion in the store through keyword placement, screenshots, and review ratings. The App Store keyword field supports 100 characters and is not shown to users — stuffing it with commas and spaces wastes character budget. Screenshots are the most impactful conversion element on both stores; A/B testing screenshot designs with store experiments produces measurable lift in install rate without any code change.

children.title: Staged Rollouts & Phased Releases
children.description: Both stores support staged rollouts that expose new versions to a percentage of users before full release — Google Play lets you define the percentage and pause/halt at any point, while App Store Phased Release always follows a fixed 7-day schedule. Monitoring crash-free rate and ANR rate in Firebase Crashlytics during the rollout window provides the signal to halt if regressions appear. A halted rollout does not push the update to new users but does not revert already-updated users, so Shorebird patches remain the only way to remediate those users without a new version.

ios.title: Swift Fundamentals
ios.description: Swift is Apple's powerful, type-safe language for iOS, macOS, and beyond. Learn its syntax, type system, protocol-oriented programming, and Swift concurrency.
topics: Variables — let/var, type inference, optionals ?, !, guard let
Functions — default params, closures, trailing closure syntax
OOP — classes, structs, enums with associated values
Error handling — throws, try/catch, Result type

resources.label: Hacking with Swift — 100 Days of Swift

ios.challenge: Build a Swift CLI app that models a social media timeline — structs for Post/User, a feed algorithm (sorted by engagement), and async fetching from a mock API.

children.title: Value vs Reference Types
children.description: Swift structs and enums are value types — copied on assignment — while classes share a reference. Choosing a class when a struct would do causes unintended shared mutable state and breaks SwiftUI's diffing, because the framework never sees a new value to trigger a re-render.

children.title: Automatic Reference Counting (ARC)
children.description: ARC tracks retain counts for class instances; when count reaches zero the object is deallocated. Forgetting [weak self] in closures creates a retain cycle — both the object and the closure keep each other alive indefinitely, leaking memory and causing silent bugs.

resources.label: Hacking with Swift — Weak and Unowned

children.title: Optional Chaining & Guard
children.description: Optionals encode the presence or absence of a value in the type system, eliminating null-pointer crashes. Force-unwrapping with ! bypasses this guarantee entirely — a nil value at runtime immediately crashes the app with a fatal error, which is why guard let and if let are preferred.

children.title: Protocols & Protocol-Oriented Design
children.description: Protocols define contracts that any type can conform to, enabling composition over inheritance. Without protocol-oriented design you end up with deep class hierarchies that are rigid and hard to test; protocols allow cheap mock implementations and keep dependencies swappable.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Protocol-Oriented Programming in Swift

children.title: Closures & Capture Lists
children.description: Closures are self-contained blocks of code that capture variables from their surrounding scope. Without explicit capture lists, strong captures keep objects alive longer than intended; in network callbacks this commonly produces retain cycles where the view controller never deinitialises.

children.title: Swift Concurrency — async/await & Actors
children.description: Swift Concurrency replaces completion-handler callback pyramids with structured, readable async/await code. Ignoring actor isolation — updating UI state from a background Task — causes data races and UI glitches; marking ViewModels with @MainActor ensures all published property changes occur on the main thread.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Meet async/await in Swift

children.title: throws, Result & Error Types
children.description: Swift's typed error handling forces you to acknowledge failures at the call site rather than ignoring them silently. Using try! converts a thrown error into a fatal crash; modeling errors with enums conforming to Error lets callers distinguish recoverable and unrecoverable failures.

children.title: Generics & Type Constraints
children.description: Generics let you write algorithms and data structures that work with any type satisfying a constraint, removing redundant code. Without generics, a networking layer requires separate implementations per response type; a generic APIClient<T: Decodable> handles all endpoints in one place.

ios.title: SwiftUI Fundamentals
ios.description: SwiftUI is Apple's declarative UI framework. Learn to think in Views, understand the state-driven rendering model, and compose interfaces with modifiers.
topics: Layout — HStack, VStack, ZStack, LazyVStack, Grid
Common views — Text, Image, Button, TextField, List, Form
View modifiers — padding, background, cornerRadius, overlay

resources.label: Hacking with Swift — 100 Days of SwiftUI

ios.challenge: Build a SwiftUI contacts app — List of contacts, search bar, add contact sheet, detail view, and a custom color theme.

children.title: View Identity & Diffing
children.description: SwiftUI re-renders only the subtree whose state changed, comparing view identity structurally. Placing @State deep inside a view that gets recreated discards the state on every parent render; using stable identifiers in ForEach prevents SwiftUI from destroying and recreating cells unnecessarily.

children.title: @State & Single Source of Truth
children.description: @State is SwiftUI's mechanism for owning mutable local view state; the framework stores the value externally so it survives view struct re-creation. Misusing @State by sharing it across sibling views instead of lifting it to a common ancestor produces desynchronised UI where one component sees a stale value.

children.title: HStack, VStack & ZStack
children.description: SwiftUI's stack containers propose sizes to children, collect their reported sizes, and then position them. Nesting deep stacks without using Lazy variants loads all children at once — on a long list this means thousands of views in memory before the user scrolls, causing severe performance degradation.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Build a Great Mac App with SwiftUI

children.title: ViewModifier & View Composition
children.description: Modifiers return new views wrapping the receiver, forming a chain that is fully described in the type system. Applying modifiers in the wrong order changes the visual result — for example, adding padding before background leaves the background colour on the text bounds, not the padded area.

children.title: @Binding — Two-Way Data Flow
children.description: @Binding creates a reference to state owned elsewhere, enabling child views to read and mutate parent state without owning it. Passing a constant binding in previews or constructing bindings from arbitrary closures instead of real state sources breaks the single-source-of-truth contract and causes one-way updates.

children.title: @ObservedObject & @EnvironmentObject
children.description: @ObservedObject subscribes a view to a reference-type model, re-rendering when any @Published property changes. Passing an @ObservedObject down five layers of views instead of using @EnvironmentObject creates prop-drilling; forgetting to inject the environment object before use crashes with a fatal diagnostic at runtime.

children.title: List & ForEach with Identifiable
children.description: List and ForEach require stable identifiers to animate insertions, deletions, and reorders correctly. Using .self as the id for a non-Hashable or non-unique value produces undefined behaviour — items may flicker, merge, or vanish as SwiftUI cannot distinguish between rows with duplicate identifiers.

children.title: #Preview Macro & Canvas
children.description: The #Preview macro (Xcode 15+) compiles and renders views in the canvas without running the app, accelerating iteration. Not providing environment dependencies to previews causes them to fail silently or crash; injecting mocks with .environment() ensures previews reflect real runtime conditions without a live device.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Build Programmatic UI with Xcode Previews

ios.title: Architecture — MVVM & Combine
ios.description: Structure your SwiftUI apps for maintainability with MVVM, reactive data flow using Combine (or the new @Observable macro in iOS 17+), and clean separation of concerns.
topics: Swift Concurrency in ViewModels — Task, MainActor

resources.label: Apple Developer — Data Flow in SwiftUI

ios.challenge: Refactor your contacts app with a ViewModel using @Observable, a ContactsRepository protocol, and dependency-injected mock for previews.

children.title: ObservableObject & @Published
children.description: ObservableObject publishes change notifications via objectWillChange before any @Published property mutates, letting SwiftUI schedule a re-render. Mutating @Published properties from a background thread bypasses the main-thread UI contract and causes runtime warnings or invisible updates that never appear on screen.

children.title: @Observable Macro (iOS 17+)
children.description: @Observable rewrites the class at compile time to track access per property rather than broadcasting a single objectWillChange signal, so only views that read a mutated property re-render. Mixing @Observable with the older ObservableObject protocol on the same class triggers double-publication and redundant renders.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Discover Observation in SwiftUI

children.title: ViewModel Responsibilities
children.description: A ViewModel transforms raw model data into view-ready state and handles user intents, keeping Views free of business logic. Putting network calls or Core Data fetches directly in a View body couples the UI to infrastructure, making it impossible to unit test the logic or swap the data source without rewriting the view.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Data Essentials in SwiftUI

children.title: Dependency Injection with Protocols
children.description: Injecting dependencies through protocol abstractions rather than concrete types makes ViewModels testable with mocks and previews work without real network calls. Hardcoding URLSession.shared or a concrete repository inside a ViewModel makes unit testing impossible without actual network traffic.

children.title: Publishers, Subscribers & Operators
children.description: Combine models asynchronous sequences of values as Publishers that subscribers listen to over time. Forgetting to store AnyCancellable tokens in a Set or property causes subscriptions to deallocate immediately — the publisher fires but no subscriber receives the value, producing silent data-loss bugs.

children.title: Combine vs Swift Concurrency
children.description: Combine excels at declarative multi-step transformation pipelines (debounce, merge, flatMap), while async/await is cleaner for single-shot operations. Mixing both without understanding back-pressure — where the subscriber cannot process values as fast as the publisher emits — can cause memory pressure or dropped events.

resources.label: Hacking with Swift — Combine vs async/await

children.title: Repository Pattern
children.description: The repository pattern centralises data-source logic behind a protocol, so the ViewModel never cares whether data comes from a remote API, a local cache, or a mock. Bypassing this layer and calling URLSession from a ViewModel ties business logic directly to transport-layer details, making offline support or API versioning a full rewrite.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — App Architecture: MVVM in SwiftUI
Hacking with Swift — Building a Repository

children.title: @MainActor & Thread Safety
children.description: @MainActor guarantees that annotated code executes on the main thread, preventing UI updates from racing with background work. Publishing SwiftUI state changes from a Task without await MainActor.run causes thread-checker warnings in Xcode and occasional rendering glitches that are extremely hard to reproduce in testing.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Protect mutable state with Swift actors

ios.title: Data Persistence — SwiftData & Core Data
ios.description: Persist data locally on iOS with SwiftData (iOS 17+ modern ORM), Core Data for older targets, and UserDefaults/Keychain for lightweight storage.
topics: CloudKit + Core Data for iCloud sync
ios.challenge: Add SwiftData persistence to your contacts app — contacts survive app restarts, enable iCloud sync with CloudKit, and store sensitive notes in the Keychain.

children.title: @Model & ModelContainer
children.description: SwiftData's @Model macro generates the persistence schema from your Swift class properties, and ModelContainer wires the store to a SwiftUI environment. Defining conflicting model relationships without specifying delete rules causes dangling references — when a parent is deleted, orphaned children remain in the store consuming space.

children.title: @Query & Predicates
children.description: @Query fetches and live-updates a list of SwiftData models in a view, similar to @FetchRequest. Forgetting to include sort descriptors returns models in undefined order that changes between app launches; complex predicates not supported by SwiftData's compiler-time checking silently fall back to fetching all objects and filtering in memory.

children.title: NSManagedObjectContext & Concurrency
children.description: Core Data managed object contexts are not thread-safe — every access must happen on the queue the context was created for. Accessing NSManagedObjects from a background thread without using perform {} triggers data races that manifest as random crashes, often after hours of use, making them extremely hard to diagnose.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Using Core Data with CloudKit

children.title: Migrations — Lightweight vs Heavy
children.description: Core Data lightweight migrations infer a mapping model automatically when changes are additive — adding attributes, renaming with renamingIdentifier. Making structural changes (splitting entities, changing attribute types) without a custom migration mapping model corrupts the persistent store on upgrade and requires manual data-recovery code.

children.title: NSPersistentCloudKitContainer
children.description: NSPersistentCloudKitContainer extends NSPersistentContainer to automatically mirror Core Data changes to a private CloudKit database. Failing to enable the CloudKit capability and iCloud entitlement causes silent sync failures — the local store saves correctly but changes never reach other devices, and the user has no indication.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Sync a Core Data store with CloudKit

children.title: Keychain for Secure Storage
children.description: The iOS Keychain encrypts small sensitive values (tokens, passwords) and persists them across app reinstalls, unlike UserDefaults which stores data in plaintext. Storing access tokens in UserDefaults exposes them to anyone who can read the device's sandbox — on a jailbroken device this is trivial.

children.title: UserDefaults & @AppStorage
children.description: UserDefaults stores small preference values (booleans, strings, numbers) in a plist file, surfaced in SwiftUI via @AppStorage. Storing large blobs (images, complex objects) in UserDefaults causes slow app launches because the entire defaults domain is loaded into memory synchronously on startup.

children.title: File Manager & Codable Persistence
children.description: FileManager gives direct access to the app's sandboxed Documents, Caches, and tmp directories for storing arbitrary files. Writing to the Documents directory instead of Caches for ephemeral data inflates iCloud backup size and can trigger App Store rejection for excessive backup usage policy violations.

ios.title: Networking & Async
ios.description: Fetch data from REST APIs using URLSession with Swift Concurrency. Decode with Codable, handle errors gracefully, and cache responses efficiently.

resources.label: Donny Wals — Modern Networking in Swift

ios.challenge: Add a REST API layer to your contacts app — fetch contacts from a mock server, decode with Codable, cache with SwiftData, and show loading/error states.

children.title: URLSession — data(from:) with async/await
children.description: URLSession is the foundation of all HTTP communication on iOS; the async/await API returns data and response together without callback nesting. Not checking the HTTP status code after a successful URLSession call means a 404 or 500 is treated as success — your Codable decoder then fails on an error JSON body instead of the expected model.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Use async/await with URLSession

children.title: Codable — Encodable & Decodable
children.description: Codable synthesises JSON encoding and decoding automatically for types with matching property names. When server keys use snake_case and Swift properties use camelCase, forgetting to set decoder.keyDecodingStrategy = .convertFromSnakeCase causes every property to decode as nil — a silent failure that is easy to miss until data appears blank in the UI.

children.title: APIClient Pattern
children.description: A generic APIClient protocol centralises request construction, authentication headers, error parsing, and response decoding so each endpoint is a one-liner. Duplicating URLSession calls across features creates divergent error handling — one feature retries on 401, another shows a broken-state UI, leading to inconsistent user experience.

children.title: Image Loading — AsyncImage & Kingfisher
children.description: AsyncImage handles remote image loading declaratively but re-downloads images every time the view appears because it has no persistent disk cache. For feeds with many images, each scroll redownloads everything, causing visible flickering and wasted bandwidth; libraries like Kingfisher add memory and disk caching transparently.

children.title: Alamofire — responseDecodable & Interceptors
children.description: Alamofire wraps URLSession with a fluent API, automatic response validation, and a powerful interceptor system for token refresh and retry logic. Building token refresh manually with URLSession requires synchronising concurrent 401 responses to avoid sending multiple refresh requests — Alamofire's RequestInterceptor serialises this correctly out of the box.

children.title: Offline-First with SwiftData Cache
children.description: An offline-first architecture caches API responses in SwiftData so the app renders useful data immediately and fetches fresh data in the background. Not implementing a cache strategy means the app shows an empty loading screen on every launch, which fails Apple's Human Interface Guidelines requirement for instant usability.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Advances in Networking Part 2

children.title: Network Error Modelling
children.description: Modelling network failures as an exhaustive enum (noConnectivity, serverError, decodingFailure, unauthorized) lets the UI show specific recovery actions rather than a generic alert. Catching all errors under a single catch clause loses context — the user cannot tell whether to check their Wi-Fi or wait for a server fix.

children.title: Loading & Empty States in SwiftUI
children.description: Representing async operation state as an enum (idle, loading, success(T), failure(Error)) drives SwiftUI views that show skeletons, content, and error messages without scattered boolean flags. Using multiple @State booleans (isLoading, hasError, isEmpty) creates impossible combinations — isLoading and hasError both true at once — causing UI inconsistencies.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Data Essentials in SwiftUI

ios.title: Navigation & Advanced SwiftUI
ios.description: Navigate between views using the modern NavigationStack API, build iPad-ready split views, and add fluid animations with SwiftUI's animation system.
ios.challenge: Add NavigationStack with a contacts list → detail → edit flow, animate the card entrance with matchedGeometryEffect, and add a sidebar layout for iPad.

children.title: NavigationStack & Path
children.description: NavigationStack (iOS 16+) replaces NavigationView with a value-driven navigation model — a path array controls the entire stack programmatically. Mixing NavigationView and NavigationStack in the same hierarchy produces conflicting navigation controllers; using NavigationView on iOS 16+ loses deep-link and state-restoration capabilities built into NavigationStack.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — The SwiftUI cookbook for navigation

children.title: navigationDestination & Deep Linking
children.description: navigationDestination(for:) maps value types to destination views, enabling type-safe deep linking by simply appending values to the path. Relying on sheet/fullScreenCover chains for navigation prevents deep links from working — you cannot restore navigation state from a URL because sheet presentation is not path-based.

children.title: NavigationSplitView for iPad & Mac
children.description: NavigationSplitView renders a sidebar, content, and detail column on large screens, collapsing to a single stack on iPhone. Using NavigationStack on iPad instead of NavigationSplitView wastes screen space and fails Apple's iPad Human Interface Guidelines, typically resulting in App Store reviewer feedback requesting a redesign.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — SwiftUI on iPad — Organize your interface

children.title: Sheets, Popovers & fullScreenCover
children.description: SwiftUI presents modal content via .sheet, .popover, and .fullScreenCover, each with different dismiss and presentation semantics. Presenting multiple sheets from the same view using a single boolean triggers only the first registered sheet; using item-based presentation with an optional Identifiable value correctly handles which sheet to show.

children.title: withAnimation & Implicit Animations
children.description: SwiftUI animations interpolate state changes; wrapping a state mutation in withAnimation triggers smooth transitions instead of instant jumps. Animating non-animatable properties (like replacing a view entirely rather than transitioning it) has no visual effect — SwiftUI cannot interpolate between incompatible view types, so understanding AnyTransition is essential.

children.title: matchedGeometryEffect & Hero Animations
children.description: matchedGeometryEffect synchronises the geometry of two views across a namespace, creating hero transitions between screens. Using it without a @Namespace variable, or attaching it to views in different SwiftUI transaction scopes, breaks the animation — the views snap instead of morphing, which looks unpolished in production.

children.title: Gesture Recognisers in SwiftUI
children.description: SwiftUI provides TapGesture, LongPressGesture, DragGesture, and MagnificationGesture that compose cleanly with view modifiers. Using UITapGestureRecognizer via UIViewRepresentable alongside SwiftUI gestures creates hit-testing conflicts where UIKit intercepts touches SwiftUI expected — the correct approach is SwiftUI-native gestures with .simultaneously or .exclusively combinators.

children.title: AnyTransition & Custom Transitions
children.description: AnyTransition describes how a view enters and leaves the screen; .asymmetric allows different insertion and removal animations. Forgetting to wrap state changes that trigger transitions in withAnimation produces instant on/off appearance — the transition code is correct but never executes because no animation context exists.

ios.title: Testing iOS Apps
ios.description: Write unit, integration, and UI tests for your iOS app using XCTest and the new Swift Testing framework introduced in Xcode 16.
topics: Code coverage in Xcode — measuring and improving
ios.challenge: Write unit tests for your ViewModel, mock the ContactsRepository, test a user flow end-to-end with XCUITest, and achieve >80% code coverage.

children.title: XCTestCase & Assertions
children.description: XCTest is Apple's built-in testing framework; XCTestCase subclasses group related tests with setUp/tearDown lifecycle hooks. Not isolating tests with fresh state in setUp causes test-order dependencies — a test passes in isolation but fails in a suite because a previous test mutated shared state, making failures non-reproducible.

children.title: Async Testing — expectation & async throws
children.description: Testing async code requires XCTestExpectation.fulfill() or marking tests as async throws so XCTest awaits completion before asserting. Using a fixed sleep(1) instead of proper async test support produces flaky tests — the test passes on a fast CI machine but fails when the async operation takes slightly longer than the sleep.

children.title: @Test & @Suite Macros
children.description: Swift Testing (SE-0459, available Xcode 16+) replaces XCTestCase with macro-based @Test functions and @Suite types, enabling parameterised tests natively. Using XCTest for parameterised data requires looping inside a single test, meaning one failure aborts all cases — Swift Testing runs each parameter independently so you see exactly which inputs fail.

children.title: Mocking with Protocols
children.description: Protocol-based mocking replaces real URLSession or repository implementations with controlled fakes in unit tests. Testing ViewModels with a real URLSession makes tests slow, network-dependent, and non-deterministic; a MockNetworkClient returning hardcoded JSON makes tests run in milliseconds and verify exact behaviour.

children.title: XCUITest — Accessibility Identifiers
children.description: XCUITest drives the app through its accessibility layer — tapping, swiping, and asserting on element existence. Not setting .accessibilityIdentifier on interactive elements forces tests to query by display text, breaking every time copy changes; identifiers decouple test selectors from UI text so refactors do not break the test suite.

children.title: Launch Arguments & UI Test Mocking
children.description: Passing ProcessInfo launch arguments from XCUITest into the app lets you configure the app to use mock data without changing production code. Without this pattern, UI tests hit real servers, making them slow, flaky, and able to accidentally mutate production data — a situation that violates App Store guidelines for automated testing.

children.title: Code Coverage in Xcode
children.description: Xcode's code coverage report shows which lines are executed during tests, highlighting untested branches. Treating 100% coverage as the goal often leads to tests that execute code without asserting correctness — coverage measures execution, not correctness, so combining coverage with meaningful assertions is what actually prevents regressions.

children.title: Test Plans & CI Integration
children.description: Xcode Test Plans group test targets with shared environment configurations, allowing different plans for unit tests, integration tests, and accessibility audits. Running all tests in a single scheme without test plans means slow UI tests block the fast unit-test feedback loop, discouraging developers from running tests frequently.

ios.title: App Store Publishing
ios.description: Ship your iOS app to the App Store. Configure code signing, test with TestFlight, automate with Xcode Cloud or Fastlane, and navigate the App Review process.
topics: Code signing — certificates, provisioning profiles, Apple Developer
App capabilities — Push Notifications, iCloud, In-App Purchases
TestFlight — internal and external beta testing
Xcode Cloud — CI/CD built into Xcode
App Store metadata — screenshots, ASO, privacy labels
App Review guidelines — common rejection reasons
ios.challenge: Set up Xcode Cloud to run your test suite on every push, build a TestFlight build on merge to main, and complete the App Store Connect submission form with screenshots.

children.title: Xcode Cloud — Workflows
children.description: Xcode Cloud runs build, test, and archive workflows on Apple's infrastructure triggered by branch events, providing native integration with App Store Connect. Not configuring post-action archive steps correctly produces a workflow that builds successfully but never delivers an artifact to TestFlight, silently blocking the release pipeline.

children.title: Fastlane — match & deliver
children.description: Fastlane automates certificate management (match), screenshot generation (snapshot), and App Store submission (deliver) in repeatable scripts. Managing certificates manually across team members leads to certificate revocations when someone's machine is lost — match stores certificates encrypted in a Git repo so any machine can re-issue them instantly.

children.title: TestFlight Internal & External Testing
children.description: TestFlight distributes pre-release builds to up to 10,000 external testers without going through full App Store review; internal testers (up to 100, team members) can receive builds within minutes. Submitting builds with debug-only features enabled or test accounts hardcoded violates TestFlight guidelines and can result in the build being rejected for external review.

children.title: Build Versioning — CFBundleVersion
children.description: App Store Connect requires a strictly increasing CFBundleVersion (build number) for every uploaded build; CFBundleShortVersionString is the user-visible version. Uploading a build with the same build number as an existing one is rejected immediately — Fastlane's increment_build_number action automates this using the App Store Connect API.

resources.label: Apple Docs — Version and Build Numbers

children.title: App Review Guidelines
children.description: Apple's App Review team evaluates every submission against the App Review Guidelines covering safety, performance, business, design, and legal categories. The most common rejection reasons are missing privacy manifest entries for required-reason APIs, incorrect age ratings, and broken demo accounts — each causes a multi-day delay in the release pipeline.

resources.label: Apple Docs — Prepare for App Review

children.title: Privacy Manifest & Required Reasons
children.description: From Spring 2024 Apple requires a PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file listing every privacy-sensitive API your app or its third-party SDKs use, along with the approved reason for that usage. Submitting without a privacy manifest for APIs like UserDefaults or NSFileManager triggers an automated rejection email — without reading the documentation this is easy to overlook until the first submission.

children.title: Provisioning Profiles & Certificates
children.description: A provisioning profile ties an App ID, signing certificate, and list of device UDIDs together to authorise an app to run. Mixing Development and Distribution profiles, or letting Xcode automatically manage signing for a CI machine, causes ad-hoc builds to install but immediately crash on launch with a code-signing error.

children.title: Entitlements & Capabilities
children.description: Capabilities such as Push Notifications, iCloud, and Sign in with Apple require matching entitlements in the .entitlements file and a corresponding App ID configuration in the Developer Portal. Adding a capability in Xcode but forgetting to enable it in the Developer Portal Portal causes a provisioning mismatch that prevents archiving.

ios.title: Advanced iOS - Extensions, Widgets and Background Tasks
ios.description: Extend your iOS apps beyond the main process using WidgetKit for home and lock-screen surfaces, App Clips for instant experiences, and BGTaskScheduler for deferred background work. Implement push notifications with rich media payloads and notification service extensions.
topics: WidgetKit timeline providers and widget families
Lock Screen widgets and StandBy mode complications
App Clips entitlements, size limits, and invocation URLs
Push notification service extensions for media attachments
Share and action extensions with App Groups data sharing

resources.label: WWDC - Complications and Widgets Reuse with SwiftUI
iOS 17 Programming for Beginners - Advanced Chapters

ios.challenge: Build a WidgetKit widget with three sizes that reads data from an App Group shared by the main app, schedule a BGProcessingTask to refresh that data nightly, and send a push notification with a thumbnail image using a notification service extension.

children.title: Widget Timeline & TimelineProvider
children.description: WidgetKit renders widgets in a separate extension process; the TimelineProvider supplies entries with timestamps that WidgetKit uses to schedule future renders without waking the app. Performing network requests inside getTimeline on the main queue blocks the provider — WidgetKit gives each provider a strict time budget and will fall back to a placeholder entry if the budget is exceeded.

children.title: App Intent & Interactive Widgets (iOS 17)
children.description: iOS 17 interactive widgets use AppIntent to handle button and toggle taps directly in the widget without launching the app. Routing interactive widget actions through a deep link instead of AppIntent opens the app unnecessarily — this violates the spirit of Lock Screen widgets where users expect glanceable actions without app-launch overhead.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Bring widgets to new places

children.title: Share Extension & App Groups
children.description: Share Extensions run in a separate process and cannot directly access the host app's sandbox; App Groups create a shared container both processes can read and write. Trying to use UserDefaults.standard from a Share Extension reads the extension's own defaults, not the app's — without App Groups, shared preferences and cached data are silently inaccessible.

children.title: Notification Service Extension
children.description: The Notification Service Extension intercepts incoming push notifications before display, enabling payload decryption and media attachment downloads. Not completing the content-handler call within the 30-second budget causes iOS to display the original (potentially encrypted or partial) notification — the extension is silently abandoned.

children.title: BGAppRefreshTask & BGProcessingTask
children.description: BackgroundTasks framework schedules two kinds of work: BGAppRefreshTask for short fetch operations and BGProcessingTask for longer maintenance work when the device is charging. Not calling setTaskCompleted(success:) at the end of a background task causes iOS to penalise the app's future scheduling — the system withholds background time for apps that repeatedly exceed their budget.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Advances in App Background Execution

children.title: Background URLSession
children.description: A background URLSession configuration transfers data even when the app is suspended, notifying the app via a delegate callback when complete. Using a foreground URLSession for large file uploads means the transfer is cancelled if the user backgrounds the app — resumable background sessions survive app termination and reconnect automatically.

children.title: APNs & UNUserNotificationCenter
children.description: Apple Push Notification service (APNs) delivers remote notifications; UNUserNotificationCenter manages local and remote notification scheduling and delegate callbacks. Not requesting notification authorisation before scheduling local notifications causes them to be silently dropped — iOS will not deliver notifications without explicit user permission, and the authorisation prompt only appears when requested.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — What's new in User Notifications

children.title: Rich Notifications & Notification Content Extension
children.description: Notification Content Extensions provide custom SwiftUI views that appear when a user long-presses a notification, enabling interactive rich media. Not including the UNNotificationExtensionInitialContentSizeRatio key in the extension's Info.plist causes the notification view to render with an incorrect default height, clipping the custom content.

ios.title: Production iOS - StoreKit 2, Analytics and App Store Optimization
ios.description: Ship and monetize iOS apps professionally by implementing StoreKit 2 for modern in-app purchases and subscriptions, integrating crash reporting with symbolicated stack traces, and running hypothesis-driven A/B tests through Firebase. Master App Store Connect metrics and listing optimization to improve conversion and discoverability.
topics: StoreKit 2 Transaction API and subscription lifecycle management
Server-side receipt verification with App Store Server API
Firebase Crashlytics dSYM upload and crash-free rate targets
Firebase A/B Testing with Remote Config parameters
App Store Connect analytics - impressions, conversion, retention
App Store listing optimization - keywords, screenshots, preview videos
Xcode Cloud workflows for TestFlight and phased App Store releases
ios.challenge: Implement a StoreKit 2 annual subscription with a promotional offer, verify transactions server-side using the App Store Server API, upload dSYMs to Crashlytics via an Xcode Cloud post-action script, and set up a Firebase A/B test comparing two paywall layouts tracking a purchase conversion event.

children.title: Product, Purchase & Transaction
children.description: StoreKit 2 (iOS 15+) provides an async/await API for fetching products, purchasing, and verifying transactions with automatic server-signed JWTs — no receipt validation server required. Not finishing transactions with Transaction.finish() after delivering content leaves them in a pending state; the system re-delivers them on every app launch, potentially granting content multiple times.

children.title: Transaction.currentEntitlements & Subscription Status
children.description: Transaction.currentEntitlements provides an AsyncSequence of all active purchases, replacing the opaque receipt blob with typed, verifiable transaction objects. Checking only the purchase-time transaction without listening to currentEntitlements misses subscription renewals, refunds, and family-sharing grants — users lose access or gain unauthorised access as subscription state changes.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — What's new in StoreKit 2 and StoreKit Testing

children.title: Firebase Analytics — Event Logging
children.description: Firebase Analytics logs custom events and user properties to Google Analytics, enabling funnel analysis and audience segmentation for A/B tests. Logging events from background threads without specifying that Firebase Analytics operations are thread-safe can cause intermittent crashes on older iOS versions — using the main thread or verified thread-safe calls is the safe path.

children.title: Firebase Crashlytics
children.description: Crashlytics symbolises crash reports using uploaded dSYM files, turning raw memory addresses into readable stack traces. Not uploading dSYM files as part of the Xcode Cloud or Fastlane build pipeline means every crash report shows unsymbolicated addresses — the crash is recorded but completely uninvestigable without manual symbolication.

children.title: Metadata — Title, Keywords & Subtitle
children.description: App Store search indexes the title (30 chars), subtitle (30 chars), and keyword field (100 chars) to surface apps in results. Repeating the app name in the keyword field wastes indexing budget — App Store Connect ignores duplicates, so every repeated word is a missed opportunity to rank for an additional search term.

children.title: App Store Screenshots & Preview Videos
children.description: App Store screenshots are the primary conversion driver — users decide to download based on images before reading the description. Submitting default Simulator screenshots without localised callout text or brand colours produces a listing indistinguishable from a prototype, dramatically reducing conversion versus competitors with designed screenshot sets.

children.title: Instruments — Time Profiler & Allocations
children.description: Instruments' Time Profiler samples the call stack to identify CPU hot paths; the Allocations instrument tracks heap growth and persistent objects. Publishing a production app without profiling on a real device risks App Store rejection for Energy or Performance metrics — Apple's automated quality checks flag apps with sustained high CPU or memory on review devices.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Getting Started with Instruments

children.title: MetricKit — Production Diagnostics
children.description: MetricKit delivers on-device performance diagnostics (launch time, hang rate, CPU usage) and crash/hang logs from real user devices without requiring users to file reports. Not adopting MetricKit means relying solely on Crashlytics for production signal — MetricKit's hang reports catch non-crashing freezes that Crashlytics misses entirely.

resources.label: Apple WWDC — Identify trends with the Power and Performance API

android.title: Kotlin Fundamentals
android.description: Kotlin is the official language for Android development. It's concise, null-safe, and has excellent coroutine support. Master it before writing your first Activity.
topics: Variables — val/var, type inference, null safety ?, !!
Functions — default params, extension functions, lambdas
OOP — data classes, sealed classes, objects, companion
Collections — List, Map, Set, higher-order functions
Scope functions — let, run, apply, also, with
android.challenge: Build a Kotlin CLI app that parses a CSV file and generates a statistical summary — mean, median, and mode — using coroutines for file I/O.

children.title: Nullable Types and the ? Operator
children.description: Kotlin encodes nullability in the type system — `String` can never be null, `String?` can. Ignoring this distinction and forcing non-null with `!!` causes NullPointerExceptions that bypass the compiler's protection entirely, defeating the main safety guarantee Kotlin provides.

children.title: Sealed Classes and Exhaustive When
children.description: Sealed classes let you model closed hierarchies like `Result.Success` and `Result.Error`. When used with `when` expressions the compiler enforces exhaustiveness, so adding a new subtype without handling it is a compile error — eliminating silent runtime gaps in state handling.

children.title: Extension Functions
children.description: Extension functions let you add behavior to existing types without subclassing. They are resolved statically at compile time, so they do not participate in virtual dispatch — calling an extension on a subtype via a supertype reference executes the supertype's version, which surprises developers expecting polymorphism.

children.title: Scope Functions: let, apply, run, also, with
children.description: Each scope function differs in context object (`this` vs `it`) and return value (the object vs lambda result). Choosing the wrong one — for example using `apply` when you need the lambda result — introduces subtle bugs where the wrong value is returned silently and used downstream.

children.title: Coroutine Scopes and Structured Concurrency
children.description: Every coroutine must belong to a scope that defines its lifetime. Launching coroutines in `GlobalScope` detaches them from any lifecycle, causing memory leaks and work that continues after a screen is destroyed. Using `viewModelScope` or `lifecycleScope` ensures coroutines are cancelled automatically.

children.title: suspend Functions and Dispatchers
children.description: `suspend` functions can only be called from a coroutine or another suspend function. Performing CPU-intensive or blocking I/O work on `Dispatchers.Main` freezes the UI thread; explicitly switching to `Dispatchers.IO` or `Dispatchers.Default` prevents ANR errors and keeps the app responsive.

children.title: Sequences vs Eager Collections
children.description: Standard collection operations like `map` and `filter` are eager — each step materializes a full intermediate list. For large datasets this wastes memory and CPU. Converting to a `Sequence` makes operations lazy and evaluated only when a terminal operator is called, removing unnecessary allocations.

children.title: Lambdas and Function Types
children.description: Lambdas that capture mutable state from the enclosing scope can introduce concurrency bugs when that state is modified from multiple coroutines. Understanding that a lambda closes over the variable reference — not its value at creation time — is essential for correct coroutine and callback code.

android.title: Jetpack Compose UI
android.description: Jetpack Compose is Android's modern declarative UI toolkit. Learn composable functions, the state-driven UI model, and Material Design 3 components.
topics: Material 3 components — Scaffold, TopAppBar, Card, Button
android.challenge: Build a Compose UI for a to-do app — LazyColumn list, add/delete/complete tasks, a TopAppBar with a filter menu, and a FAB.

children.title: How Recomposition Works
children.description: Compose re-executes composable functions whenever their observed state changes. If a composable reads a value directly from a ViewModel without stability guarantees, every unrelated state change can trigger unnecessary recompositions across large subtrees, causing janky 16ms frame drops.

children.title: State Hoisting
children.description: Moving state up to the nearest common ancestor makes composables stateless, testable, and reusable. A composable that manages its own state internally cannot be driven from a test or a parent composable, and its state is lost on recomposition unless explicitly remembered.

children.title: Modifier Order Matters
children.description: Modifiers are applied in the order they are declared and each one wraps the next — placing `clickable` before `padding` makes the tap target smaller than the visual area, while the reverse extends the clickable region to include the padding. Wrong order causes hit-test and visual inconsistencies.

children.title: LazyColumn and Keys
children.description: LazyColumn recycles composables by position by default. Without stable `key` parameters, insertions and deletions at arbitrary positions cause incorrect animations and state being matched to the wrong item — for example a text field retaining input that belongs to a different list item after a delete.

children.title: Dynamic Color and MaterialTheme
children.description: Material 3 derives its color scheme from `MaterialTheme.colorScheme`. Hard-coding colors with `Color(0xFF…)` bypasses the theme, breaking dark mode and dynamic color on Android 12+. Every color reference should come from the theme to ensure consistency across system-level changes.

children.title: Typography and Shape Systems
children.description: Material 3 provides a complete typography scale (`displayLarge` through `labelSmall`) and a shape system. Using ad-hoc `fontSize` values or `RoundedCornerShape` literals scattered through composables creates visual inconsistency and makes design-system changes require grep-and-replace rather than a single token update.

children.title: LaunchedEffect and Key Stability
children.description: `LaunchedEffect` launches a coroutine that is restarted whenever its key changes. Using an unstable or frequently-changing object as the key causes the effect to restart on every recomposition, leading to network requests firing repeatedly or animations restarting mid-play.

children.title: DisposableEffect for Cleanup
children.description: `DisposableEffect` registers side effects that need cleanup — event listeners, sensors, or system callbacks. Forgetting to return `onDispose { }` with proper cleanup leaks the listener past the composable's lifetime, causing duplicate callbacks or crashes from accessing destroyed objects.

android.title: Architecture — MVVM & Clean
android.description: Android apps need a clear architecture to stay maintainable. Learn MVVM with ViewModels, StateFlow, and dependency injection with Hilt.
topics: Clean Architecture layers — data, domain, presentation
android.challenge: Restructure your to-do app with MVVM + Hilt — a ViewModel with StateFlow, a repository interface, use cases for add/delete/filter, and proper DI.

children.title: ViewModel Surviving Configuration Changes
children.description: ViewModel instances are retained across configuration changes such as rotation because they live in a `ViewModelStore` tied to the Activity lifecycle, not the Activity itself. Storing UI controllers or Context references inside a ViewModel causes memory leaks because the ViewModel outlives those objects.

children.title: StateFlow vs SharedFlow
children.description: `StateFlow` always holds a value and replays the latest to new collectors — ideal for UI state. `SharedFlow` has configurable replay and is suited for one-shot events like navigation commands. Misusing `StateFlow` for events causes events to be re-delivered after recomposition, triggering duplicate navigations or toasts.

children.title: Single Source of Truth
children.description: The repository is the single source of truth — it decides whether data comes from the network, cache, or local database. Bypassing the repository and calling data sources directly from ViewModels means caching, error handling, and offline logic must be duplicated across every ViewModel that needs the same data.

children.title: Use Cases (Interactors)
children.description: Use cases encapsulate a single piece of business logic and depend only on repository interfaces, not concrete implementations. Putting business logic directly in a ViewModel couples it to the Android framework, making it impossible to unit-test without instrumentation and impossible to reuse across different ViewModels.

children.title: Hilt Components and Scoping
children.description: Hilt organizes the dependency graph into components with different lifetimes — `SingletonComponent`, `ActivityComponent`, `ViewModelComponent`. Scoping a dependency to the wrong component creates either too many instances (wasting resources) or a single shared instance that holds stale data between sessions.

children.title: @HiltViewModel and Assisted Injection
children.description: `@HiltViewModel` integrates ViewModels with Hilt's component system, allowing Hilt to provide dependencies automatically. Without it, manually instantiating a ViewModel in a `Factory` is error-prone and bypasses scoping — causing dependencies to outlive their intended scope or be recreated unnecessarily.

children.title: Dependency Rule
children.description: In Clean Architecture the dependency rule states that inner layers (domain) must not depend on outer layers (data, presentation). Importing Android framework classes or Room entities into use cases breaks this rule, making business logic impossible to test without a device and tightly coupling it to infrastructure details.

children.title: UI State as Data Classes
children.description: Representing the entire UI state as a single immutable data class emitted from a `StateFlow` makes state changes atomic and testable. Scattering multiple `LiveData` or `StateFlow` properties across a ViewModel causes race conditions where the UI briefly renders contradictory combinations — loading spinner with data visible simultaneously.

android.title: Local Storage — Room & DataStore
android.description: Persist data on the device with Room (SQLite ORM) for structured data and DataStore for preferences — both with full coroutine and Flow support.
android.challenge: Migrate your to-do app to Room — DAO with insert/update/delete/query, reactive Flow from queries, a DataStore preference for the selected theme.

children.title: DAO and Reactive Queries with Flow
children.description: Room DAOs that return `Flow<T>` emit a new value automatically whenever the underlying table changes. If you return a plain `List<T>` instead, the query runs once and the UI never updates when data changes — forcing manual re-queries and making the app appear stale after insertions or deletions.

resources.label: Room with Kotlin Flow — Android Docs
Save Data in a Local Database with Room

children.title: Database Migrations
children.description: When you change a Room `@Entity` schema, Room requires a migration that describes how to transform the old database to the new one. Using `fallbackToDestructiveMigration()` silently deletes all user data on every schema version bump — acceptable in development, catastrophic in production without a warning.

children.title: @Entity, @PrimaryKey, and Indices
children.description: Every Room entity needs a `@PrimaryKey` and Room translates it directly to a SQLite table. Missing an index on a column used in `WHERE` clauses or `JOIN`s causes Room to perform a full table scan on every query, degrading performance severely as the table grows.

children.title: TypeConverters for Complex Types
children.description: SQLite supports only primitive types, so Room cannot store Lists, enums, or custom objects directly. Without a `@TypeConverter` Room throws a compile-time error. Storing JSON strings as a workaround works but breaks type safety and prevents Room from querying inside those fields.

children.title: DataStore vs SharedPreferences
children.description: DataStore replaces SharedPreferences with a coroutine- and Flow-based API that is safe to call from any dispatcher. SharedPreferences `apply()` writes asynchronously without error reporting, and `commit()` blocks the calling thread — both behaviors that cause data loss or ANRs in production.

children.title: Proto DataStore for Typed Storage
children.description: Proto DataStore uses Protocol Buffers to store strongly-typed objects, catching schema mistakes at compile time. Preferences DataStore uses stringly-typed keys, so a typo in a key name reads `null` instead of the saved value — a silent failure that appears as a missing preference on every app start.

children.title: EncryptedSharedPreferences and Keystore
children.description: Regular SharedPreferences and DataStore store data as plaintext in the app's data directory. On rooted devices or with a physical backup, this data is trivially readable. EncryptedSharedPreferences backed by the Android Keystore hardware key ensures credentials and tokens cannot be extracted without the device's unlock credential.

children.title: Room Database Encryption
children.description: Room databases are SQLite files that can be read directly if the device is compromised. SQLCipher for Android and the Room integration with encrypted databases ensures that even a raw file copy is unreadable without the encryption key, which should itself be stored in the Android Keystore.

android.title: Networking with Retrofit & Coroutines
android.description: Fetch data from the internet with Retrofit, parse JSON with Kotlin Serialization, cache images with Coil, and handle errors and loading states cleanly.
topics: Sealed class Result<T> for API responses
Coil for image loading in Compose
android.challenge: Add a REST API layer to your to-do app — sync tasks with a backend, load user avatars with Coil, and implement offline caching with Room as the single source of truth.

children.title: Retrofit with Suspend Functions
children.description: Retrofit supports `suspend` functions natively — the network call is dispatched on `Dispatchers.IO` automatically and the result is returned on the calling coroutine's dispatcher. Using callbacks or `Call<T>` manually alongside coroutines creates two competing concurrency models that are error-prone to cancel and compose.

children.title: OkHttp Interceptors
children.description: OkHttp interceptors intercept every request and response, making them ideal for adding auth headers, logging, and retry logic in one place. Adding auth headers in every Retrofit call site instead means that any API call that forgets the header silently returns 401, and changing the auth scheme requires touching every call site.

children.title: @Serializable and Custom Serializers
children.description: Kotlin Serialization requires classes to be annotated with `@Serializable` and processes them at compile time — there is no reflection overhead. Forgetting the annotation or using a non-serializable type in a data class causes a runtime `SerializationException` that is hard to trace when the API response itself is valid.

children.title: Sealed Result Type for API Errors
children.description: Wrapping network responses in `sealed class Result<T> { class Success(val data: T); class Error(val exception: Throwable) }` makes error handling explicit at every call site. Without this pattern, exceptions propagate up the call stack and either crash the app or are silently swallowed by a generic catch block that shows no user feedback.

children.title: Coil in Compose with AsyncImage
children.description: Coil's `AsyncImage` composable handles image loading, caching, and placeholder display within Compose's recomposition model. Loading images manually with coroutines and `BitmapFactory` in a composable bypasses Coil's memory and disk caches, causing the same image to be downloaded on every recomposition and triggering OOM errors.

children.title: Image Caching Strategy
children.description: Coil uses a two-level cache: an in-memory LRU cache and a disk cache. Disabling the disk cache means that images are re-downloaded on every cold start, wasting bandwidth and increasing load times. Misconfiguring cache sizes causes the LRU to evict images that are still visible, causing visible flicker.

children.title: Room as Single Source of Truth
children.description: In an offline-first architecture the database is always the source of truth for the UI. The repository fetches from the network, writes to Room, and the UI observes Room via Flow. Rendering network responses directly in the UI bypasses the cache, breaks offline mode, and causes inconsistencies between cached and live data.

children.title: Network Connectivity Monitoring
children.description: Using `ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback` instead of polling lets the app react to connectivity changes instantly without battery-draining background polling. Attempting network requests without checking connectivity first leads to crashes or long timeout waits on devices with no signal.

android.title: Navigation & Deep Links
android.description: Navigate between screens, pass arguments, and handle deep links with Jetpack Navigation for Compose.
topics: Bottom navigation bar with Navigation Compose
Deep links — intent filters, navigation links
android.challenge: Add bottom navigation to your to-do app (Tasks, Stats, Settings tabs), deep link to a specific task from a notification, and animate between screens.

children.title: NavHost and Destination Routes
children.description: Navigation Compose defines destinations as route strings in a `NavHost`. Using hardcoded string routes scattered across composables causes typos that only crash at runtime during navigation. Centralizing route constants or using a typed routes approach makes all navigation paths refactor-safe at compile time.

children.title: Passing Arguments Between Destinations
children.description: Navigation Compose passes arguments as URL-encoded strings in the route. Passing complex objects directly causes crashes because only primitive types and Parcelables are supported by the backstack. The recommended pattern is to pass only an ID and re-load the full object from the ViewModel in the destination.

children.title: Nested Navigation Graphs
children.description: Nested navigation graphs group related destinations into sub-graphs with their own back stack scope. Without nesting, a back press from a detail screen can skip intermediate screens or pop all the way to the app's root rather than returning to the correct parent section, breaking user expectations.

children.title: Back Stack Manipulation with popUpTo
children.description: `popUpTo` removes destinations from the back stack when navigating, which is essential for login flows where pressing Back after login should not return to the login screen. Without it, the user can navigate back to a screen they should no longer access after authentication state changes.

resources.label: Navigate to a Destination — Android Docs

children.title: Intent Filters and URL Deep Links
children.description: Deep links declared in the `AndroidManifest.xml` with intent filters allow external apps and web pages to open a specific destination inside your app. Without proper intent filter configuration or matching routes in the `NavHost`, incoming deep link intents are silently ignored and the user sees the home screen instead of the intended content.

children.title: App Links vs Deep Links
children.description: App Links are verified HTTP/HTTPS deep links that Android routes exclusively to your app without showing a disambiguation dialog. Without Digital Asset Links verification, any app can claim the same URL scheme and Android shows a chooser — allowing malicious apps to intercept links that carry tokens or sensitive state.

children.title: saveState and restoreState for Tab Persistence
children.description: When navigating between bottom navigation tabs, passing `saveState = true` and `restoreState = true` preserves each tab's back stack and scroll position. Without these flags every tab switch restores the tab to its root destination, destroying any progress the user made — a common complaint that degrades perceived app quality.

children.title: Current Destination and Selected State
children.description: Tracking the selected bottom navigation item via `navController.currentBackStackEntryAsState()` ensures the highlighted tab always matches the actual visible destination. Tracking tab selection with a local `var` instead causes the highlight to desync when the user navigates via deep links or the system Back button.

android.title: Testing Android Apps
android.description: Test your ViewModel logic with JUnit, your Compose UI with the Compose testing APIs, and your coroutine flows with Turbine.
topics: JUnit 4/5 — unit testing ViewModels, use cases
Turbine — testing StateFlow and Flow emissions
Compose UI testing — composeTestRule, semantics, actions
android.challenge: Write tests covering your ViewModel state transitions, DAO queries with in-memory Room, and two Compose UI tests for adding and deleting a task.

children.title: TestCoroutineDispatcher and runTest
children.description: `runTest` from `kotlinx-coroutines-test` runs coroutines synchronously in tests, letting you assert on StateFlow values without sleeping or polling. Using real delays or `runBlocking` in ViewModel tests makes the test suite slow and brittle, with timing-dependent failures that pass locally and fail on CI.

children.title: MockK for Kotlin Mocking
children.description: MockK is designed for Kotlin and handles `object`, `companion object`, extension functions, and coroutines natively. Using Mockito for Kotlin mocking requires extra adapters and still fails to mock `final` classes or suspend functions without complex workarounds, making tests fragile and harder to maintain.

children.title: Testing StateFlow with Turbine
children.description: Turbine provides a structured API to collect Flow emissions in tests — `awaitItem()`, `awaitComplete()`, `cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents()`. Collecting a StateFlow in a `launch` coroutine and reading from a list races the test and the collector, causing flaky assertions when emissions arrive out of order.

children.title: Fakes vs Mocks for Repositories
children.description: Using fake repository implementations (classes that implement the interface with in-memory data) makes ViewModel tests deterministic and fast. Heavy mocking of repositories with MockK can produce tests that verify mock setup rather than real behavior, giving false confidence when the real implementation diverges.

children.title: composeTestRule and Semantics
children.description: Compose UI tests interact with the component tree via semantic properties — `onNodeWithText`, `onNodeWithContentDescription`, `onNodeWithTag`. Testing with `onNodeWithTag` requires adding `Modifier.testTag()` to composables; relying on visual text alone breaks tests when strings are localized or dynamically generated.

children.title: Testing State Changes with Actions
children.description: Compose test actions like `performClick()` and `performTextInput()` trigger recomposition synchronously in the test environment. Forgetting to call `awaitIdle()` after an action that triggers a coroutine causes assertions to run before the StateFlow update propagates to the UI, producing false negatives.

children.title: In-Memory Room Database
children.description: Room provides `Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder` for tests — the database exists only for the test's lifetime and is destroyed afterward, with no disk I/O. Using a real on-disk database in tests causes tests to interfere with each other when they share state, and leftover files between runs cause non-deterministic failures.

children.title: Testing DAO Queries
children.description: DAO tests should verify query correctness — that `@Query` annotations return the expected rows for given table state. Without these tests, errors in SQL `WHERE` clauses or missing `ORDER BY` clauses only surface in production when users see incorrect or unsorted data.

android.title: Publishing to Google Play
android.description: Ship your Android app to the world. Configure Gradle for release builds, shrink code with R8/ProGuard, and automate deployment with GitHub Actions and Fastlane.
topics: Gradle — build variants, product flavors, signing config
Google Play Console — internal, alpha, beta, production tracks
Fastlane supply — automated Play Store deployment
GitHub Actions — build signed AAB, upload to Play
android.challenge: Set up a GitHub Actions workflow that builds a signed release AAB, runs your test suite, and automatically uploads to the Play Store internal track on main branch merge.

children.title: Build Variants and Product Flavors
children.description: Build variants combine build types (debug/release) with product flavors (free/paid, staging/production) to generate multiple APKs from a single codebase. Without variants, developers manually change API endpoints and feature flags before release, a process that is error-prone and has shipped production apps pointing at staging servers.

children.title: App Signing Configuration
children.description: Release builds must be signed with a keystore to be accepted by Google Play. Committing the keystore or its passwords to source control exposes the signing credentials permanently — anyone with repository access can publish updates under your identity. Use `local.properties` or CI secret environment variables instead.

resources.label: Secure Build Secrets with GitHub Actions

children.title: R8 Minification and Obfuscation
children.description: R8 shrinks, optimizes, and obfuscates the release build, reducing APK size and making reverse engineering harder. Without keeping rules for reflection-based libraries (Retrofit, Gson, Room), R8 renames classes that are referenced only by string at runtime, causing `ClassNotFoundException` crashes that only appear in release builds.

resources.label: Shrink, Obfuscate, and Optimize — Android Docs

children.title: App Bundle vs APK
children.description: Android App Bundles (AAB) let Google Play deliver optimized APKs per device configuration — only the resources and native code needed for that device are included. Uploading a universal APK includes all densities and ABIs, inflating download size by 30–50% for users who receive unnecessary assets.

children.title: Release Tracks — Internal, Alpha, Beta, Production
children.description: Play Console's release tracks allow staged rollouts to progressively larger audiences before full production release. Releasing directly to production without internal testing skips the safety net that catches crashes and permission issues on real devices that your emulator and CI never reproduce.

resources.label: Manage App Releases — Play Console Help

children.title: Phased Rollouts and Halt
children.description: Phased rollouts release an update to a percentage of users, letting you monitor crash rates before expanding. Without phased rollouts, a critical bug introduced in a release immediately affects 100% of your user base — the rollout cannot be paused, only halted (which requires uploading a fix before resuming).

resources.label: Set Up a Phased Rollout — Play Console

children.title: GitHub Actions for Android Builds
