children.description: GitHub Actions can build, test, and upload a signed AAB to the Play internal track on every merge to main. Without automation, the release process is manual and skips tests under time pressure — the human factor that most release-day incidents are traced back to.

children.title: Fastlane for Automated Deployment
children.description: Fastlane automates screenshot capture, metadata updates, and Play Store uploads through its `supply` action. Managing Play Store listings manually is slow and error-prone — localized descriptions become stale, screenshot sets become inconsistent, and the human doing the release becomes a deployment bottleneck.

resources.label: Android CI/CD with Fastlane and GitHub Actions

android.title: Advanced Android - Background Processing and System Integration
android.description: Build rich system integrations using WorkManager for guaranteed background tasks, advanced notification channels, and app widgets powered by the Glance API. Explore Wear OS fundamentals and understand how Android manages process priority and battery restrictions.
topics: WorkManager chains, constraints, and expedited tasks
Notification channels, rich notifications, and bubbles
Foreground services and battery optimization compliance
App widgets with Glance and RemoteViews
Wear OS tile and complication development basics
Doze mode, App Standby buckets, and JobScheduler interaction
android.challenge: Implement a WorkManager periodic task that syncs data in the background respecting network and charging constraints, surfaces progress via a foreground service notification, and updates a Glance widget with the last sync timestamp.

children.title: WorkManager Guarantees and Constraints
children.description: WorkManager guarantees task execution even if the app is killed or the device restarts, using JobScheduler and AlarmManager under the hood depending on API level. Using plain coroutines or foreground services for deferrable work means the work is lost on process death — the user's sync or upload silently never completes.

children.title: Chaining Work Requests
children.description: WorkManager allows chaining `WorkRequest`s in a sequence or parallel DAG, where each step only starts when its dependencies succeed. Without chaining, developers implement sequential background tasks with callbacks or `Thread.sleep`, creating fragile coordination that breaks when any step fails or is retried by the system.

children.title: Notification Channels (API 26+)
children.description: Since Android 8.0, every notification must be assigned to a channel that the user can control individually. Without channels, all notifications are silently blocked on API 26+ devices, causing critical alerts — background sync failures, incoming messages — to disappear with no user-visible indicator.

children.title: Foreground Service Requirements
children.description: Foreground services must display a persistent notification and declare `foregroundServiceType` in the manifest since Android 14. Launching a foreground service without the correct type or notification causes a `ForegroundServiceStartNotAllowedException` crash on newer OS versions, breaking background upload and location tracking features.

children.title: Glance Composable API
children.description: Glance provides a Compose-like API for building app widgets backed by `RemoteViews`. The widget's composable runs in a separate process, so it cannot directly access ViewModel state — data must be passed through `GlanceStateDefinition` or WorkManager output data, not through shared memory or singleton objects.

children.title: Updating Widget State
children.description: Glance widgets are updated by calling `GlanceAppWidget.update()` or scheduling updates through `AppWidgetManager`. Relying on the system's periodic update interval (as short as 30 minutes) for time-sensitive data means users see stale information. WorkManager should trigger widget updates immediately after data changes.

children.title: Doze Mode and App Standby
children.description: When a device is idle, Android restricts network access, waits for maintenance windows before running jobs, and defers alarms. Code that assumes network is always available or that coroutines will complete within seconds fails silently during Doze — syncs never happen, notifications are delayed indefinitely.

resources.label: Optimize for Doze and App Standby

children.title: BroadcastReceiver and System Events
children.description: Static `BroadcastReceiver` declarations in the manifest for implicit broadcasts are blocked since Android 8.0 — only a small set of protected broadcasts still works statically. Developers who rely on `CONNECTIVITY_CHANGE` as a manifest receiver will never receive it, causing reconnection logic to silently stop working after the user upgrades.

android.title: Production Android - Store Optimization, Billing and Feature Flags
android.description: Maximize app success on the Play Store by integrating Play Billing Library for subscriptions, using Firebase to run controlled experiments, and applying crash analytics to triage regressions before they reach a wide audience. Learn listing optimization tactics and phased rollout strategies.
topics: Play Billing Library 6 - subscriptions and one-time products
Purchase verification and server-side receipt validation
Firebase Crashlytics ANR and crash triage workflow
Remote Config feature flags and parameter hygiene
A/B testing with Firebase A/B Testing and Grow experiments
Play Store listing optimization - screenshots, short description, keywords
Phased rollouts and halt-and-resume release management
android.challenge: Add a monthly subscription product using Play Billing Library with server-side purchase token verification, gate the premium feature behind a Firebase Remote Config flag, run a 50/50 A/B test on the paywall copy, and monitor the conversion funnel in Firebase Analytics.

children.title: Subscriptions and One-Time Products
children.description: Play Billing Library 6 introduced a unified API for both subscriptions and one-time purchases via `ProductDetails`. Mixing the legacy `SkuDetails` API with the new API in the same app causes `BillingClient` state inconsistencies and `ITEM_UNAVAILABLE` errors that are impossible to reproduce in the sandbox, only manifesting in production.

children.title: Server-Side Purchase Verification
children.description: After a purchase, the client receives a purchase token that must be verified server-side using the Google Play Developer API before granting entitlement. Trusting only the client-side `PurchaseState.PURCHASED` check is trivially bypassable — any user can fake a successful purchase response and receive premium features for free.

children.title: Crash Triage and ANR Detection
children.description: Firebase Crashlytics captures fatal crashes and ANRs in production, grouping them by stack trace and providing device and OS version breakdowns. Without Crashlytics, crashes are only discovered through Play Console's aggregated reports — which have a 24-hour lag and lack the custom keys and logs needed to reproduce issues efficiently.

children.title: Custom Keys and Non-Fatal Logging
children.description: Crashlytics lets you attach custom key-value pairs and log non-fatal exceptions that provide context around a crash. Without custom keys, a NullPointerException stack trace tells you where the crash happened but not which user action, which API response, or which feature flag configuration led there — making it unreproducible.

sideRight.title: Firebase Remote Config and Feature Flags

children.title: Remote Config Fetch and Activate
children.description: Remote Config values are only available after both `fetch()` and `activate()` have been called — `fetch` downloads values to cache, `activate` makes them visible to `getValue()`. Calling only `fetch` means the app always uses default values, and feature flags appear to have no effect — a common mistake that wastes entire flag rollout cycles.

children.title: A/B Testing with Firebase Experiments
children.description: Firebase A/B Testing runs controlled experiments by assigning users to variants and measuring engagement or revenue metrics. Without statistical significance gates, teams end the experiment early when it shows positive results — a form of p-hacking that produces false conclusions and ships features that hurt retention at scale.

children.title: Store Listing ASO — Screenshots and Description
children.description: App Store Optimization (ASO) directly determines install conversion rate. The first two lines of the short description are the only text visible before 'More' — burying the core value proposition below the fold loses a measurable percentage of potential installs. A/B testing listing assets via Play Console Experiments quantifies the impact.

children.title: Ratings, Reviews, and Reply Strategy
children.description: Responding to negative reviews within 24 hours and resolving the underlying issue measurably improves rating over time — Google surfaces updated ratings to users who left a review after an app is updated. Ignoring reviews means regressions stay visible in the store long after a fix is shipped.

resources.label: Reply to Reviews — Play Console Help

kotlin-dev.title: Kotlin Fundamentals
kotlin-dev.description: Start with Kotlin's expressive, concise syntax on the JVM. Learn the basics that make Kotlin a joy to write.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Build a CLI expense tracker that reads input and prints categorized totals.

children.title: Type Inference & val vs var
children.description: Kotlin's compiler infers types at declaration, but confusing val (immutable reference) with var (mutable reference) leads to accidental shared state mutation. Val does not mean the object itself is immutable — a val list can still be mutated — so misunderstanding this produces race conditions and debugging sessions that are hard to trace back to the root.

children.title: Extension Functions
children.description: Extension functions let you add behaviour to existing classes without subclassing or decorators. They are resolved statically at compile time, not dynamically, so an extension on a base type will not dispatch polymorphically to a subtype — a subtle difference from virtual methods that trips up developers coming from Java or Python.

children.title: JVM Bytecode & Interop
children.description: Kotlin compiles to JVM bytecode and interoperates seamlessly with Java, but the mapping is not always 1:1. Companion object members compile to static nested class members, not Java static fields, so Java code calling a Kotlin companion must use the Companion accessor unless you annotate with @JvmStatic. Getting this wrong silently breaks Java-facing APIs.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Calling Kotlin from Java

children.title: IntelliJ IDEA Project Structure
children.description: IntelliJ's module and source-set model maps directly to Gradle source sets, and misconfiguring them causes correct code to show red errors or tests to be excluded from runs. Understanding how the IDE reads Gradle metadata prevents phantom errors and lets you reliably configure multi-module projects from day one.

resources.label: JetBrains Blog — Getting Started with Kotlin

children.title: String Templates & Multiline Strings
children.description: Kotlin string templates evaluate expressions lazily inline, and triple-quoted raw strings preserve every whitespace character including leading indentation. Forgetting to call trimIndent() on a multiline string used in JSON or YAML construction produces payload errors that are invisible in the IDE because the string renders correctly in previews.

children.title: When Expressions vs Statements
children.description: The when construct in Kotlin is an expression, meaning it returns a value and the compiler enforces exhaustiveness when assigned to a variable. Treating it as a mere switch statement and omitting the else branch compiles in statement form but silently does nothing for unmatched cases, masking logic gaps that only surface at runtime.

children.title: Kotlin REPL & Scratch Files
children.description: IntelliJ's Kotlin REPL and scratch file runner let you evaluate expressions against your project's classpath without compiling the whole module. Skipping this tool means you waste build cycles testing one-off hypotheses; learning it accelerates exploration and helps you verify extension functions or DSL ideas before wiring them into production code.

children.title: Kotlin Compiler Flags & Language Version
children.description: Kotlin language version and API version are independent settings; mismatching them causes stdlib symbols to be unavailable at the API level you target. When working in multi-module projects or publishing libraries, a missing -api-version constraint lets internal experimental APIs leak into public contracts and silently break downstream consumers on version upgrades.

kotlin-dev.title: OOP & Null Safety
kotlin-dev.description: Model your domain with Kotlin's classes and embrace null safety — the feature that kills NullPointerExceptions.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Model a domain (orders, items, customers) with data and sealed classes and zero nullable misuse.

children.title: Sealed Classes & Class Hierarchies
children.description: Sealed classes restrict subclassing to the same compilation unit, giving the compiler a closed set to check against in when expressions. If you model domain states with open classes instead, the compiler cannot warn you about unhandled states, and new states added later silently fall through to default branches, producing subtle logic errors that only manifest in edge cases.

children.title: Object Declarations & Companion Objects
children.description: Object declarations in Kotlin are thread-safe singletons initialized on first access via class-loading guarantees from the JVM. Misusing companion objects as mutable global state stores creates hidden coupling between modules and makes unit testing impossible without dependency injection, because the companion is bound to the class and cannot be replaced in tests.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Object Expressions and Declarations

children.title: Platform Types & Java Interop Nullability
children.description: When Kotlin calls Java code, the returned type is a platform type (e.g. String!) with unknown nullability, and the compiler allows you to assign it to a non-null Kotlin type without warning. This means a null from a Java library silently becomes a NullPointerException at the Kotlin call site, undermining null safety unless you explicitly annotate Java APIs or use defensive let blocks.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Java Interop — Null Safety

children.title: Safe Calls, Elvis & Non-null Assertion
children.description: The !! operator asserts non-null and throws KotlinNullPointerException if wrong, while ?. safe-calls short-circuit to null. Overusing !! to silence the compiler defeats null safety entirely and produces crashes in production; the correct pattern is to handle nullability at the boundary with Elvis ?: defaults or smart casts after explicit null checks.

children.title: Structural Equality & copy()
children.description: Data classes auto-generate equals() and hashCode() based on primary constructor properties, making two distinct instances with the same values equal. Properties declared in the class body (not the constructor) are excluded from these generated methods, which means you can have two objects that compare as equal but behave differently — a trap when using data classes as map keys or in sets.

children.title: Destructuring Declarations
children.description: Destructuring maps positionally to componentN() functions generated for data classes, not by property name. Reordering constructor parameters silently reassigns destructured variables to the wrong values without any compiler warning, making this one of the most dangerous refactors in data class-heavy code.

children.title: open, abstract, and final by Default
children.description: Kotlin classes are final by default; you must explicitly mark them open to allow subclassing. This is intentional to prevent fragile base class problems, but it requires deliberate design decisions upfront. Forgetting to mark a class open and then mocking it with a framework that relies on subclassing (like Mockito without the kotlin-mock plugin) causes test failures that look unrelated to the class design.

children.title: Delegation Pattern with by
children.description: Kotlin's by keyword implements interface delegation at the compiler level, forwarding method calls to a delegate without boilerplate. Mutable state on the delegate is shared, not copied, so if two delegating classes share the same delegate instance, mutations through one are visible through the other — a subtle aliasing bug that defeats the isolation you expect from delegation.

kotlin-dev.title: Functional Kotlin & Collections
kotlin-dev.description: Write idiomatic, functional Kotlin. Master higher-order functions and the rich collections API.
topics: Scope functions — let, run, apply, also, with
kotlin-dev.challenge: Process a dataset with pure collection pipelines and write three useful extension functions.

children.title: Higher-Order Functions & Inline
children.description: Higher-order functions accept or return lambdas, but each lambda creates an anonymous class instance on the JVM, adding heap pressure in hot paths. The inline modifier copies the lambda body to the call site at compile time, eliminating the object allocation, but inlining large functions increases bytecode size. Understanding this trade-off prevents performance regressions in utility functions called inside loops.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Higher-Order Functions and Lambdas

children.title: Function Types & SAM Conversions
children.description: Kotlin function types (A) -> B are first-class, but SAM (Single Abstract Method) conversions from Java functional interfaces work differently. A Kotlin lambda passed to a Java SAM interface is converted automatically, but a Kotlin interface with one method does not get SAM conversion unless declared with fun interface. Mixing these without understanding the rule produces type-mismatch errors at lambda pass sites.

children.title: Immutable vs Mutable Collections
children.description: Kotlin's listOf() returns a read-only view, not a truly immutable object; the underlying implementation may still be a mutable Java ArrayList. Casting a read-only list to MutableList is technically possible and succeeds at runtime, silently breaking immutability guarantees and producing concurrent modification exceptions in multi-threaded code.

children.title: Collection Transformations: map, filter, flatMap
children.description: Each chained collection transformation on a List creates an intermediate list in memory. For large datasets, chaining five transforms allocates five full intermediate lists before producing the result. This is harmless for small data but becomes a memory bottleneck in batch processing, and the fix (switching to Sequences) is a one-word change that many developers delay because the eager version appears to work fine.

children.title: Sequences vs Eager Collections
children.description: Sequences process elements one at a time through the entire chain before moving to the next element, avoiding intermediate allocations. The trade-off is that terminal operations (toList, first, count) trigger full evaluation, and stateful intermediate operations like sorted() require buffering all elements anyway. Blindly replacing all collection chains with sequences can actually worsen performance for small in-memory lists due to iterator overhead.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Sequence Processing vs Iterable

children.title: generateSequence & sequence Builder
children.description: The sequence { } builder uses coroutine mechanics under the hood to yield values lazily, allowing you to model infinite or computationally expensive streams. Forgetting to add a termination condition in generateSequence produces an infinite sequence that silently consumes CPU when iterated without a take() or first() terminal — a bug that is impossible to spot from the sequence definition alone.

children.title: let, run, with, apply, also
children.description: Kotlin's five scope functions differ in two axes: how they refer to the context object (this vs it) and what they return (the object vs the lambda result). Choosing the wrong one produces subtle bugs; using also where apply is needed returns the result of the last expression instead of the object, silently discarding a builder chain and returning Unit to the caller.

children.title: Operator Overloading & DSL Building
children.description: Kotlin allows overloading operators via named conventions (plus, get, invoke, etc.), enabling expressive DSLs. The danger is that overloaded operators look identical to built-in ones, so a custom plus on a domain object that does not behave additively produces logic errors that are invisible during code review because the syntax looks standard.

kotlin-dev.title: Coroutines & Concurrency
kotlin-dev.description: Concurrency the Kotlin way. Write asynchronous, non-blocking code with coroutines and structured concurrency.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Build a concurrent feed aggregator that fetches multiple sources in parallel with structured concurrency.

children.title: Structured Concurrency & CoroutineScope
children.description: Structured concurrency guarantees that a coroutine cannot outlive the scope that launched it. Without it, fire-and-forget GlobalScope launches produce orphaned coroutines that keep running after the owning component is destroyed, leaking resources and causing state mutations on destroyed UI or closed network connections. Every production coroutine should be tied to a lifecycle-aware scope.

children.title: suspend Functions & Continuation Passing
children.description: A suspend function does not create a thread; it compiles to a state machine that saves its execution point into a Continuation object and resumes from there when the awaited work completes. Calling a CPU-bound suspend function without switching dispatchers blocks whichever thread the coroutine resumes on, potentially freezing the main thread even though the code looks asynchronous.

children.title: Dispatchers.IO vs Default vs Main
children.description: Dispatchers.IO uses a thread pool sized for blocking I/O (up to 64 threads by default); Dispatchers.Default uses a CPU-bounded pool sized to core count; Dispatchers.Main runs on the UI thread. Running a database query on Default starves CPU work by blocking compute threads; running image decoding on IO still blocks UI if you collect the result on Main without switching. Getting dispatcher choice wrong causes UI jank or throughput collapse under load.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Coroutine Context and Dispatchers

children.title: withContext & Dispatcher Switching
children.description: withContext switches the dispatcher for a block and resumes on the outer dispatcher when the block completes, without spawning a new coroutine. Misusing launch(Dispatchers.IO) { } to switch context instead creates a child coroutine that cannot propagate exceptions back to the parent cleanly, breaking structured concurrency and silently swallowing errors unless you add an explicit exception handler.

children.title: SupervisorScope & Exception Propagation
children.description: In a regular coroutineScope, a failure in any child cancels all siblings and the parent. SupervisorScope changes this so each child fails independently. Using the wrong scope model means a single failing network request cancels an entire page of parallel requests, while using SupervisorScope everywhere hides crashes that should abort the entire operation — the choice must match the failure semantics of the domain.

children.title: CancellationException & Cooperative Cancellation
children.description: Coroutine cancellation is cooperative: a coroutine must periodically check isActive or call a suspend point to observe cancellation. Swallowing CancellationException in a try-catch block prevents the cancellation signal from propagating, causing the coroutine to run indefinitely even after its scope is cancelled. CancellationException must always be rethrown or left uncaught.

children.title: async/await & Deferred
children.description: async returns a Deferred that starts immediately (by default) and must be awaited to retrieve its value and propagate exceptions. Forgetting to call await() on a Deferred means exceptions inside the async block are silently ignored, and the concurrent work may complete after the parent scope finishes, invalidating results. Always await every async in the same scope that launched it.

children.title: coroutineScope Builder for Parallel Work
children.description: coroutineScope creates a new scope that completes only when all child coroutines finish, making it the safe way to fan out work and collect results in one place. Using a plain GlobalScope.async for parallel work breaks structured concurrency: if one branch throws, the other keeps running and the exception is not visible to the caller until it calls await on the failed Deferred.

kotlin-dev.title: Flow & Async Streams
kotlin-dev.description: Handle streams of values over time with Kotlin Flow — the reactive backbone of modern Kotlin apps.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Model a live ticker as a Flow with operators and expose current state through a StateFlow.

children.title: Cold vs Hot Streams
children.description: Flow is cold: the producer block does not execute until a collector subscribes, and each collector gets its own independent execution. SharedFlow and StateFlow are hot: they emit regardless of collectors and share a single upstream execution. Treating a cold Flow like a hot stream (e.g. expecting two collectors to receive the same emissions from a network call) causes each collector to independently repeat the network request, doubling backend load.

children.title: Flow Operators: map, filter, transform
children.description: Flow operators are intermediate and lazy; they do not run until a terminal operator (collect, first, toList) is called. Chaining operators and storing the result in a variable does not start work — the chain is just a description. This trips up developers who expect map { } to execute eagerly the way it does on a List and then wonder why their side effects never ran.

children.title: StateFlow as State Holder
children.description: StateFlow holds a single current value and replays it to new collectors, making it the idiomatic replacement for LiveData in Kotlin-first architectures. It uses structural equality to suppress identical consecutive emissions, so updating a StateFlow with a new object that equals the current value does nothing. Mutating a data object in-place instead of creating a copy with copy() means updates are silently dropped.

children.title: SharedFlow & Replay Cache
children.description: SharedFlow can be configured with a replay cache so late subscribers receive recent emissions. Setting replay=0 means a new collector misses all emissions that happened before it subscribed — critical for event buses where you want events delivered exactly once. Setting replay too high means observers receive stale events on resubscription and handle the same event multiple times.

children.title: flowOn & Context Preservation
children.description: flowOn changes the upstream context (the producer), not the downstream context (the collector). Misreading this leads developers to place flowOn after collection operators expecting it to affect where collection runs, when in fact it only shifts where emissions are produced. Correct placement of flowOn is critical for keeping emission off the main thread while allowing collection on Main for UI updates.

children.title: buffer, conflate & collectLatest
children.description: By default, a Flow producer suspends when the collector is slow, providing natural backpressure. buffer() decouples emission and collection into concurrent coroutines; conflate() drops intermediate values when the collector is busy; collectLatest cancels the previous collection block when a new value arrives. Choosing the wrong strategy either wastes memory buffering stale values or processes outdated data and discards results.

children.title: Channel Types & Capacity
children.description: Channels are hot, bidirectional communication primitives with configurable buffer capacity. An UNLIMITED channel never suspends the sender but buffers unboundedly, producing OutOfMemoryError under sustained load. A RENDEZVOUS channel (capacity 0) suspends the sender until the receiver is ready, creating tight coupling. Choosing the wrong capacity for inter-coroutine pipelines causes either memory exhaustion or deadlocks when both sides wait for each other.

children.title: Closing Channels & Fan-Out
children.description: A channel must be explicitly closed after all values are sent; otherwise, consumers waiting on receive() suspend forever, hanging the entire pipeline. In fan-out patterns where multiple coroutines consume from one channel, each element is delivered to exactly one consumer — this is not broadcast. Misunderstanding this causes partial processing where only some consumers receive data and others starve.

kotlin-dev.title: Build Tooling with Gradle
kotlin-dev.description: Every real Kotlin project is built with Gradle. Learn the build lifecycle, the Kotlin DSL and dependency management.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Convert your project to a multi-module Gradle build using the Kotlin DSL and a version catalog.

children.title: Build Lifecycle: Initialization, Configuration, Execution
children.description: Gradle runs in three phases: initialization (finds projects), configuration (evaluates all build scripts and constructs the task graph), and execution (runs selected tasks). Code in the configuration phase runs on every build even if no task needs it. Placing expensive work like network calls or file I/O at configuration time instead of inside task actions makes every gradle sync slow and breaks remote build caching.

children.title: Incremental Builds & Build Cache
children.description: Gradle skips tasks whose inputs and outputs have not changed since the last run, giving UP-TO-DATE status. A task that writes to a non-declared output file is invisible to the up-to-date check and will always re-run, defeating incremental builds. Declaring all task inputs and outputs via @Input/@OutputFile annotations is mandatory for build cache correctness in CI environments.

children.title: build.gradle.kts vs Groovy DSL
children.description: The Kotlin DSL provides type-safe build scripts with IDE autocompletion, but it is stricter than Groovy: extension properties must exist at configuration time and dynamic properties are not allowed. Porting a Groovy build script naively to Kotlin DSL often fails because Groovy's dynamic dispatch silently resolves missing properties at runtime while Kotlin fails at compile time, surfacing previously hidden configuration errors.

children.title: Version Catalogs & Dependency Management
children.description: Gradle Version Catalogs centralize dependency coordinates and versions in libs.versions.toml, enabling consistent versions across all modules and IDE-assisted refactoring. Without them, the same library appears with different versions in different modules because each module's build.gradle.kts hardcodes strings, and version conflicts are resolved silently by Gradle's conflict resolution rules in ways that surprise developers.

children.title: implementation vs api vs compileOnly
children.description: implementation hides a dependency from consumers of your module; api exposes it as part of your public API; compileOnly provides it at compile time but not at runtime. Using api everywhere leaks transitive dependencies to all consumers, bloating their classpaths and causing version conflicts. Using implementation for a type that appears in your public method signatures causes compile errors in consumer modules.

resources.label: Gradle Docs — Java Library Plugin Configurations

children.title: Multi-Module Project Structure
children.description: Splitting a Kotlin project into modules enables parallel compilation, finer-grained build caching, and enforced API boundaries. The common mistake is creating too many fine-grained modules early — each module boundary adds configuration overhead and inter-module dependency declarations. The right split is driven by independent changeability and deployment boundaries, not arbitrary code organization.

children.title: Configuration Cache
children.description: Gradle's configuration cache serializes the task graph after the configuration phase so subsequent builds skip configuration entirely. Build scripts that access external state (environment variables, file reads) directly at configuration time without declaring them as inputs are incompatible with the configuration cache and cause cache misses or incorrect builds. Adopting the configuration cache requires auditing every build script for undeclared inputs.

resources.label: Gradle Blog — Configuration Cache Deep Dive

children.title: Gradle Daemon & Memory Settings
children.description: The Gradle daemon stays alive between builds to reuse JVM startup and class-loading time. A daemon with too little heap (-Xmx1g for a large multi-module project) triggers frequent GC pauses that make incremental builds slower than a cold build. Setting org.gradle.jvmargs in gradle.properties to a value appropriate for your module count is a mandatory tuning step often omitted in generated project templates.

kotlin-dev.title: Backend with Ktor
kotlin-dev.description: Build asynchronous backend services with Ktor, JetBrains' lightweight Kotlin-first web framework.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Build a JSON REST API with Ktor for a tasks service, with routing and serialization.

children.title: Plugin System & Feature Installation
children.description: Ktor uses an install() DSL to compose features (plugins) such as ContentNegotiation, Authentication, and Sessions into the application pipeline. Plugins intercept the request pipeline at specific phases, and their order of installation determines execution order. Installing authentication after routing means route-level guards never run, silently exposing endpoints that appear protected in code.

children.title: Application Module Pattern
children.description: Ktor applications are composed of modules, which are extension functions on Application. Each module groups related plugin installations and routes. Putting all configuration in a single module makes testing harder because you cannot install only the subset of plugins needed for a unit test. Splitting modules by concern lets you load only authentication or only routing in isolation for focused tests.

children.title: Route Nesting & Path Parameters
children.description: Ktor routes are nested DSL blocks that build a radix tree; a request is matched by walking the tree until a handler is found. Path parameters are extracted with call.parameters[.name.] and return null if the parameter is absent, but a missing parameter in a required path segment causes a 404 rather than a 400, misleading clients about the error type unless you add explicit validation at the handler level.

children.title: Type-Safe Routing with Resources
children.description: Ktor's Resources plugin adds compile-time checked route definitions using annotated data classes. Without it, route strings are duplicated between the server route definition and client link generation, and a typo in either creates a 404 that is only discoverable at runtime. Type-safe resources make route refactoring a compiler-verified operation and eliminate an entire class of URL mismatch bugs.

children.title: kotlinx.serialization & Content Negotiation
children.description: Ktor's ContentNegotiation plugin uses kotlinx.serialization to encode and decode request and response bodies based on the Content-Type header. If a class is not annotated with @Serializable, kotlinx.serialization throws a SerializationException at runtime, not compile time, because the serializer lookup is reflective by default. Using the KSP plugin with strict mode surfaces missing annotations at build time.

resources.label: Ktor Docs — Content Negotiation and Serialization

children.title: Polymorphic Serialization
children.description: Serializing sealed class hierarchies with kotlinx.serialization requires registering a SerializersModule that maps type discriminators to subclasses. Without this, deserializing a JSON payload with a type field throws a MissingFieldException or silently produces the wrong subtype. This is a common gap when designing API response envelopes that wrap different result types under a common sealed interface.

children.title: JWT Authentication in Ktor
children.description: Ktor's JWT plugin validates tokens on every request by verifying the signature and expiry using the configured algorithm and secret. A misconfigured algorithm (signing with HS256 but verifying with RS256) silently rejects all tokens with a 401 without any log message explaining why. Always log the specific JWT verification failure reason during development and ensure algorithm configuration is environment-specific.

children.title: CORS & Security Headers
children.description: Ktor's CORS plugin must be installed before any routing plugins; otherwise preflight OPTIONS requests return 404 before reaching the CORS handler. Configuring anyHost() in production allows cross-origin requests from any domain, undermining the entire purpose of CORS. Production CORS configuration must enumerate allowed origins explicitly, and this is a common oversight in scaffolded Ktor projects.

kotlin-dev.title: Data & Persistence
kotlin-dev.description: Persist data from your Ktor backend using Exposed, JetBrains' Kotlin SQL framework, against PostgreSQL.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Back your tasks API with PostgreSQL via Exposed, with a connection pool and transactional writes.

children.title: DSL vs DAO APIs
children.description: Exposed provides two query APIs: the DSL API (table objects, query builder) and the DAO API (Entity subclasses with Active Record pattern). They can be mixed in one project but operate on the same transaction. Using DAO entities outside a transaction throws an IllegalStateException, while the DSL API allows building query descriptions outside transactions and only fails at execution — a subtle difference that causes confusing errors when entity access is deferred.

children.title: Transactions & Connection Pooling
children.description: All Exposed database operations must run inside a transaction { } block that acquires a connection from the pool, executes the work, and releases it. Nesting transaction calls creates savepoints by default, not new connections, so a rollback inside a nested transaction rolls back only to the savepoint. Misunderstanding this causes outer transactions to commit partial work when an inner exception is caught and swallowed.

children.title: Table Objects & Column Types
children.description: Exposed Table objects define schema in code and map columns to Kotlin types. The varchar(n) column type enforces length only at the Exposed layer; the database enforces it independently. Defining a column as text() in Exposed but varchar(255) in the actual schema causes silent truncation on insert without any exception at the Kotlin level, corrupting data that appears to save successfully.

children.title: SchemaUtils & Migration Strategy
children.description: SchemaUtils.create() creates missing tables but never alters existing ones, making it safe to run on startup. SchemaUtils.createMissingTablesAndColumns() adds missing columns but cannot change column types or remove columns. Using these for schema evolution in production is insufficient; production schema changes require a dedicated migration tool like Flyway or Liquibase with versioned SQL scripts that Exposed does not provide.

children.title: N+1 Query Problem in Exposed DAO
children.description: The Exposed DAO API loads relationships lazily: accessing entity.children inside a loop fires one SQL query per parent entity. With 100 parent rows, this produces 101 queries instead of 2, causing severe performance degradation that is invisible in development with small datasets. The fix is to use eager loading with load() or switch to the DSL API with explicit JOINs, but the problem only becomes visible under production load.

children.title: PostgreSQL-Specific Features with Exposed
children.description: Exposed supports database-specific extensions via the exposed-json and exposed-kotlin-datetime modules, but PostgreSQL-specific types like JSONB, arrays, and UUIDs require explicit column definitions using customColumn(). Using a generic text() column for JSON data loses PostgreSQL's indexing and query capabilities for JSON fields, producing full table scans on JSON attribute queries that should use GIN indexes.

children.title: Exposed with Coroutine Dispatchers
children.description: Exposed transactions block the calling thread because JDBC is inherently synchronous. Running Exposed queries on Dispatchers.Default blocks CPU threads with I/O work, reducing the throughput available for actual computation. All database calls must be wrapped in withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { transaction { } } to move blocking JDBC work off the compute pool and onto the I/O thread pool.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Blocking Calls in Coroutines

children.title: HikariCP Connection Pool Configuration
children.description: HikariCP is the standard connection pool for JVM applications with Exposed. The maximumPoolSize must be tuned to match the database's max_connections divided by the number of application instances. Setting it too high exhausts the database connection limit under load; setting it too low causes connection timeouts when request concurrency exceeds the pool size. The connectionTimeout setting determines how long a coroutine waits before throwing an exception.

kotlin-dev.title: Testing in Kotlin
kotlin-dev.description: Test Kotlin code idiomatically. Use JUnit5, MockK and Kotest to cover units, coroutines and endpoints.
kotlin-dev.challenge: Write unit tests with MockK and Kotest plus Ktor endpoint tests for your tasks API.

children.title: JUnit5 Extension Model
children.description: JUnit5 replaces JUnit4 Rules with Extensions registered via @ExtendWith. Extensions can intercept test lifecycle events (beforeEach, afterEach, beforeAll) and inject parameters. Mixing JUnit4 @Rule annotations in a JUnit5 test silently does nothing because the runner no longer processes them, producing tests that appear to pass but skip the setup or teardown logic the Rule was meant to provide.

children.title: Parameterized Tests with @MethodSource
children.description: JUnit5 parameterized tests with @MethodSource reference a companion object or top-level function that returns a Stream of Arguments. The source method must return a type compatible with the test parameter types; a type mismatch throws ParameterResolutionException at runtime instead of compile time. Parameterized tests dramatically reduce duplication for boundary condition coverage but require understanding the source method resolution rules.

resources.label: Baeldung — JUnit5 Parameterized Tests in Kotlin

children.title: mockk() vs spyk() vs relaxedMockk()
children.description: mockk() creates a strict mock that throws for any un-stubbed call; spyk() wraps a real instance and delegates to real methods unless overridden; relaxedMockk() returns default values for un-stubbed calls. Using relaxedMockk() in a test that should verify behaviour allows the test to pass even when the code under test skips the critical call entirely, producing false positives in the test suite.

children.title: Slot & Capture for Argument Verification
children.description: MockK's slot<T>() captures the argument passed to a mocked function so you can assert on its value after the call. Using only verify { mock.method(any()) } confirms the method was called but says nothing about correctness of the argument. Capturing the argument and asserting on its fields catches a class of bugs where the right method is called with the wrong data, which is the most common production defect in service layer code.

children.title: Kotest Spec Styles
children.description: Kotest offers multiple spec styles (BehaviorSpec, DescribeSpec, FreeSpec, StringSpec) that determine how tests are organized and named. Choosing a spec style that does not support beforeEach (like StringSpec) and then expecting lifecycle callbacks to work causes setup code to be skipped silently. Understanding which spec styles support which lifecycle hooks prevents brittle tests that share state incorrectly.

children.title: Kotest Property-Based Testing
children.description: Kotest's property testing generates hundreds of random inputs for your assertions using Arb (arbitrary) generators. A test that passes for all manually chosen examples can still fail for an edge case (e.g. empty string, Int.MAX_VALUE, Unicode combining characters) that property testing finds in seconds. Writing one property test often replaces a dozen example-based tests while covering more ground.

children.title: runTest & TestCoroutineScheduler
children.description: runTest from kotlinx-coroutines-test runs coroutines in a controlled environment where virtual time can be advanced manually, making delay() and timeout tests instant. Using runBlocking instead of runTest in a coroutine test does not control virtual time, causing tests with delay() to actually wait in real time and making the test suite order-of-magnitude slower. Always use runTest for any test touching coroutine timing.

children.title: Turbine for Flow Testing
children.description: Turbine is a testing library that wraps a Flow and lets you assert on emissions one at a time with awaitItem(), awaitComplete(), and awaitError(). Without it, collecting a Flow in tests requires launching a coroutine, collecting into a list, and cancelling — a pattern that races between collection and assertion. Turbine's sequential assertion model eliminates the race and makes Flow test failures point to the exact emission that was wrong.

kotlin-dev.title: Multiplatform & Deployment
kotlin-dev.description: Share Kotlin across targets and ship it. Explore Kotlin Multiplatform and deploy your Ktor service.
topics: Building a fat jar / native image
kotlin-dev.challenge: Containerize your Ktor service, add a CI pipeline, and extract shared logic into a multiplatform module.

children.title: Source Set Hierarchy & expect/actual
children.description: Kotlin Multiplatform uses source sets (commonMain, jvmMain, iosMain, etc.) with a hierarchy that shares code top-down. The expect/actual mechanism declares an API in commonMain and provides platform-specific implementations in each target's source set. A missing actual declaration for any target causes a compile error only for that target, which can be missed if CI only builds one platform — multi-target CI is mandatory for KMP projects.

resources.label: Kotlin Docs — Expected and Actual Declarations

children.title: Shared Business Logic vs Platform UI
children.description: The golden rule of KMP is to share business logic, domain models, and network/data layers in commonMain while keeping UI in platform-specific source sets. Attempting to share UI code with common abstractions creates deep impedance mismatches: Android Compose and iOS SwiftUI have fundamentally different state management models. Projects that push too much into commonMain end up with leaky abstractions that are harder to maintain than two separate UI implementations.

children.title: Multi-Stage Dockerfile for JVM Apps
children.description: A multi-stage Dockerfile uses a JDK build stage to compile and package the fat JAR, then copies only the JAR into a minimal JRE runtime image. Skipping multi-stage and using a JDK in the final image ships hundreds of megabytes of compiler tooling to production, inflating image pull times and attack surface. The JAR-only final image should use a distroless or Alpine JRE base for minimal footprint.

children.title: JVM Flags for Containers
children.description: The JVM defaults to heap sizing based on the host machine's RAM, not the container's memory limit. Without -XX:+UseContainerSupport (enabled by default in JDK 10+) and -XX:MaxRAMPercentage, the JVM claims more heap than the container's limit and is killed by the OOM killer with no JVM OutOfMemoryError, making the failure look like a mysterious crash rather than a memory issue.

children.title: GitHub Actions Kotlin Workflow
children.description: A Kotlin CI pipeline must cache both the Gradle wrapper and the Gradle dependency cache (~/.gradle/caches) to avoid re-downloading all dependencies on every run. Without caching, a typical Kotlin project with Ktor and Exposed downloads several hundred megabytes per build, making CI 3-5x slower. The cache key should include the hash of build.gradle.kts and libs.versions.toml so the cache is invalidated precisely when dependencies change.

children.title: Gradle Build Scans for CI Debugging
children.description: Gradle Build Scans publish a detailed timeline of every task, its inputs, outputs, and why it was not cached. Enabling build scans in CI with --scan transforms debugging a mysteriously slow or broken build from guesswork to a five-minute investigation. Without them, diagnosing why a task re-ran instead of using the cache requires deep Gradle knowledge and manual inspection of log output.

children.title: Health Checks & Graceful Shutdown
children.description: A production Ktor service must expose /health and /ready endpoints and handle SIGTERM with graceful shutdown that stops accepting new connections, drains in-flight requests, and closes database connections in order. Kubernetes sends SIGTERM before removing the pod from the service endpoint list; without a shutdown delay and graceful drain, requests arriving during the termination window get connection refused errors visible to end users.

children.title: Structured Logging & Correlation IDs
children.description: Structured logging outputs JSON with fixed fields (timestamp, level, message, traceId) rather than free-form strings, enabling log aggregation platforms to query and correlate logs across services. Without a correlation ID propagated from the HTTP request through every log line and downstream call, debugging a production failure in a multi-service system requires manually stitching together log lines by timestamp, which is error-prone and slow.

swift-dev.title: Swift Fundamentals
swift-dev.description: Learn Swift, Apple's modern, safe and expressive language. Start with syntax and core types in Xcode Playgrounds.
swift-dev.challenge: Build a Playground tip calculator that handles different bill amounts and split sizes.

children.title: Value Types vs Reference Types
children.description: Swift distinguishes structs (value types) from classes (reference types), and getting this wrong is the root cause of most unexpected mutation bugs. When you assign a struct, you get an independent copy; classes share identity. Choosing the wrong type leads to hard-to-trace side effects, especially when passing objects across threads.

children.title: Type Inference and Static Typing
children.description: Swift's compiler infers types aggressively, but every variable still has a single, fixed type decided at compile time. Misunderstanding inference leads to relying on type coercions that do not exist in Swift — there is no implicit numeric widening, and mixing Int with Double requires explicit conversion. Fixing these errors at compile time rather than runtime is one of Swift's primary safety guarantees.

children.title: Build Targets and Schemes
children.description: Xcode separates what to build (targets) from how to build it (schemes). Misconfigured schemes are the most common reason tests run against the wrong simulator, code signing fails on device, or an app archive includes debug symbols in production. Understanding targets also matters when adding Swift packages — each dependency is linked per-target, not per-project.

children.title: Swift Playgrounds for Exploration
children.description: Playgrounds execute Swift incrementally, making them ideal for testing language behavior without spinning up a full simulator. They are also the fastest way to verify how a standard library API behaves on edge cases — empty collections, nil optionals, overflow — before embedding the logic in production code. Skipping Playgrounds means debugging these assumptions inside a full app build cycle.

children.title: Collections and Algorithms
children.description: Swift's Array, Dictionary, and Set are all value types with copy-on-write semantics, so mutations on a copy do not affect the original unless you explicitly reassign. Forgetting this when passing collections to functions is a frequent source of logic bugs where you expect the caller's collection to be updated but it silently is not.

children.title: String Handling and Unicode
children.description: Swift Strings are Unicode-correct and their Character type maps to an extended grapheme cluster, not a byte or code point. This means string indexing is O(n) and you cannot use integer subscripts — a design that prevents common buffer overruns but breaks assumptions from languages like Python or JavaScript. Misunderstanding this causes both correctness bugs with emoji and surprising performance regressions in tight loops.

children.title: Guard, Defer and Early Exit
children.description: guard lets you validate conditions and exit early, keeping the happy path un-nested and readable. defer schedules cleanup code to run at scope exit regardless of how the scope exits — including throws and returns. Misunderstanding defer ordering (last-in first-out) leads to resources being released in the wrong sequence, causing use-after-free-style logic bugs.

children.title: Closures and Trailing Syntax
children.description: Swift closures are first-class and capture variables by reference by default, which can create strong reference cycles when a closure stored on a class captures self. The @escaping annotation signals that a closure will outlive the function call and forces you to be explicit about capture semantics. Missing @escaping or an incorrect capture list is one of the most common sources of memory leaks in Swift apps.

swift-dev.title: Types, Optionals & Error Handling
swift-dev.description: Master Swift's safety features. Use optionals to model absence and handle failures with typed errors.
swift-dev.challenge: Build a parser that decodes user input into an enum model and reports failures with typed errors.

children.title: Optional Chaining vs Force Unwrapping
children.description: Force unwrapping with ! crashes at runtime when the optional is nil, while optional chaining with ? short-circuits and returns nil safely. Over-relying on force unwraps to silence compiler warnings produces code that crashes in production on data edge cases that never appeared in development. The Swift type system is specifically designed to make nil explicit, and bypassing it negates that entire safety guarantee.

children.title: if let, guard let, and nil Coalescing
children.description: Swift's binding patterns — if let, guard let, and the ?? operator — are the idiomatic tools for unwrapping optionals without crashing. Choosing the wrong one affects code structure: guard let exits the current scope on nil while if let creates a nested scope, directly impacting readability and where the unwrapped variable is accessible. Using ?? with a wrong fallback silently swallows nil cases that should instead surface as errors.

resources.label: Swift Docs — The Basics — Optional Binding

children.title: Associated Values and Exhaustive Switching
children.description: Swift enums can carry typed associated values, turning them into discriminated unions that model state machines with zero illegal states. The compiler enforces exhaustive switch statements, meaning adding a new case forces you to handle it everywhere — this eliminates entire classes of unhandled-state bugs that plague flag-based state management. Ignoring this by using default cases in switches removes the compiler's ability to warn you when new states are added.

children.title: Result Type and Typed Throws
children.description: Result<Success, Failure> encodes success or failure in the type system, forcing callers to handle both paths. Swift 6 added typed throws so function signatures can specify the exact error type thrown, eliminating the need to cast errors at the call site. Relying on untyped throws or overusing try? silently discards error information, making it impossible to recover gracefully from specific failure modes.

children.title: Conforming to Error and LocalizedError
children.description: Any Swift type that conforms to the Error protocol can be thrown, but conforming to LocalizedError adds user-facing descriptions that surface in alert dialogs and logging. Defining errors as enums with associated values lets you carry context (e.g., a failed URL or status code) alongside the error type, which is essential for retry logic and diagnostic logging. Throwing raw strings or generic NSError loses this structured information.

children.title: Error Propagation with throws and rethrows
children.description: Marking a function throws propagates errors to the caller rather than handling them locally, keeping error handling at the appropriate layer. rethrows is a special case for higher-order functions: the function itself does not throw, but it rethrows errors from a closure argument. Misunderstanding rethrows leads to unnecessary try at every call site or, worse, silently swallowed errors when a throwing closure is passed to a non-throwing wrapper.

resources.label: Swift Docs — Error Handling — Propagating Errors

children.title: Type Aliases and Phantom Types
children.description: typealias improves readability, but phantom types take this further by using the type system to enforce domain constraints at compile time — for example, distinguishing a validated email string from a raw string without any runtime cost. Skipping these techniques leads to stringly-typed APIs where the compiler cannot prevent you from passing a user ID where an order ID is expected.

children.title: Implicit and Explicit Conversions
children.description: Swift never coerces numeric types implicitly — adding an Int to a Double requires explicit conversion, and truncating a Double to Int is always explicit. This prevents the class of bugs in C and JavaScript where implicit widening or narrowing silently changes values. Understanding this upfront prevents a large number of compile errors that feel confusing to developers coming from dynamically typed languages.

swift-dev.title: Protocols, Generics & Value Semantics
swift-dev.description: Write flexible, reusable Swift with protocol-oriented programming, generics and an understanding of value semantics.
swift-dev.challenge: Design a generic caching protocol with two conforming implementations and value-type models.

children.title: Protocol Conformance and Default Implementations
children.description: Protocols define capability contracts, and protocol extensions let you provide default implementations that all conforming types inherit without subclassing. This is the foundation of Swift's Protocol-Oriented Programming paradigm — favor composition of protocols over deep inheritance hierarchies. Missing this pattern leads to overuse of class inheritance, which reintroduces reference semantics and the shared-mutable-state problems that value types were meant to eliminate.

children.title: Existentials and Opaque Types (any vs some)
children.description: any Protocol creates an existential type — a box that holds any conforming value but erases the concrete type, adding runtime overhead and restricting what operations are available. some Protocol is an opaque type that preserves the concrete type for the compiler while hiding it from callers, enabling optimization and protocol usage with associated types. Confusing the two causes either unnecessary boxing overhead or compiler errors about protocols with associated types not conforming to themselves.

resources.label: Swift Docs — Opaque and Boxed Types

children.title: Generic Functions and Type Constraints
children.description: Generics let you write algorithms once and have the compiler specialize them for each concrete type, eliminating boilerplate without sacrificing type safety. Type constraints with where clauses restrict which types can be used, ensuring the compiler can verify that the required operations (e.g., Comparable, Hashable) exist at compile time. Writing non-generic code where generics apply results in duplicated logic and silent divergence between copies.

children.title: Associated Types and Generic Protocols
children.description: Protocols with associated types (PATs) are parameterized on a type that conforming types define, making them the backbone of Swift's standard library (Collection, Sequence, Publisher). Before Swift 5.7, PATs could not be used as existentials directly — they required generics or type erasure wrappers like AnyPublisher. Understanding why this restriction exists prevents confusion when the compiler rejects protocol types in positions where a concrete generic type is required.

children.title: Copy-on-Write Optimization
children.description: Swift's standard library types like Array and Dictionary implement copy-on-write (CoW): they share the underlying buffer until a mutation occurs, at which point a copy is made. This means value semantics are cheap for read-heavy workloads but can trigger unexpected allocations when a collection is mutated inside a hot loop. Understanding CoW lets you design custom value types that are equally efficient by implementing the same buffer-sharing pattern with isKnownUniquelyReferenced.

children.title: Mutability with let and var
children.description: Declaring a struct with let makes it and all its stored properties immutable — you cannot call mutating methods on it. For classes, let only prevents reassignment of the reference; the object's properties can still be mutated freely. This asymmetry catches developers off guard and is why prefer let everywhere is a core Swift style rule: the compiler enforces immutability for value types but not for reference types.

children.title: Protocol Composition with &
children.description: Protocol composition (ProtocolA & ProtocolB) creates an unnamed type that conforms to multiple protocols simultaneously, allowing fine-grained capability requirements without creating new protocol types. This is the preferred way to express that a function requires both Codable and Identifiable behavior on a single argument. Overusing base classes to achieve the same result couples unrelated behaviors and prevents conformance of types you do not own.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Swift Standard Library Protocols

children.title: Dependency Injection via Protocols
children.description: Injecting dependencies as protocol types rather than concrete types decouples modules and enables mock implementations in tests. A NetworkService protocol can be satisfied by a live URLSession-backed implementation in production and a canned-response mock in tests — without the production code knowing which is active. Hardcoding concrete types makes unit testing impossible without running a real network, leading teams to skip tests entirely.

swift-dev.title: Memory & Modern Concurrency
swift-dev.description: Understand how Swift manages memory and write safe concurrent code with async/await and actors.
topics: Retain cycles & how to break them
swift-dev.challenge: Refactor a callback-based downloader to async/await and isolate shared state in an actor.

children.title: Strong, Weak, and Unowned References
children.description: ARC automatically deallocates objects when their strong reference count drops to zero, but circular strong references between objects keep both alive forever, leaking memory indefinitely. weak references do not prevent deallocation and become nil automatically; unowned references also do not increase the count but crash if accessed after deallocation. Choosing the wrong ownership annotation in a delegate pattern or closure capture list is the most common source of memory leaks and EXC_BAD_ACCESS crashes in Swift apps.

children.title: Retain Cycles in Closures
children.description: A closure that captures self strongly and is itself stored on self creates a retain cycle — neither can be freed because each holds a strong reference to the other. The fix is a capture list ([weak self] or [unowned self]) at the closure's opening brace, which must then be unwrapped or guarded before use. Forgetting capture lists in completion handlers, NotificationCenter callbacks, and Timer closures is the most reliably reproducible memory leak pattern in iOS development.

resources.label: Swift Docs — ARC — Strong Reference Cycles for Closures
Apple Developer — Xcode Memory Graph Debugger

children.title: async/await and the Cooperative Thread Pool
children.description: Swift's async/await suspends a function at await points without blocking the underlying thread, allowing the cooperative thread pool to reuse threads for other work. Unlike GCD, you do not manually dispatch to queues — the runtime decides where resumed continuations run. Blocking a thread inside an async context with Thread.sleep or DispatchSemaphore.wait starves the thread pool and can deadlock the app because the pool has a limited number of threads.

children.title: Task, TaskGroup, and Cancellation
children.description: Task creates a new top-level concurrent unit of work; TaskGroup allows structured concurrency where child tasks run in parallel and the parent automatically awaits all of them. Cancellation is cooperative — a task must check Task.isCancelled or call try Task.checkCancellation() to actually stop. Launching fire-and-forget tasks with Task {} without storing a reference makes them impossible to cancel, leading to work that continues after the UI that triggered it has been dismissed.

sideRight.title: Actor Isolation and Data Race Safety

children.title: Actors and Isolated State
children.description: Actors protect their mutable state by serializing all access — only one caller can execute inside an actor's isolated context at a time. Accessing an actor's properties from outside requires await, making the async hop visible and auditable. Misunderstanding actor isolation leads to trying to access actor-isolated properties synchronously from non-isolated contexts, which Swift 6's strict concurrency checking rejects at compile time rather than crashing at runtime.

children.title: MainActor and UI Updates
children.description: @MainActor is a global actor that serializes execution on the main thread, replacing DispatchQueue.main.async for updating UIKit and SwiftUI state. Annotating a class or function with @MainActor makes the compiler enforce that all mutations of its state happen on the main thread. Performing UI mutations off the main thread without @MainActor can cause visual glitches or crashes that only appear under load and are nearly impossible to reproduce deterministically.

children.title: Sendable Protocol and Data Race Prevention
children.description: Sendable marks types that are safe to share across concurrency domains — value types are implicitly Sendable, but reference types must either be immutable or protect their state with synchronization. Swift 6's complete concurrency checking makes Sendable conformance a compile-time guarantee rather than a documentation convention, eliminating data races at the source. Attempting to share non-Sendable types across task or actor boundaries causes compile errors that force you to make the sharing safe explicitly.

children.title: Continuations: Bridging Callbacks to async/await
children.description: withCheckedContinuation and withCheckedThrowingContinuation wrap legacy callback-based APIs (URLSession completionHandler, CLLocationManager delegate) into async functions that can be awaited. A continuation must be resumed exactly once — resuming zero times leaks the suspended task forever, and resuming twice triggers a runtime trap. The checked variants add runtime assertions that detect both errors during development, whereas the unchecked variants skip the assertion for performance-critical code.

swift-dev.title: SwiftUI Fundamentals
swift-dev.description: Build declarative UIs with SwiftUI. Compose views, lay them out, and react to data automatically.
swift-dev.challenge: Build a multi-screen recipe browser UI in SwiftUI with navigation and a detail view.

children.title: View Protocol and Declarative Composition
children.description: SwiftUI Views are value types that describe what should be rendered; the framework diffs the view tree and applies minimal updates to the underlying render layer. Every struct conforming to View must have a body property, and nested views compose recursively. Treating SwiftUI views like UIKit views — creating them once and mutating them imperatively — breaks the rendering model because SwiftUI recreates view values on every state change rather than reusing instances.

children.title: ViewModifiers and the Modifier Chain
children.description: Modifiers in SwiftUI return a new view wrapping the original rather than mutating it in place, so the order of modifiers matters — applying padding before background produces a different result than the reverse. Each modifier adds a type layer, creating a deeply nested generic type that the compiler resolves at compile time with no runtime overhead. Writing a custom ViewModifier encapsulates repeated modifier chains and keeps call sites clean, but applying modifiers in the wrong order silently produces incorrect layouts.

children.title: The Layout Protocol and Size Negotiation
children.description: SwiftUI's layout system works by parents proposing a size to children, children choosing their own size within that proposal, and parents placing children in their coordinate space. Understanding this parent-proposes, child-decides contract explains why .frame(width:height:) is a proposal, not a command — a child can still choose a different size. Misunderstanding layout leads to views that clip unexpectedly, frames that do not behave as expected, or unnecessary use of GeometryReader for problems solvable with simpler layout tools.

children.title: HStack, VStack, ZStack, and Grid
children.description: SwiftUI's container views (HStack, VStack, ZStack) arrange children along axes or in layers, while LazyHStack and LazyVStack defer instantiation until a view scrolls into the visible region. Using eager stacks inside ScrollView for long lists allocates all child views upfront, causing severe memory pressure and janky scrolling on large data sets. Grid provides two-dimensional layout with alignment across rows and columns, replacing the nested-HStack workaround that breaks dynamic type scaling.

children.title: Structural Identity vs Explicit Identity
children.description: SwiftUI tracks view identity either structurally — by a view's position in the view tree — or explicitly via the .id() modifier. Changing a view's structural identity (wrapping it in a conditional that swaps its type) causes SwiftUI to destroy and recreate it, resetting all state. Using .id() with a changing value deliberately triggers this recreation to reset state, but accidentally placing .id() on the wrong view can cause entire subtrees to re-render on every state change.

children.title: Canvas Rendering and drawingGroup
children.description: SwiftUI renders each composited layer with a backing store by default; drawingGroup() flattens a subtree into a single Metal-backed layer, trading compositing overhead for a single GPU draw call. This optimization is only beneficial for views with many overlapping semi-transparent layers — applying it everywhere adds unnecessary overhead for simple layouts. Understanding the rendering tree lets you target drawingGroup precisely at the bottlenecks Instruments identifies.

children.title: NavigationStack and Deep Linking
children.description: NavigationStack manages a path of values that drives the navigation hierarchy, replacing the imperative push/pop API of UINavigationController with a declarative data model. Binding the path to an @State or @Observable model enables deep linking and state restoration because you can reconstruct an entire navigation stack by setting the path array programmatically. Using NavigationLink with a destination closure creates eager view instantiation in older API styles, which was the primary reason large apps had slow navigation in SwiftUI 1 and 2.

children.title: Scene and App Entry Points
children.description: A SwiftUI app's entry point conforms to App and declares one or more Scene values that the system manages — WindowGroup for multi-window iPad support, DocumentGroup for document-based apps. The system owns scene lifecycle (active, background, inactive) and calls the scene modifier callbacks accordingly. Ignoring scene lifecycle and performing work only in view onAppear leads to incorrect behavior when apps are backgrounded and restored, since onAppear fires on every navigation rather than once per launch.

swift-dev.title: SwiftUI State & Data Flow
swift-dev.description: Master how data drives SwiftUI. Use the state property wrappers and the observation system to build dynamic apps.
swift-dev.challenge: Add state, forms and animated transitions so users can create and edit recipes in your app.

children.title: @State and View-Local State
children.description: @State stores a value privately within a view and triggers a re-render whenever the value changes. Because the view struct is recreated on each render, @State must be stored outside the struct's value-semantics lifetime — SwiftUI manages the storage in a persistent side table. Using a plain var instead of @State for mutable view data means changes do not trigger re-renders, producing a view that appears frozen; conversely, lifting state too high causes unnecessary subtree invalidation on every keystroke.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Managing User Interface State

children.title: @Binding and Two-Way Data Flow
children.description: @Binding creates a reference to a @State value owned by a parent, enabling two-way data flow between parent and child without duplicating state. When a child writes to a binding, the parent's @State is updated and SwiftUI re-renders the affected subtree. Passing a @Binding where a one-way value is sufficient creates unnecessary coupling and can cause distant views to re-render; passing a copied value where a binding is needed means the child's changes are lost when its view is destroyed.

children.title: @Observable and Fine-Grained Invalidation
children.description: @Observable (Swift 5.9+) replaces ObservableObject for class-based models, providing property-level change tracking rather than whole-object invalidation. Only the views that read a specific property are invalidated when that property changes, eliminating the over-rendering that plagued ObservableObject-based architectures. Migrating partially — mixing @Observable classes with @ObservedObject wrappers — causes double invalidation and is a common source of performance regressions after adopting iOS 17 features.

children.title: @Environment and Dependency Propagation
children.description: @Environment reads values from the SwiftUI environment, which flows implicitly down the view hierarchy without passing through every intermediate view. It is the canonical way to distribute app-wide dependencies (color scheme, locale, accessibility settings, custom services) without prop-drilling. Creating a custom EnvironmentKey with a default value makes the dependency available everywhere without requiring explicit injection, but forgetting to call .environment() at a high enough ancestor means views silently receive the default value instead of the real one.

children.title: Equatable Views and Unnecessary Renders
children.description: SwiftUI re-evaluates a view's body whenever any state it depends on changes. Making a view conform to Equatable and using EquatableView lets SwiftUI skip body evaluation when the view's inputs have not changed, which is critical for expensive leaf views deep in a complex hierarchy. Relying on @State or computed properties that always return new values (e.g., Date() or UUID()) ensures the view re-renders on every frame, which can drop to unacceptable frame rates.

children.title: Task Lifetime and View Lifecycle
children.description: The .task() view modifier launches an async task tied to the view's lifetime — the task is automatically cancelled when the view disappears. This replaces onAppear + Task {} patterns that leave dangling tasks running after the view is gone, updating state on deallocated view models and causing crashes. Using .task(id:) with a changing value restarts the task whenever the id changes, making it the correct tool for reacting to search query changes or selected item transitions.

children.title: withAnimation and Animation Curves
children.description: Wrapping a state mutation in withAnimation causes SwiftUI to animate all views that depend on the changed state using the specified animation curve. The animation is attached to the transaction, not to a specific view, so all affected views animate simultaneously. Applying .animation() directly to a view as a modifier causes it to animate on any state change regardless of source, which can produce unwanted animations when unrelated state updates touch the same view subtree.

children.title: matchedGeometryEffect for Hero Transitions
children.description: matchedGeometryEffect links two views by a shared identifier so SwiftUI interpolates between their frames during a transition, producing hero animation effects without manual coordinate conversion. Both views must be in the same namespace created with @Namespace. Using matchedGeometryEffect incorrectly — without ensuring both views are in the tree simultaneously during the animation frame — causes the view to jump rather than animate, and over-applying it to dozens of views at once degrades frame rate.

swift-dev.title: Combine & Reactive Programming
swift-dev.description: Handle asynchronous events the reactive way with Combine — Apple's framework for processing values over time.
topics: Combine vs async/await — when to use which
swift-dev.challenge: Build a live search-as-you-type feature using Combine with debouncing and cancellation.

children.title: Publisher, Subscriber, and the Subscription Lifecycle
children.description: Combine's pipeline begins with a Publisher that produces values over time and ends with a Subscriber that receives them. The subscription object returned by sink or assign must be held in a Set<AnyCancellable> — releasing it immediately cancels the subscription, causing the pipeline to produce no values and making it appear broken with no error. Every Combine pipeline is lazy and produces no work until a subscriber attaches.
