children.title: Subjects: PassthroughSubject and CurrentValueSubject
children.description: Subjects are publishers that also accept values imperatively via send(), bridging the gap between event-driven and reactive code. CurrentValueSubject holds the latest value and replays it to new subscribers, making it suitable for state; PassthroughSubject does not hold state and only delivers values to currently attached subscribers. Sending values to a subject after calling send(completion:) is silently ignored, which is a frequent source of confusion when error paths complete a subject and subsequent sends disappear.

children.title: Transforming Operators: map, flatMap, and compactMap
children.description: map transforms each value synchronously; flatMap transforms each value into a new publisher and subscribes to it, flattening the nested publisher into the outer stream. Using flatMap without a maxPublishers limit on high-frequency events creates an unbounded number of inner subscriptions that all run concurrently, which can exhaust connections or memory. compactMap filters out nil values from Optional-producing transformations, replacing the common filter + map combination.

children.title: Back-Pressure with Buffer and Throttle
children.description: Combine implements back-pressure through the Subscribers.Demand system: a subscriber requests a specific number of values from the upstream, which is throttled to not overwhelm it. When an upstream produces values faster than the subscriber can consume them, values are either buffered, dropped, or the pipeline is throttled depending on the strategy. Ignoring back-pressure in high-throughput pipelines — such as sensor data or socket streams — leads to unbounded memory growth as values queue up in intermediate buffers.

sideRight.title: Scheduling and Thread Safety in Combine

children.title: receive(on:) and subscribe(on:)
children.description: subscribe(on:) changes the thread on which the subscription and upstream work runs; receive(on:) switches the thread for downstream operators and subscribers. A common error is swapping the two: calling receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) before an expensive operator means the operator still runs on the main thread, blocking UI. The correct pattern is subscribe(on: backgroundQueue).receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) to do heavy work in the background and deliver results on the main thread.

children.title: Error Handling in Pipelines
children.description: A single unhandled error completes the entire Combine pipeline and no further values are delivered — the pipeline is gone unless you explicitly rebuild it. catch replaces the failed publisher with a new one, allowing the stream to continue; replaceError converts an error into a final value. Using sink(receiveCompletion:receiveValue:) and ignoring the completion case silently swallows errors and leaves views in an indeterminate loading state.

children.title: onReceive and Publisher Integration
children.description: SwiftUI's .onReceive(publisher) modifier subscribes a view to any Combine publisher and fires a closure on every emitted value, automatically cancelling the subscription when the view disappears. This is the canonical bridge between Combine pipelines and SwiftUI views for event streams that do not belong in an @Observable model. Subscribing to a high-frequency publisher inside onReceive without debounce causes excessive view re-renders that are indistinguishable from infinite loops during profiling.

children.title: debounce and distinctUntilChanged for Search
children.description: debounce delays delivery of values until a specified idle interval has passed, collapsing a rapid sequence of keystrokes into a single search query. distinctUntilChanged (removeDuplicates) prevents sending the same query twice when the user types and deletes the same character. Omitting these operators on a search text field fires a network request on every keystroke, saturating the network and delivering out-of-order results that corrupt the displayed list.

swift-dev.title: Networking & Persistence
swift-dev.description: Connect to APIs and store data locally. Use URLSession with Codable and persist with SwiftData.
swift-dev.challenge: Fetch recipes from a REST API with Codable and persist favorites locally with SwiftData.

children.title: URLSession and async/await Data Tasks
children.description: URLSession.shared.data(from:) returns the raw response body as Data and a URLResponse; you must cast to HTTPURLResponse and check the status code manually — URLSession does not throw for 4xx or 5xx responses. Treating a 404 as a successful result because no error was thrown is a common bug that causes silent empty states in the UI. Always validate the HTTP status code before attempting to decode the body.

children.title: Codable: Encoding and Decoding
children.description: Codable (Encodable & Decodable) provides compiler-synthesized JSON serialization for any type whose stored properties are themselves Codable. CodingKeys lets you remap between JSON snake_case keys and Swift camelCase property names. Relying on synthesized keys without CodingKeys when the API uses different naming causes silent nil values after decoding — every field missing from the mapping decodes to nil or throws a keyNotFound error at runtime.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Encoding and Decoding Custom Types

children.title: SwiftData Models and Persistence Container
children.description: SwiftData is Apple's Swift-native persistence framework that replaces Core Data boilerplate with @Model macro annotations and a ModelContainer that manages the SQLite store. Forgetting to insert a model instance into a ModelContext before querying it means the object is not persisted and disappears on app restart. SwiftData's strict actor isolation requirements (the main actor for UI-facing contexts) mean accessing a context off the main thread crashes in debug builds.

children.title: UserDefaults and the Preferences Domain
children.description: UserDefaults is a property-list-backed key-value store intended for user preferences, not large data sets. Storing more than a few kilobytes in UserDefaults causes the entire domain to be loaded into memory at launch, increasing startup time. Keys are global strings with no type safety, so a typo returns nil silently; wrapping access in a property wrapper or enum-keyed extension prevents this class of bugs while maintaining compile-time safety.

children.title: URLSession Configuration and Background Transfers
children.description: URLSession configurations control caching policy, timeout intervals, and whether transfers continue when the app is in the background. Background sessions survive app suspension and deliver results via delegate callbacks when the app is relaunched, making them mandatory for large file downloads. Using the shared session for downloads means a task killed in the background loses all progress, forcing users to restart from zero.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Downloading Files in the Background

children.title: Authentication Challenges and Security Transport
children.description: App Transport Security requires HTTPS for all connections by default, and attempting HTTP connections to non-exempted domains fails silently on iOS. URLAuthenticationChallenge delegates handle server certificate validation, and implementing them incorrectly — accepting any certificate — introduces man-in-the-middle vulnerabilities. Certificate pinning adds defense against compromised CAs but requires a rotation strategy to avoid bricking the app when the certificate expires.

children.title: URLCache and HTTP Caching
children.description: URLSession respects HTTP cache-control headers and stores responses in URLCache, enabling offline reads and reducing redundant network requests. Overriding cache policy with .reloadIgnoringLocalCacheData on every request defeats caching entirely and increases data usage for users on cellular networks. Configuring a large enough memory and disk cache and using ETags or Last-Modified headers allows conditional requests that return 304 Not Modified, saving bandwidth while ensuring freshness.

children.title: FileManager and Sandbox Directories
children.description: iOS apps run inside a sandbox with specific directories for different data types: Caches (purgeable by the OS), Documents (backed up by iCloud), and Application Support (persistent, not user-visible). Writing user-generated data to the Caches directory risks data loss when the OS purges it under storage pressure; writing large temporary files to Documents inflates iCloud backup size and may trigger App Store rejection. Always choose the correct directory based on data recoverability requirements.

swift-dev.title: Testing & Debugging
swift-dev.description: Ship reliable Swift. Write unit and UI tests, and profile your app with Instruments.
swift-dev.challenge: Add unit tests for your networking and persistence layers plus one UI test for the create flow.

children.title: XCTest Unit and Async Tests
children.description: XCTest is Apple's built-in testing framework; async test methods are marked async throws and call await directly without the old XCTestExpectation boilerplate. Async test failures that occur after the test method returns — because an async task was not awaited — pass the test while the code is broken, making await non-negotiable for async assertions. Setting up test doubles (mocks, stubs) via protocol injection is the only way to test networking code without hitting real servers.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Testing Your Apps in Xcode

children.title: Swift Testing Framework
children.description: Swift Testing (swift-testing package, available from Xcode 16) uses @Test macros and #expect instead of XCT assertions, producing more readable failure messages that show the actual and expected values inline. Test suites are structs with @Suite, and parameterized tests run a single @Test function across multiple inputs without copying the function body. Mixing XCTest and Swift Testing in the same target requires care — the two frameworks have different lifecycles and the test runner must be configured to use both.

children.title: XCUITest and Accessibility Identifiers
children.description: XCUITest drives the app through the accessibility layer — finding buttons and labels by their accessibility identifier, label, or type. Views without accessibility identifiers or with auto-generated ones are fragile to UI changes, causing test failures on every redesign. Setting explicit .accessibilityIdentifier() values on interactive elements creates a stable test contract that is independent of visual design, and the same identifiers improve the app's actual accessibility for VoiceOver users.

children.title: Snapshot and Performance Testing
children.description: Snapshot tests render a view to an image and compare it to a reference snapshot stored in the repo, catching unintended visual regressions at the pixel level without manual review. Performance tests use XCTMeasure to baseline the execution time of critical paths and fail when a future change regresses beyond the allowed deviation. Both test types are only valuable if the snapshots and baselines are committed to version control and reviewed alongside the code they protect.

children.title: Time Profiler and CPU Analysis
children.description: Instruments' Time Profiler samples the call stack at a fixed interval and builds a flame graph showing which functions consume the most CPU time. Profiling in Release mode is essential — the optimizer dramatically changes the call graph and a Debug profile will lead you to optimize the wrong functions. Identifying and fixing the top three hot paths in a scrolling list is typically enough to recover smooth 60 fps rendering.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Analyzing the Performance of Your App

children.title: Allocations and Leaks Instruments
children.description: The Allocations instrument shows all live heap objects and their retain counts, allowing you to identify which classes are never deallocated. The Leaks instrument auto-detects retain cycles by scanning the heap for objects with no root reference from the application. Running both instruments after every navigation flow is the only reliable way to catch leaks before they accumulate to the point of causing memory pressure warnings and forced termination on low-end devices.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Gathering Information About Memory Use

children.title: LLDB and Breakpoint Strategies
children.description: LLDB supports conditional breakpoints, watchpoints on memory addresses, and expression evaluation that can mutate live program state. Symbolic breakpoints on system methods — such as UIViewAlertForUnsatisfiableConstraints or malloc_error_break — catch errors at their source rather than after they propagate into visible symptoms. Overusing print() for debugging leaves noise in production code; LLDB expressions are evaluated only during debug sessions and leave no trace in the binary.

children.title: OSLog and Unified Logging
children.description: OSLog writes structured log messages to the unified logging system, which aggregates them in Console.app with subsystem and category filtering. Unlike print(), OSLog messages are not included in release builds unless you explicitly use the public privacy qualifier, preventing accidental leakage of user data in production logs. Defining a custom Logger with your app's bundle ID and a category makes it trivial to filter logs across the entire subsystem during debugging.

swift-dev.title: Swift Packages & Deployment
swift-dev.description: Package and ship Swift beyond a single app. Build reusable packages, try server-side Swift, and distribute.
swift-dev.challenge: Extract reusable logic into a Swift package and stand up a small Vapor API it can share.

children.title: Package.swift Manifest and Targets
children.description: Package.swift is a Swift source file that declares products (libraries and executables), targets (source directories), and dependencies (remote or local packages). A target only has access to the products it explicitly lists as dependencies, and transitive dependencies are not automatically visible — attempting to import them compiles but produces linker errors. Structuring a large app as a multi-package workspace with clear target dependencies enforces module boundaries and dramatically speeds up incremental builds.

children.title: Versioning and Dependency Resolution
children.description: SwiftPM resolves dependencies using semantic versioning ranges specified in Package.swift and locks resolved versions in Package.resolved. Committing Package.resolved to version control ensures all team members and CI use exactly the same dependency graph. Using an open-ended upper version bound (from: .1.0.0.) can silently pull in a major-version bump with breaking API changes the next time a developer runs package resolution on a fresh checkout.

children.title: Vapor Application and Routing
children.description: Vapor builds on Swift NIO's non-blocking event loop and models HTTP handling as asynchronous routes. Each route handler receives a Request and returns an async Response or Encodable value that Vapor serializes automatically. Performing blocking I/O (database calls, file reads) on the event loop thread instead of using Vapor's async APIs stalls the loop and makes the server unresponsive to all other concurrent requests — the same mistake as blocking Node.js's event loop.

children.title: Fluent ORM and Database Migrations
children.description: Fluent is Vapor's database-agnostic ORM; models are Swift classes annotated with @Field property wrappers that map to database columns. Migrations are versioned structs that describe schema changes and must be run in order — running them out of order on a production database causes irreversible corruption. Defining a revert method in every migration is the only way to safely roll back changes during a failed deployment without restoring from backup.

children.title: Code Signing and Provisioning Profiles
children.description: iOS apps must be code-signed with a certificate tied to an Apple Developer account and a provisioning profile that binds the certificate to a specific app ID and set of devices or the App Store. An expired certificate or a profile that does not include the test device causes installation to fail with a cryptic error. Automatic signing in Xcode handles most cases, but enterprise and multi-team projects often require manual signing to control exactly which certificate and profile are embedded in the build.

children.title: TestFlight and External Beta Testing
children.description: TestFlight distributes pre-release builds to up to 10,000 external testers after a brief App Store review; internal testers (up to 100 members of your team) receive builds immediately without review. Each build uploaded to App Store Connect is assigned a build number that must be strictly increasing — reusing a build number causes the upload to be rejected. Automating builds with Xcode Cloud or fastlane ensures consistent build numbers and code signing and eliminates the class of distribution failures caused by manual steps.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Distributing Your App for Beta Testing

children.title: App Store Review Guidelines
children.description: Apple's review team checks apps against a detailed set of guidelines covering safety, performance, business, design, and legal requirements. Common rejection reasons include incomplete metadata, broken demo credentials, use of private APIs detected by static analysis, and missing privacy manifest entries for third-party SDKs that access user data. Submitting without reading the guidelines first is the leading cause of week-long review delays caused by fixable rejections.

resources.label: Apple Developer — App Store Review Guidelines

children.title: Privacy Manifests and Required Reason APIs
children.description: As of iOS 17.4, apps and third-party SDKs that access certain APIs (UserDefaults, file timestamps, disk space, active keyboard APIs) must declare a privacy manifest (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy) that lists the reasons for access from Apple's predefined list. Missing or incorrect manifests trigger automated rejection emails that delay release. The manifest also declares what data types the app collects and whether they are linked to user identity, feeding the App Store's privacy nutrition label.

resources.label: Apple Developer — Describing Use of Required Reason APIs

maui.title: C# Fundamentals
maui.description: MAUI is built on C# and .NET. Start with the language fundamentals and the .NET runtime.
maui.challenge: Build a CLI unit converter in C# that validates input and handles errors.

children.title: C# Type System
children.description: C# is a statically typed language where every value has a compile-time type enforced by the CLR. Misunderstanding value types vs. reference types leads to subtle copy-semantics bugs and unexpected null-reference exceptions that are hard to trace at runtime.

children.title: .NET CLR & SDK
children.description: The CLR manages memory, JIT compilation, and cross-platform execution for all .NET MAUI apps. Not understanding how the CLR handles garbage collection and assemblies makes it impossible to diagnose memory leaks or deployment failures on mobile targets.

children.title: Control Flow & Methods
children.description: Solid method design and control flow are the building blocks of any MAUI feature. Poor method design produces untestable code and makes view model logic impossible to isolate during unit testing.

children.title: Strings & Collections
children.description: MAUI UIs constantly manipulate strings and lists. Using the wrong collection type or performing naive string concatenation in loops causes measurable performance regressions on mobile devices with limited memory.

children.title: Namespaces & Assemblies
children.description: Namespaces organize types and assemblies are the unit of deployment in .NET. Mismanaging assembly references or namespace collisions breaks MAUI multi-platform builds where platform-specific assemblies must be resolved correctly for each target.

children.title: C# Beginner Learning Path
children.description: Microsoft's official C# beginner course follows the exact language features used in MAUI development. Skipping structured fundamentals leads to cargo-culting XAML and view model patterns without understanding why they work.

children.title: Exception Handling
children.description: Unhandled exceptions on mobile platforms crash the app silently or show a blank screen with no user-facing message. Understanding try/catch/finally and exception hierarchies is required to build resilient MAUI apps that degrade gracefully on network or I/O failures.

children.title: Nullable Reference Types
children.description: C# 8+ nullable reference types let the compiler catch null-dereference bugs before runtime. In MAUI, binding a null view model silently renders blank UI — enabling nullable analysis makes these problems compile-time errors instead.

maui.title: Object-Oriented C# & .NET
maui.description: Model your app with object-oriented C#. Learn classes, interfaces and how to use the .NET ecosystem.
maui.challenge: Design a small domain model with interfaces and wire it up with .NET dependency injection.

children.title: Classes, Inheritance & Interfaces
children.description: MAUI's handler architecture relies heavily on interface abstractions — each control is represented by an interface implemented differently per platform. Without understanding interface segregation and inheritance, customizing controls or building reusable service layers becomes guesswork.

children.title: Records & Structs
children.description: Records provide immutable, value-equality data models that are ideal for DTOs and state objects in MVVM. Using mutable classes where records are appropriate breaks referential equality checks in collection diffing and forces unnecessary INotifyPropertyChanged boilerplate.

children.title: .NET Dependency Injection
children.description: MAUI's MauiAppBuilder exposes a built-in DI container identical to ASP.NET Core's. Registering services with the wrong lifetime (e.g., transient instead of singleton for a database connection) causes resource exhaustion or state corruption on mobile targets.

children.title: Abstract Classes vs Interfaces
children.description: Abstract classes provide default behavior while interfaces define contracts without implementation. Choosing the wrong abstraction in MAUI's handler or service layer blocks multi-platform overrides and complicates unit test mocking.

children.title: NuGet Package Management
children.description: NuGet is the gateway to the entire .NET MAUI plugin ecosystem including camera, maps, and connectivity. Version mismatches between MAUI-compatible packages and transitive dependencies produce cryptic linker errors that are nearly impossible to diagnose without understanding dependency resolution.

children.title: Generics & Collections
children.description: Generics power type-safe collections and reusable repository patterns in MAUI apps. Using non-generic IList or ArrayList forces boxing of value types and creates runtime cast failures that a generic constraint would have caught at compile time.

children.title: SOLID Principles in C#
children.description: SOLID principles directly govern how MAUI view models, services, and repositories should be structured. Violating the single-responsibility principle bloats view models with networking code, making them untestable and difficult to reuse across platforms.

children.title: Extension Methods & Fluent APIs
children.description: Extension methods enable MAUI's fluent C# UI API (Microsoft.Maui.Controls.Hosting) and are used throughout MVVM Toolkit helpers. Not understanding them makes the builder configuration code look like magic and prevents writing clean helper utilities.

maui.title: Async & LINQ
maui.description: Write responsive, data-driven C#. Master async/await for non-blocking code and LINQ for querying collections.
maui.challenge: Fetch and transform JSON from a public API asynchronously and query the results with LINQ.

children.title: Task & async/await State Machine
children.description: The C# compiler transforms async methods into a state machine that resumes on the captured SynchronizationContext. In MAUI, all UI updates must happen on the main thread — awaiting without ConfigureAwait on a background service then touching UI causes cross-thread exceptions on iOS and Android.

children.title: Cancellation & Async Exceptions
children.description: Mobile apps must cancel in-flight requests when the user navigates away or the app is backgrounded. Ignoring CancellationToken in async data-fetching methods causes unnecessary network usage and can prevent the MAUI app lifecycle from suspending cleanly.

children.title: LINQ Deferred Execution
children.description: LINQ queries are lazy by default — the enumeration only runs when iterated. In MAUI view models, binding a deferred LINQ query directly to a CollectionView re-executes the query on every layout pass, causing silent performance degradation that is invisible in debug builds.

children.title: IEnumerable vs IQueryable
children.description: IEnumerable executes queries in-memory while IQueryable translates them to the data source (e.g., SQLite EF). Using IEnumerable against a SQLite EF context in MAUI pulls the entire table into memory before filtering, which is catastrophic on low-RAM mobile devices.

children.title: System.Text.Json
children.description: System.Text.Json is the default serializer for .NET MAUI REST interactions. Misunderstanding its source-generation mode — required when the iOS linker strips reflection metadata — causes silent deserialization failures where all properties return null.

children.title: Async Streams (IAsyncEnumerable)
children.description: IAsyncEnumerable allows streaming data sequences from APIs without buffering the entire response, which matters on mobile where memory is constrained. Returning a List from a paginated API instead of streaming with await foreach increases time-to-first-display unnecessarily.

children.title: SynchronizationContext in MAUI
children.description: MAUI's SynchronizationContext ensures that awaited continuations resume on the UI thread when called from UI code. Calling MainThread.BeginInvokeOnMainThread unnecessarily inside already-awaited code doubles the dispatch overhead and can cause frame drops on scrolling lists.

children.title: Parallel & Task Combinators
children.description: Task.WhenAll and Task.WhenAny let you run independent async operations concurrently, which is critical in MAUI for loading dashboard data from multiple endpoints simultaneously. Awaiting them sequentially instead multiplies latency by the number of calls.

maui.title: .NET MAUI Basics
maui.description: Build your first cross-platform app. Learn the MAUI project structure, pages and controls that run on iOS, Android, macOS and Windows.
maui.challenge: Build a multi-page MAUI app shell that runs on Android and Windows.

children.title: Single-Project Multi-Platform Architecture
children.description: MAUI's single project consolidates iOS, Android, macOS, and Windows targets into one .csproj with conditional compilation. Misunderstanding how platform-specific folders and MSBuild conditions resolve at build time causes platform resources to be missing in release builds even though they appear fine in debug.

children.title: App Lifecycle
children.description: MAUI maps a unified lifecycle (Created, Resumed, Deactivated, Destroyed) onto different native lifecycle events per platform. Ignoring lifecycle events causes background location tracking to continue after the app is backgrounded on iOS, triggering App Store rejection.

children.title: Pages & Navigation Fundamentals
children.description: MAUI offers ContentPage, TabbedPage, NavigationPage and Shell as distinct navigation containers. Nesting incompatible page types — such as placing a TabbedPage inside a NavigationPage without Shell — produces platform-specific rendering bugs that only appear on one target.

children.title: Handler Architecture
children.description: MAUI controls are virtual views mapped to native platform controls via handlers. Customizing a control by subclassing it instead of using a handler mapper modifies the cross-platform layer and can cause the change to break other platforms or be lost after a MAUI upgrade.

children.title: Resources & Styles in MAUI
children.description: MAUI resolves resource dictionaries at runtime in a merge hierarchy from App.xaml down to the page level. Defining a style in both a page and App.xaml without understanding merge order causes silent style overrides that only appear on specific screen sizes or themes.

children.title: Running on Each Platform
children.description: Each MAUI target requires platform-specific provisioning: Android needs a keystore, iOS needs a provisioning profile, and Windows needs a certificate. Skipping platform setup during early development builds bad habits that result in last-minute code-signing failures when preparing store submissions.

children.title: Microsoft Learn MAUI Path
children.description: Microsoft's official .NET MAUI learning path covers all core concepts in the order they depend on each other. Jumping straight to advanced topics like Shell or MVVM without completing the fundamentals path leads to copy-pasted code that works without being understood.

resources.label: Build Mobile and Desktop Apps with MAUI

children.title: MAUI Sample Apps
children.description: Microsoft publishes reference sample apps that demonstrate idiomatic patterns for data binding, navigation, and platform integration. Studying samples alongside the docs closes the gap between isolated API reference and real-world architecture decisions.

maui.title: XAML & UI Layout
maui.description: Design polished UIs declaratively with XAML. Master layouts, styling and reusable controls.
maui.challenge: Build a responsive product detail screen in XAML with a reusable style dictionary.

children.title: XAML Syntax & Markup Extensions
children.description: XAML markup extensions like {Binding}, {StaticResource}, and {x:Static} are evaluated at parse time by the XAML loader. Misunderstanding when extensions are resolved vs. when bindings fire causes data to appear stale or resources to return null when used before the visual tree is attached.

children.title: Control Templates
children.description: Control templates define the visual structure of a control separately from its behavior, enabling full UI customization without subclassing. Skipping templates and overriding OnPaintSurface or using platform renderers directly locks you into one platform and breaks the cross-platform abstraction.

children.title: Grid, StackLayout & FlexLayout
children.description: MAUI's layout system measures and arranges children in a two-pass algorithm (measure then arrange). Nesting multiple StackLayouts inside a ScrollView creates O(n^2) layout passes that destroy scroll performance on Android, where the layout engine is already more expensive than iOS.

children.title: Responsive & Adaptive Layout
children.description: MAUI apps run on screens from 4-inch phones to 27-inch desktops. Using fixed pixel dimensions instead of star-sizing in Grid or proportional values in FlexLayout produces UIs that clip or leave large gaps on non-mobile form factors, which is critical for Windows and macOS targets.

children.title: Styles & Resource Dictionaries
children.description: Implicit styles (styles without an x:Key) automatically apply to all controls of the target type within scope. Defining an implicit style for Entry in App.xaml without intending it to be global overrides every Entry in the app, including those inside third-party controls.

children.title: CSS Styling in MAUI
children.description: MAUI supports a subset of CSS for styling controls, providing a familiar syntax for web developers. However, CSS in MAUI has lower specificity than XAML styles, so mixing both systems without understanding their precedence order leads to styles silently not applying.

children.title: Fonts, Images & Icons
children.description: MAUI uses a single-project approach where images placed in Resources/Images are automatically resized for all platform densities at build time. Manually copying platform-specific image assets or using wrong naming conventions causes missing images on high-DPI Android and Retina iOS screens.

children.title: Visual State Manager
children.description: The MAUI Visual State Manager controls appearance changes in response to control states (Normal, Focused, Disabled) without code-behind. Handling state changes manually via event handlers couples UI logic to view code and breaks the MVVM separation that keeps views unit-testable.

maui.title: MVVM & Data Binding
maui.description: Structure MAUI apps the right way. Adopt the MVVM pattern with data binding and the MVVM Community Toolkit.
maui.challenge: Refactor your product screen to MVVM with bound view models and commands.

children.title: INotifyPropertyChanged Internals
children.description: Data binding in MAUI subscribes to INotifyPropertyChanged using weak event patterns. Raising PropertyChanged with a mismatched property name silently breaks the binding without any exception, causing UI elements to never update even though the backing property changes.

children.title: Commands & ICommand
children.description: ICommand decouples button actions from code-behind by binding gestures directly to view model methods. Forgetting to call ChangeCanExecute on a Command after state changes leaves buttons permanently enabled or disabled because the CanExecute delegate is only re-evaluated on explicit notification.

children.title: BindingContext & Binding Paths
children.description: Every MAUI view resolves bindings by walking up the visual tree to find the nearest BindingContext. Setting BindingContext in XAML constructors before InitializeComponent runs causes null-reference exceptions because the visual tree does not yet exist to propagate the context to child elements.

children.title: Value Converters
children.description: Value converters transform binding values between the view model and the UI, for example converting a boolean to a Visibility or a DateTime to a formatted string. Performing this transformation directly in the view model mixes presentation concerns into business logic and forces the view model to know about UI types.

children.title: ObservableProperty & RelayCommand Generators
children.description: CommunityToolkit.Mvvm source generators emit the INotifyPropertyChanged boilerplate at compile time from [ObservableProperty] and [RelayCommand] attributes. Without understanding what code is generated, debugging binding failures becomes guesswork because the actual property names differ from what is written in the source file.

children.title: Messenger & Weak References
children.description: The MVVM Toolkit's WeakReferenceMessenger enables decoupled communication between view models without creating memory leaks. Using strong event subscriptions instead causes the subscriber view model to be kept alive by the publisher, preventing garbage collection and growing memory on each navigation.

children.title: ObservableCollection & CollectionView
children.description: ObservableCollection fires CollectionChanged events that drive CollectionView updates without full rebinds. Replacing the entire collection property instead of adding/removing items forces a full layout recalculation on every change, causing visible list flicker and destroying scroll position on mobile.

children.title: Data Templates & Selectors
children.description: DataTemplateSelector enables rendering different item templates in a single CollectionView based on item type, which is essential for chat UIs or heterogeneous feeds. Using a single DataTemplate and hiding elements with IsVisible instead wastes layout time measuring invisible controls on every scroll.

maui.title: Navigation & Platform Features
maui.description: Build full apps with structured navigation and access to native device capabilities.
maui.challenge: Add Shell-based navigation and use the device geolocation API with proper permission handling.

children.title: Shell Routing & URI Navigation
children.description: MAUI Shell uses URI-based routing where each route must be registered before navigation. Navigating to an unregistered route throws a silent exception on some platforms and a hard crash on others, making route registration a critical initialization step that must complete before any navigation attempt.

children.title: Passing Data Between Pages
children.description: Shell supports query parameters and object-based navigation data passed to the destination page's [QueryProperty]. Using the constructor or static fields to pass navigation data breaks deep-linking and back-stack restoration, which the app store review process may test explicitly.

children.title: Permissions Handling
children.description: Android and iOS require runtime permission requests for camera, location, and microphone — requesting them at app launch instead of just-in-time causes users to deny them at the most confusing moment. MAUI's Permissions API provides a consistent cross-platform model but each platform enforces its own grant-once semantics.

children.title: Device Sensors & Essentials APIs
children.description: MAUI Essentials wraps platform sensor APIs (GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope) behind a consistent interface. Accessing sensors without stopping them in the OnSleep lifecycle event drains the battery on mobile and triggers background-process violations that cause iOS to terminate the app.

children.title: Platform-Specific Code & Partial Classes
children.description: MAUI uses partial classes and platform folders to provide platform-specific implementations while sharing the cross-platform interface. Using #if preprocessor directives scattered throughout shared code instead of partial classes pollutes the shared layer and makes platform differences invisible during code review.

children.title: Handler Customization
children.description: Handlers replace the Xamarin.Forms renderer pattern and allow you to customize the native view (e.g., remove Entry underlines on Android) using the handler mapper. Appending mapper modifications in the wrong place in the startup pipeline means they are applied before the control's native view exists, silently having no effect.

children.title: Deep Links & App Links
children.description: App Links on Android and Universal Links on iOS allow external URLs to open specific pages inside the MAUI app via Shell URI routing. Misconfiguring the platform entitlements or the associated domain file causes the link to open in the browser instead of the app, which breaks marketing campaigns and OAuth redirect flows.

children.title: Push Notification Integration
children.description: Push notifications require registering the device token with both the platform (APNs/FCM) and your backend on every app launch, because tokens can rotate. Caching the token locally without re-registering after updates causes notifications to silently stop delivering after an OS update.

resources.label: Push Notifications with Azure Notification Hubs

maui.title: Data & Networking
maui.description: Connect your app to the cloud and store data locally. Consume REST APIs and persist with SQLite.
maui.challenge: Add REST sync and local SQLite caching so your app works offline and updates when online.

children.title: HttpClient Lifecycle & IHttpClientFactory
children.description: Creating a new HttpClient per request exhausts the socket pool and causes connection refused errors under moderate load. IHttpClientFactory manages HttpClient lifetimes through DI, ensuring connections are pooled correctly — misusing it by disposing the factory-issued client early undermines the entire pooling mechanism.

children.title: REST API Consumption & DTOs
children.description: Deserializing API responses directly into domain models couples your app's internal types to the API contract, meaning any API field rename breaks the entire data layer. DTOs with explicit JsonPropertyName attributes insulate the domain model from API changes and enable independent versioning.

children.title: SQLite with sqlite-net-pcl
children.description: SQLite-net maps C# objects to SQLite tables but runs synchronously by default. Calling synchronous SQLite methods on the main thread in a MAUI app blocks the UI thread, causing Android Not Responding dialogs and iOS watchdog process kills after a few seconds of stall.

children.title: Secure Storage & Preferences
children.description: MAUI SecureStorage uses the platform keychain (iOS) or Android Keystore to encrypt sensitive values like auth tokens. Storing tokens in Preferences (which writes plain text on Android) exposes them on rooted devices, which fails PCI-DSS and HIPAA compliance requirements.

children.title: Offline-First Architecture
children.description: Offline-first patterns require a local cache that is the source of truth, with a sync layer that merges remote changes. Apps that make every action depend on a live network connection fail to pass App Store review on devices with simulated poor connectivity and provide a poor experience in mobile network conditions.

children.title: Error & Connectivity Handling
children.description: Network errors on mobile are normal — carriers drop packets, tunnels time out, and background restrictions throttle requests. Treating any HttpRequestException as fatal and showing an error screen instead of implementing retry-with-backoff causes unnecessary user-facing failures that lower app store ratings.

children.title: Azure Mobile Apps & Sync
children.description: Azure Mobile Apps provides an offline sync SDK that handles conflict resolution between local SQLite data and server state. Building a custom sync mechanism without understanding last-write-wins vs. merge conflict strategies leads to silent data loss when two devices edit the same record offline.

children.title: Authentication with MSAL
children.description: MSAL.NET handles OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect token acquisition for Azure AD and social logins in MAUI. Implementing token refresh manually instead of letting MSAL manage the token cache causes unnecessary re-authentication prompts and breaks silent sign-in on app resume.

maui.title: Testing & Debugging
maui.description: Ship reliable MAUI apps. Unit-test your view models and debug across platforms.
maui.challenge: Write xUnit tests for your view models and mock the network layer.

children.title: xUnit Test Fundamentals
children.description: xUnit is the recommended test framework for .NET MAUI view model testing because it creates a new class instance per test fact, preventing state leakage between tests. Using MSTest's shared instance model or NUnit's SetUp without understanding isolation causes flaky tests where test ordering affects results.

children.title: Testing View Models with Mocks
children.description: View model unit tests must run headless without the MAUI dispatcher or platform context. Not injecting the IDispatcher as a dependency and instead calling MainThread.BeginInvokeOnMainThread directly makes view models untestable outside a running app, blocking CI pipelines.

children.title: Debugging on Devices & Emulators
children.description: MAUI debugs over USB (Android) or via a Mac build host (iOS) with full Visual Studio debugger integration. Not setting up conditional breakpoints and only testing in the emulator misses device-specific crashes caused by missing hardware features or stricter permission enforcement on physical devices.

children.title: Hot Reload in MAUI
children.description: MAUI Hot Reload lets you edit XAML and see changes instantly without redeploying the app. Misunderstanding its limitations — it cannot reload changes to view model logic, constructors, or DI registrations — leads to confusion when expected behavior changes are not reflected and the developer wastes time debugging a stale build.

children.title: Performance Profiling with dotnet-trace
children.description: dotnet-trace and the Visual Studio Diagnostic Tools capture CPU samples, memory allocations, and GC events from running MAUI apps. Optimizing based on intuition instead of profiler data almost always fixes the wrong bottleneck — mobile UI performance problems are usually layout thrashing or overdraw, not algorithmic complexity.

children.title: Memory Leak Detection
children.description: MAUI's event-driven binding system creates strong references between controls and view models unless the control's BindingContext is cleared on navigation. Accumulating page instances in memory because event handlers were not unsubscribed is the most common MAUI memory leak, detectable only with a memory profiler.

children.title: Code Coverage with Coverlet
children.description: Coverlet integrates with xUnit and dotnet test to measure which branches of view model code are exercised by tests. Low branch coverage on command CanExecute paths is especially dangerous in MAUI because unexercised CanExecute logic silently enables destructive actions in edge-case states.

children.title: UI Testing with Appium
children.description: Appium provides cross-platform UI automation for MAUI apps using the same test code on iOS and Android. Skipping UI tests and relying only on view model unit tests misses integration failures caused by Shell routing, gesture recognizer conflicts, or keyboard-layout interactions that are invisible without running the app.

maui.title: Azure, Distribution & CI/CD
maui.description: Take your app to production. Add cloud services, automate builds, and publish to the app stores.
topics: CI/CD with GitHub Actions / Azure DevOps
maui.challenge: Wire an Azure Function backend, set up CI to build signed apps, and publish a test release.

children.title: Azure Functions for Mobile Backends
children.description: Azure Functions provide serverless HTTP endpoints that scale to zero between requests, making them ideal for mobile backends. Calling Azure Functions without understanding cold-start latency causes the first request after inactivity to appear to hang on mobile, which users interpret as a crash.

children.title: Azure Notification Hubs
children.description: Azure Notification Hubs route push notifications from a single API call to millions of APNs and FCM endpoints with tag-based audience segmentation. Bypassing Notification Hubs and calling APNs/FCM directly requires managing platform credentials separately and rebuilding audience segmentation from scratch.

children.title: iOS Code Signing & Provisioning
children.description: iOS requires every build to be signed with a distribution certificate and provisioning profile that lists the app's bundle ID and entitlements. Building with a development profile and submitting to the App Store, or mismatching entitlements between the profile and the Info.plist, results in immediate App Store Connect rejection.

children.title: Android Keystore & App Signing
children.description: Android apps must be signed with the same keystore across all updates — losing the keystore permanently locks you out of publishing updates to existing installs on the Play Store. Google Play App Signing can escrow the key, but the upload key must still be managed carefully.

children.title: GitHub Actions for MAUI
children.description: MAUI CI requires macOS runners for iOS builds and Windows runners for Windows/Android builds — mixing targets on the wrong runner OS silently skips platform builds without failing the pipeline. Using a matrix strategy to run platform builds in parallel cuts total pipeline time significantly.

children.title: Azure DevOps Pipelines for MAUI
children.description: Azure DevOps provides hosted agents with Xcode and Android SDK pre-installed for MAUI pipelines. Failing to cache NuGet packages and workload installations in the pipeline adds 5-10 minutes to every run and makes build costs unpredictable in enterprise environments with many commits per day.

children.title: Publishing to the App Stores
children.description: App Store and Google Play submission requires completing metadata, screenshots, privacy policy, and content ratings before the first release, not as an afterthought. Starting the submission process without preparing these assets adds weeks of delay because Apple's review process requires all metadata to be complete before any technical review begins.

resources.label: Publish MAUI App to App Store
Publish MAUI App to Google Play

children.title: Distributing Test Builds
children.description: Microsoft AppCenter (successor to HockeyApp) distributes pre-release builds to internal testers with crash reporting and analytics before a public store release. Distributing test builds as ad-hoc IPA files via email instead of AppCenter bypasses crash analytics, so production bugs go undetected until after the public launch.

pwa-dev.title: Web Foundations
pwa-dev.description: A PWA is a website first. Solidify the HTML, CSS and HTTP foundations that everything else builds on.
topics: Why HTTPS is required for PWAs
pwa-dev.challenge: Build a fast, accessible, responsive single-page site served over HTTPS.

children.title: Semantic HTML & Accessibility
children.description: Using the right HTML elements conveys meaning to browsers, screen readers, and search engines. If you rely on generic divs for everything, assistive technologies cannot navigate your app, and PWA audits will flag accessibility failures that hurt your Lighthouse score.

children.title: Browser Rendering Pipeline
children.description: The browser parses HTML into a DOM, CSS into a CSSOM, combines them into a render tree, then paints pixels. Misunderstanding this pipeline leads to layout thrashing and jank because reading layout properties after a DOM write forces an immediate synchronous reflow.

children.title: The HTTP Request Lifecycle
children.description: Every PWA feature — service workers, caching, push — sits on top of HTTP. Not knowing how requests flow through DNS, TCP, TLS, and headers means you will misread network waterfall charts and cannot diagnose why resources are slow or blocked.

children.title: Why HTTPS is Mandatory for PWAs
children.description: Service workers, the Web App Manifest install prompt, and push notifications are all gated behind a secure origin. Serving over plain HTTP silently disables all PWA features without any visible error, so your app will appear broken without a clear reason why.

children.title: Flexbox & Grid
children.description: Flexbox and CSS Grid are the layout engines of responsive PWAs. Without them you end up with fragile float or absolute-position hacks that break on different screen sizes, defeating the install-worthy, app-like feel that PWAs aim for.

children.title: CSS Custom Properties & Theming
children.description: CSS custom properties enable runtime theming — dark mode, brand colors, and dynamic values — without JavaScript rewrites. Skipping them means duplicating style rules for every theme variant, making the codebase brittle when design tokens change.

children.title: Network Panel & Throttling
children.description: The DevTools Network panel shows every request, its timing, headers, and cache status. Without this skill you cannot verify whether your service worker is intercepting correctly or confirm that resources are loaded from cache on repeat visits.

children.title: Application Panel for PWAs
children.description: The Application panel in Chrome DevTools exposes the manifest, service worker status, cache storage, and IndexedDB. Skipping this tool leaves you guessing whether your manifest is valid or why a service worker is stuck in the waiting state.

pwa-dev.title: Modern JavaScript
pwa-dev.description: PWAs are driven by JavaScript. Master modern language features and asynchronous patterns.
pwa-dev.challenge: Build a weather app that fetches live data and handles loading and error states.

children.title: Native ES Modules vs Bundles
children.description: ES modules let the browser load JavaScript as a dependency graph, but unbundled modules create hundreds of round trips that cripple performance on mobile networks. Understanding when to use native modules versus a bundler like Vite or Rollup is critical to keeping your PWA fast.

resources.label: web.dev — Serve modern code to modern browsers

children.title: Tree Shaking & Code Splitting
children.description: Tree shaking removes dead code at build time, and code splitting breaks your bundle into chunks loaded on demand. Ignoring both results in a single massive JS file that blocks the main thread during parse, directly hurting your Largest Contentful Paint score.

resources.label: web.dev — Reduce JavaScript payloads with code splitting

children.title: Promises & async/await
children.description: Service workers, the Fetch API, the Cache API, and IndexedDB all return Promises. Misunderstanding promise chaining or forgetting to await leads to silent race conditions where cached responses are read before the cache has been populated.

children.title: The Event Loop & Microtasks
children.description: Service worker events such as install and activate use waitUntil(), which extends the lifetime of the event until a promise settles. Not understanding the microtask queue means your promises can resolve after the event has already been torn down, causing inconsistent caching behavior.

resources.label: javascript.info — Event loop: microtasks and macrotasks

children.title: Fetch & Response Cloning
children.description: The Fetch API's Response body is a one-time readable stream. Inside a service worker fetch handler you must call response.clone() before caching and also returning a response; consuming the same body twice throws an error that breaks your caching strategy silently.

resources.label: web.dev — Network reliability with the Fetch API

children.title: CORS & Opaque Responses
children.description: Cross-origin fetches without CORS headers return opaque responses with status 0 and no readable body. If you cache an opaque response, you cannot inspect its status code to detect errors, and it counts as an unknown large size against Cache Storage quota.

children.title: Async Error Boundaries
children.description: Unhandled promise rejections crash silently in service workers — the worker throws no visible error and the user sees nothing. Every async path in a service worker must be wrapped in try/catch or chained with .catch() to ensure network failures are caught and fallbacks served.

children.title: Global Error Events
children.description: The unhandledrejection and error events on self inside a service worker are your last line of defense for catching unexpected failures. Without listening to these, errors disappear into the void and you will never know when a caching or fetch handler has silently failed in production.

pwa-dev.title: Responsive & App-Shell UI
pwa-dev.description: Make your web app feel native. Build a responsive app shell that loads instantly and adapts to any device.
pwa-dev.challenge: Build an app-shell layout with instant navigation and skeleton loading states.

children.title: What the App Shell Pattern Is
children.description: The app shell is the minimal HTML, CSS, and JavaScript needed to render the UI skeleton immediately from cache, before any data arrives. Without it, every visit forces the browser to download your full layout over the network first, causing a visible blank screen that feels nothing like a native app.

children.title: Separating Shell from Content
children.description: The shell (navigation, headers, layout chrome) is stable and should be precached; content (articles, data) is dynamic and should be fetched at runtime. Blending them into one response means you must invalidate and re-download the entire shell every time any data changes, wasting cache quota and causing update delays.

resources.label: web.dev — Instant loading with the PRPL pattern

children.title: Viewport Meta & Fluid Layouts
children.description: Without the viewport meta tag, mobile browsers render the page at desktop width and then scale it down, making text illegible and tap targets too small for fingers. A fluid layout built on percentages and min/max values eliminates the need for separate mobile breakpoints for simple components.

children.title: Safe Areas & Notch Handling
children.description: Devices with notches and gesture bars have safe area insets that can overlap your UI. Using env(safe-area-inset-*) in CSS prevents buttons and content from being hidden behind hardware cutouts, which is a common oversight when porting a website to an installed PWA on modern phones.

resources.label: web.dev — Designing for the safe area

children.title: Touch Target Sizes
children.description: The average fingertip covers about 44x44 CSS pixels. Interactive elements smaller than this generate accidental mis-taps and frustrate users. Google's Lighthouse audit explicitly checks for tap target sizes, so undersized controls directly cost you points on the accessibility audit.

children.title: 300ms Click Delay & touch-action
children.description: Mobile browsers historically added a 300ms delay after a tap to detect double-taps. Setting touch-action: manipulation on interactive elements removes this delay without disabling pinch-zoom on the page, giving taps an instant native feel that users expect from installed apps.

children.title: Skeleton Screens
children.description: A skeleton screen renders a placeholder layout in the exact shape of the real content while data loads. Users perceive skeleton-first loading as faster than a spinner because something meaningful is visible immediately, reducing the cognitive sense of waiting even when load time is identical.

resources.label: MDN — CSS animations for skeleton loaders

children.title: Optimistic UI Updates
children.description: Updating the UI before the server confirms a write makes actions feel instant. If you wait for the round trip first, every button press on a slow mobile connection feels sluggish and broken. The key is to roll back gracefully and surface an error if the server ultimately rejects the operation.

pwa-dev.title: The Web App Manifest
pwa-dev.description: Make your app installable. Configure the web app manifest so users can add it to their home screen.
pwa-dev.challenge: Add a complete manifest with maskable icons and a custom install prompt to your app.

children.title: Core Manifest Properties
children.description: The name, short_name, start_url, display, and theme_color fields are the minimum required for browsers to show the install prompt. An incorrect start_url — especially one that does not match the scope — causes the installed PWA to open in a browser tab instead of standalone mode, breaking the native-app illusion.

resources.label: web.dev — Add a web app manifest

children.title: Display Modes
children.description: The display field controls how much browser chrome surrounds your app: browser, minimal-ui, standalone, or fullscreen. Choosing the wrong mode exposes the address bar inside your installed app or hides navigation controls users need, directly affecting how app-like the experience feels.

children.title: Icon Sizes & Purposes
children.description: Browsers require icons at specific sizes (192x192 and 512x512 at minimum) for home screen icons and splash screens. A missing or wrong-sized icon causes Chrome to skip the install prompt entirely, silently preventing users from ever installing your PWA.

children.title: Maskable Icons
children.description: On Android, launcher icons are placed inside adaptive icon shapes (circles, squircles, etc.) that crop a portion of the image. Without a maskable icon variant with a safe zone, your icon can have its content clipped and look broken on users' home screens across different phone manufacturers.

children.title: Installability Criteria
children.description: Chrome shows the install prompt only when your app is served over HTTPS, has a valid manifest with required fields, has a registered service worker with a fetch handler, and the user has engaged with the page. Failing any single criterion silently prevents the prompt — there is no error message for the user or developer.

resources.label: web.dev — What makes a good PWA
developers.google.com — Add to Home Screen criteria

children.title: The beforeinstallprompt Event
children.description: The browser fires beforeinstallprompt before showing the install UI, giving you the chance to defer or customize when the prompt appears. Ignoring this event and relying on the automatic banner means you cannot time the install offer around a high-engagement moment, which dramatically lowers conversion rates.

resources.label: web.dev — How to provide your own in-app install experience

children.title: Splash Screen Generation
children.description: Android generates a splash screen automatically from your manifest's name, background_color, and icon. Without the correct combination, the splash screen shows a blank white flash or a tiny unrecognizable icon, undermining the polished first impression of an installed app.

children.title: Theme Color & System UI
children.description: The theme_color in the manifest tints the browser toolbar and the Android task switcher thumbnail. Mismatching this with your actual app chrome color creates a jarring visual inconsistency that signals to users that the PWA is a second-class citizen compared to native apps.

pwa-dev.title: Service Workers
pwa-dev.description: The heart of every PWA. Master the service worker lifecycle and intercept network requests.
pwa-dev.challenge: Register a service worker that intercepts requests and serves a custom offline page.

children.title: Install, Activate & Waiting States
children.description: A new service worker goes through installing, then waits if an old version controls any open tabs, and only activates after all old clients are closed or skipWaiting() is called. If you do not understand this, a deployed update may sit in the waiting state for days on users' devices while they see stale content.

children.title: waitUntil() and extendableEvent
children.description: Calling event.waitUntil() inside install or activate tells the browser to keep the service worker alive until the promise resolves. Without it, the worker can be killed before your caching completes, leaving a partially populated cache that causes random cache misses.

children.title: The fetch Event Handler
children.description: The service worker's fetch event fires for every resource request made by controlled pages. If you do not call event.respondWith(), the request falls through to the network as normal; calling it with the wrong response or a rejected promise results in a failed network request visible to the user.

children.title: Scope & Request Filtering
children.description: A service worker only intercepts requests within its scope — the directory path where the worker script is registered. Placing the worker at /app/sw.js means it cannot intercept requests to /api/ unless the scope is explicitly set to /. Scope mismatches are a frequent source of confusion when caching fails silently.

children.title: Registering a Service Worker
children.description: Registration should happen after the page load event so the service worker download does not compete with page-critical resources. Registering too early delays First Contentful Paint on the first visit; registering incorrectly logs a silent error that prevents the PWA from ever having a service worker.

children.title: Safe Update Strategy with skipWaiting
children.description: Calling skipWaiting() forces a new service worker to activate immediately, but if it also calls clients.claim() and the new cache structure is incompatible with the still-running old page, you can get a cache mismatch that crashes the app. Always design cache updates to be backward-compatible or coordinate with a page reload.

children.title: DevTools Service Worker Panel
children.description: Chrome DevTools Application > Service Workers shows registration status, errors, and lets you trigger update checks, unregister, or force activation of a waiting worker. Without this panel, debugging lifecycle issues means clearing site data repeatedly and hoping the behavior changes.

children.title: Bypassing the Service Worker for Testing
children.description: The Network panel's Disable cache checkbox and the Service Workers panel's Bypass for network option let you test fresh network behavior without unregistering the worker. Developers who do not know these tools waste time unregistering and re-registering workers during development.

pwa-dev.title: Caching Strategies & Offline
pwa-dev.description: Make your app work offline. Apply caching strategies and store data locally for a resilient experience.
pwa-dev.challenge: Make your weather app fully usable offline with Workbox caching and IndexedDB storage.

children.title: Cache-First vs Network-First
children.description: Cache-first serves the cached response immediately and never shows stale data to the user unless the cache is populated; it is ideal for static assets that rarely change. Network-first always attempts the network and falls back to cache on failure; it is correct for API responses where freshness matters but at the cost of slower responses on bad connections.

children.title: Stale-While-Revalidate
children.description: Stale-while-revalidate serves the cached version instantly while simultaneously fetching an update in the background for the next visit. Misapplying it to resources like authentication tokens, where freshness is critical, means users receive outdated access data and may see content they no longer have permission to view.

children.title: Workbox Precaching
children.description: Workbox precaching downloads and caches a list of assets at service worker install time, assigning each a revision hash. Skipping the revision means Workbox cannot detect when a file changes, serving users the stale version indefinitely — a silent caching bug that is very hard to diagnose without knowing how precaching works.

children.title: Workbox Runtime Caching
children.description: Runtime caching applies a strategy to requests that were not precached — API calls, images from a CDN, fonts. Without configuring expiration plugins and max-entries limits, the Cache Storage quota fills up over time, causing the browser to evict your most important cached assets to reclaim space.

children.title: Cache Versioning & Cleanup
children.description: Each deployed version should use a new cache name. During the activate event you must delete old caches using caches.delete(); leaving stale caches behind wastes storage quota and can cause the browser to serve outdated assets from an orphaned cache that nothing is managing.

children.title: Storage Quota & Eviction
children.description: Browsers allocate Cache Storage from a shared origin quota. When the device runs low on disk space, the browser uses a least-recently-used policy to evict entire origins without warning. Not checking navigator.storage.estimate() leaves you blind to how close you are to the quota limit before data disappears.

children.title: When to Use IndexedDB vs Cache API
children.description: The Cache API stores HTTP request/response pairs and is ideal for assets and API responses you want to serve offline. IndexedDB stores structured JavaScript objects and is the correct choice for user-generated data, draft state, or any data you need to query, sort, or update without re-fetching a whole resource.

children.title: Using idb for a Cleaner API
children.description: The raw IndexedDB API is verbose and callback-based, making it easy to write transaction bugs where you try to read data after the transaction has already committed. The idb wrapper library exposes a Promise-based interface that maps directly to the same storage engine without any abstraction overhead.

pwa-dev.title: Push Notifications & Background Sync
pwa-dev.description: Re-engage users like a native app. Add push notifications and sync data in the background.
pwa-dev.challenge: Add push notifications with a VAPID-secured backend and queue failed requests with Background Sync.

children.title: Push Subscription & VAPID Keys
children.description: The browser generates a push subscription endpoint that routes messages through the push service (FCM for Chrome). VAPID keys authenticate your server to the push service and prevent unauthorized parties from sending messages to your users. Leaking VAPID private keys allows anyone to send push notifications as your app.

children.title: Handling the push Event in the Service Worker
children.description: Push messages arrive at the service worker's push event, even when no page is open. You must call event.waitUntil() around the notification display, or the service worker will be killed before the notification appears. Without this, push notifications silently disappear on devices with strict background process management.

children.title: Notification Permission Flow
children.description: Requesting notification permission too early — before the user understands why they would want notifications — results in the majority of users clicking Block, permanently preventing notifications for that origin. There is no way to re-ask a blocked user programmatically; they must manually change browser settings.

children.title: Notification Options & Actions
children.description: Notification options like badge, icon, body, tag, and actions shape the appearance and interactivity of the notification in the system tray. Using the tag field collapses multiple notifications from the same source into one, preventing notification spam that causes users to disable all notifications from your app.

children.title: Background Sync for Deferred Requests
children.description: Background Sync lets you queue a failed network request (e.g., a form submission) and have the service worker retry it automatically when connectivity is restored, even if the user has closed the tab. Without it, form data submitted offline is lost silently, which is a catastrophic user experience for any input-heavy PWA.

children.title: Periodic Background Sync
children.description: Periodic Background Sync allows the service worker to refresh content on a schedule (e.g., fetching news articles hourly) so users open the app to fresh data even without an active connection. The browser grants this permission based on site engagement score, so it is not available to newly installed PWAs with no usage history.

children.title: Storing Subscriptions on the Server
children.description: Push subscriptions expire and change endpoint URLs over time; W3C push services can silently invalidate old subscriptions. If your server never removes stale subscriptions, you accumulate thousands of dead endpoints, waste bandwidth attempting delivery, and may trigger push service rate-limiting that blocks all your notifications.

children.title: Payload Encryption
children.description: Web Push payloads are end-to-end encrypted between your server and the browser using the keys from the subscription object. Sending large, unencrypted payloads or ignoring the 4KB payload limit causes pushes to be silently dropped by the push service without any error response to your server.

pwa-dev.title: Device & Web APIs
pwa-dev.description: Tap into device capabilities from the browser. Use the modern web platform APIs that close the gap with native.
pwa-dev.challenge: Add camera capture and Web Share to your app, with graceful fallbacks where unsupported.

children.title: Geolocation API & Permissions
children.description: The Geolocation API is permission-gated and only works over HTTPS. Calling it without first explaining the use case causes browsers to show a generic prompt that users dismiss out of habit, and a denied permission cannot be re-requested programmatically — the user must change site settings manually.

children.title: Device Orientation & Motion
children.description: Device orientation events provide gyroscope and accelerometer data for gesture-based features. On iOS 13+, accessing these events requires an explicit user-gesture permission call that many developers miss, causing orientation features to silently return no data on Apple devices.

children.title: getUserMedia & Media Capture
children.description: getUserMedia() streams camera and microphone data and requires explicit user permission. Requesting both camera and microphone simultaneously increases the likelihood of denial; always request the minimum permissions you need and handle the NotAllowedError gracefully with a clear explanation to the user.

resources.label: web.dev — Capture audio and video in HTML5

children.title: HTML Media Capture for File Input
children.description: The capture attribute on an input[type=file] element opens the device camera directly for photo or video capture on mobile. It is a simpler alternative to getUserMedia for one-shot capture use cases and works without any JavaScript permission handling, making it the correct choice for basic camera upload features.

resources.label: web.dev — Taking photos with the camera

children.title: Web Share API
children.description: The Web Share API invokes the native OS share sheet, letting users share URLs, text, and files to any installed app. It must be called from a user gesture; calling it outside a gesture throws an error. On desktop, it is only supported in a subset of browsers, so always feature-detect before using it.

resources.label: web.dev — Share like a native app with the Web Share API

children.title: Async Clipboard API
children.description: The modern Clipboard API requires the clipboard-read and clipboard-write permissions and is only available in focused browser tabs. The older document.execCommand('copy') fallback works without permission but is deprecated. Always try the modern API first and fall back to execCommand with a visible message when it is unavailable.

children.title: Feature Detection with 'in' and typeof
children.description: New Web APIs ship at different times across browsers. Checking if ('geolocation' in navigator) before calling the API prevents runtime errors in unsupported browsers and lets you offer a graceful fallback. Hard-coding browser version checks or user-agent sniffing breaks as soon as a browser ships support unexpectedly.

children.title: Progressive Enhancement vs Graceful Degradation
children.description: Progressive enhancement starts with a baseline that works everywhere and layers advanced features on top; graceful degradation starts with the full experience and handles breakage. For PWAs, enhancement is the correct model because service worker and push support vary widely, and a missing feature should never break the core user journey.

pwa-dev.title: Performance & Lighthouse
pwa-dev.description: Fast is a feature. Measure and optimize your PWA against Core Web Vitals using Lighthouse.
topics: Core Web Vitals — LCP, INP, CLS
pwa-dev.challenge: Get your PWA to a 90+ Lighthouse score across performance, PWA and best-practices audits.

children.title: LCP — Largest Contentful Paint
children.description: LCP measures how long it takes for the largest visible element (hero image, heading) to render. A slow LCP is almost always caused by a render-blocking resource or an un-preloaded image. Since Google uses LCP as a search ranking signal, ignoring it directly reduces organic traffic to your PWA.

children.title: INP — Interaction to Next Paint
children.description: INP (which replaced FID) measures how quickly the page responds visually to every user interaction. Long JavaScript tasks on the main thread delay the paint after an input, making the UI feel frozen. Breaking long tasks with scheduler.yield() or setTimeout(0) keeps the event loop responsive.

children.title: Running and Reading Lighthouse Reports
children.description: Lighthouse runs audits across five categories: Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, SEO, and PWA. The PWA category specifically checks for a valid manifest, service worker, and HTTPS — failing any check removes the install badge. Reading the opportunity and diagnostic sections tells you exactly which changes will improve your score most.

children.title: CLS — Cumulative Layout Shift
children.description: CLS measures unexpected layout shifts caused by images without dimensions, dynamically injected content, or late-loading fonts. A high CLS score means buttons move under a user's finger after they have already started a tap, causing accidental clicks and a deeply frustrating experience on mobile.

children.title: Image Optimization & Modern Formats
children.description: Images are typically the largest assets on a page and the primary cause of slow LCP. Using WebP or AVIF formats reduces file size by 30-80% compared to JPEG without visible quality loss. Omitting width and height attributes on images causes layout shifts as they load, directly worsening your CLS score.

children.title: Font Loading & font-display
children.description: Custom web fonts block rendering by default if they have not loaded yet, causing a flash of invisible text. Setting font-display: swap in the @font-face rule tells the browser to render text in a fallback font immediately and swap to the custom font when ready, preventing invisible text from hurting your LCP.

children.title: Code Splitting & Dynamic Import
children.description: Dynamic import() splits your bundle so code for each route is only downloaded when the user navigates there. Without it, the user downloads JavaScript for every page on first load, blocking interactivity on slow connections and wasting bandwidth for routes the user may never visit.

resources.label: web.dev — Code splitting with dynamic imports

children.title: Resource Hints: preload, prefetch, preconnect
children.description: Preload tells the browser to fetch a critical resource (LCP image, font) as soon as possible; prefetch hints at lower-priority resources needed for likely next navigations; preconnect opens a TCP and TLS connection early to a third-party origin. Using preload for non-critical resources wastes bandwidth and can actually slow the resources it was meant to help.

pwa-dev.title: Packaging & Deployment
pwa-dev.description: Ship your PWA everywhere. Deploy it, and optionally package it for the app stores.
topics: Deploying to a CDN / static host
Packaging for stores with PWABuilder / TWA
pwa-dev.challenge: Deploy your PWA to a CDN and package it for the Play Store using a Trusted Web Activity.

children.title: Static Hosting & CDN Delivery
children.description: PWAs are static assets (HTML, JS, CSS, manifest) that should be served from a CDN edge close to the user. Hosting on a single-region server adds 100-300ms of latency on every request for users in other regions, directly hurting your LCP scores. Platforms like Netlify, Vercel, and Cloudflare Pages provide global CDN distribution by default.

children.title: Service Worker Hosting Rules
